coredata.dc:title: Flexible micro-supercapacitors: Materials and architectures for smart integrated wearable and implantable devices

coredata.dc:description: Flexible Micro-supercapacitors (FMSCs) are revolutionizing smart wearable and implantable devices with their high energy density, superior power density, and exceptional mechanical flexibility. These properties make FMSCs ideal for dynamic, contoured surfaces of wearables and the limited spaces in implants, enhancing design, comfort, and user experience. As these devices evolve, incorporating advanced biosensors, communication technologies, and sophisticated processors, the demand for compact, potent energy storage solutions grows. FMSCs address this need by providing efficient energy storage and delivery, significantly extending operational times. Their rapid charging and discharging capabilities are especially beneficial in healthcare applications, where timely data acquisition and transmission are crucial for effective patient monitoring and intervention. Advances in material science, fabrication techniques, and integration methods continuously improve FMSC performance, broadening their applications and ensuring they remain at the technology forefront. This review explores the current state of FMSC research, focusing on recent advancements, identifying ongoing challenges, and examining potential future directions. It underscores the critical role of FMSCs in enhancing the functionality and autonomy of contemporary and future portable and wearable electronics, paving the way for seamless electronic integration into daily life.

Carbon nanotube based FMSCs (a) Schematic diagram of preparation process of Ti3C2Tx/SWCNT/CNF hybrid aerogels. Microstructure of Ti3C2Tx/SWCNT/CNF hybrid aerogel. CV curves of TSC3 electrode at different scan rates along with structure diagram of the interdigitated micro-supercapacitors. Reproduced with permission [110]. Copyright 2023, Elsevier. (b) Schematic fabrication flow of the AMSC. Representative digital image showing an LED illuminated by an AMSC and CV curves of the as-assembled AMSCs bent at various angles at a scan rate of 100 mV s− 1. Reproduced with permission [112].Copyright 2018, Royal Society of Chemistry. (c) The induction of extrinsic hydrophilicity in coiled carbon nanotube (CNT) yarns via electrochemical oxidation (ECO), the visualization of charge transfer reactions from a water molecule to pristine and ECO-treated CNTs,(d) the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis spectra depicting C 1 s and O 1 s for both pristine and ECO-treated coiled CNT yarns, and a captured photograph showcasing a water droplet absorbed onto the middle of the ECO-treated coiled CNT yarn. Additionally, an inset image provides a comparison with water droplets on spider silk. Reproduced with permission [113]. Copyright 2022, ACS Publications.

Graphene based FMSCs. (a) The schematic illustrates the femtosecond laser-induced graphene (FsLIG) production process on fallen leaves. FsLIG microelectrodes are patterned directly on the leaf surface using ultrashort UV laser pulses in a single step. The laser irradiation rapidly transforms carbon compounds (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin) into graphene through carbonization, graphitization, and exfoliation. Simultaneously, the biomineral whewellite decomposes into inorganic crystals, which act as nucleation sites for mesoporous graphene formation. A pseudo-color SEM image depicts the FsLIG microelectrodes on the leaf, with an inset showing the porous microstructure (scale bar: 10 µm). Cross-sectional SEM imaging, paired with EDS elemental maps, indicates an FsLIG microelectrode thickness of approximately 90 µm. TEM images reveal two distinct FsLIG morphologies, with insets providing detailed views at 5 nm and 20 nm scales. Reproduced with permission. [119] Copyright 2022, Wiley Publications. (b) Schematics of SSFL strategy to fabricate the graphene/MnO2 MSCs. Reproduced with permission. [120]Copyright 2020, Nature Publications. (c) Schematic of O-LIM-LIG Hybrid Synthesis Using Picosecond Pulsed Laser. Reproduced with permission. [121] Copyright 2023, ACS Publications (d) Manufacturing process of MXene−rGO composite aerogels self-healing MSCs. Reproduced with permission. [122] Copyright 2018, ACS Publications.

Reproduced with permission.[141] Copyright 2019, Nature Publishing. (d) Schematic of the all-MXene-based integrated system. Reproduced with permission. [149] Copyright, 2019, ACS Publications. Reproduced with permission [152]. Copyright, 2024, Elsevier.

Photolithography technique (a) Schematic of the fabrication of a flexible micro-supercapacitor device. The interdigitated micropatterns fabricated via photolithography on a Si/SiO2 substrate and transferred on polyimide film via selective etching transfer. Reproduced with permission [258]. Copyright 2022, Elsevier. (b) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the asymmetric MSC. Photographs of the device at different fabrication stages (AM: active materials). Illumination process of PIC-CNTs-MnO2. Plausible energy storage mechanism of PIC-CNTs-MnO2//PICK-CNTs MSCs. (c) SEM images of PAN-CaCO3-CNTs, PIC-CNTs, and PIC-CNTs-MnO2. Reproduced with permission [259]. Copyright 2021, ACS Publications.

Laser scribing technique (a) Schematic illustrating the fabrication process of Ni foam-based MSCs devices. The interdigitated Ni foams current collectors were prepared by direct laser machining and the corresponding MSCs image, scale bar 5 mm. Electrodeposited pseudocapacitive materials of MnO2 and PPy to form Ni foam-MnO2 electrode and Ni foam-PPy electrode. Assemble all-solid-state 3D Ni foam-based MSCs devices. Reproduced with permission [261]. Copyright 2022, Elsevier. (b) Schematic illustration of the manufacturing process of flexible FMSCs. Reproduced with permission [262]. Copyright 2023, Elsevier. (c) Demonstrations of laser-patterned elastomeric MSCs with versatile designs. Photos of five MSCs connected in series (relaxed, stretched, bent, rolled, twisted, and crumpled), showing excellent mechanical robustness. Five series-connected MSCs lit up a commercial LED even under 60 % tensile strain, demonstrating an intrinsically stretchable power source. CV curves of a device with a curved pattern that was stretched to 80 % and released to 0 % in the direction parallel to the microelectrode fingers. The device retained 38 % of its original capacitance when stretched to 80 % and regained its original value after being released. The inset shows an example of a single MSC with a wavy curved configuration that served to enhance the strain limit to 80 %. Schematic equivalent circuit and photographs of the integration of MSCs into a laser-patterned elastomeric circuit based on rGO/Au/PDMS, in which the switch could be turned on/off by folding to create the contact and the resistor could be tuned by the length of the printed circuits and by the stretching-induced change in resistance. Reproduced with permission [263]. Copyright 2018, Nature Publications.

(c) Integration of textile MSCs with common clothes. A photo of four textile MSCs sewed on a garment in the shape of four letters, i.e., “BINN.” GCD curves of letter-shaped MSCs at different current densities. [83,268] Copyright 2016, Wiley Online Library. (Reproduced with permission [81]. Copyright 2016, Wiley Online Library.

Fluorescence images show cell viability at different times; green (calcein-AM) represents live HEC-1-A cells. Inset below the arrow is a cross-sectional SEM image of the assembled supercapacitor implant without encapsulating. Scale bars, 30 μm. Copyright 2021, Wiley Online Library.

Multifunctional wearable sensor applications (a) Diagram of working mechanism for self-powering MXene/TiS2//MXene/TiS2 sensor, pressure-sensitive response current of wearable, flexible sensor toward different grasping posture, finger bending at various angles, and breathing, walking and running in step. Rreproduced with permission [285]. Copyright 2022, ACS Publications. (b) Integration of the dual-mode sensor, strain sensor, and supercapacitor on a deformable substrate. (Right top) Optical image of the integrated system attached to hand-skin. Reproduced with permission [286]. Copyright 2018, Wiley Online Library. (c) Photo of the integrated sensing system. Rreproduced with permission [254]. Copyright 2023, Frontiers Publications. (d)Optical and thermal image of a finger touching the sensor on paper. Plots show ΔI/I0 and Vtherm responses of a self-powered dual-mode sensor to loading–unloading cycles of finger touch. The sensing temperature on the top surface is calculated by TS = Vtherm/ST + T0, where T0 is the ambient temperature (26.0 °C) and ΔT = 4 K. Reproduced with permission [286]. Copyright 2018, Wiley Online Library.

Photodetector applications (a) Schematic of integrated asymmetric MSC with photodetector. Reproduced with permission [292]. Copyright 2016, ACS Publications. (b) Schematic illustration of the self-powered photodetection system including an energy harvester (a commercial solar panel), a diode to avoid current backflow to the solar panel, energy storage (an MSC), and a functional device (a ZnO-based photodetector).Reproduced with permission. Photocurrent response of the integrated device as photodetector at different strain states, a UV light intensity: 5 mW cm−2. The sensitivity and specific capacitance of integrated device at varied strain levels. The sensitivity and specific capacitance at the original state is normalized to 1. The schematic illustration of the integrated device as photodetector. Photocurrent response of the integrated device as photodetector before and after repeated stretching 100 times. Sensitivity and specific capacitance of the integrated device at different stretching times. The sensitivity and specific capacitance at original state is normalized to 1. Reproduced with permission [294]. Copyright 2017, Elsevier. (d) Circuit diagram of integrated system. The system consists of a coil as power receiver, MSC, and a photoconductive-type photodetectors of perovskite NWs. Photograph of the integrated system. (e)SEM images of perovskite NWs. XRD patterns of perovskite NWs. (f) The self-discharge curve of the asymmetric MSC. The photocurrent dependence on time of the device under illumination on/off states at a bias of 0.85 V supplied by an external power source. The photocurrent dependence on time of the device under illumination on/off states driven by the asymmetric MSC. Reproduced with permission [292]. Copyright 2016, ACS Publications.

[309] Copyright 2022, Elsevier. Schematic diagram of the whole device, inset photograph demonstrates that the integrated system worn on the forearm. Schematic illustrations of (b) the MSC, (c) the FGF SS, and (d) SCs on PI substrates (e) Photograph of integrated system attached to the skin of the wrist. (f) Photograph of wrist bending (g) Resistance change of the FGF sensor driven by stored energy of the MSCs, charged with (red) external power supply, (green) solar simulator, and (blue) solar light, in response to the pulse signal of the radial artery. (h) Resistance changes of the FGF sensor driven by stored energy of the MSC, charged with (red) external power supply, (green) solar simulator, and (blue) solar light, in response to the bending of the wrist. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible Micro-supercapacitors (FMSCs) are revolutionizing smart wearable and implantable devices with their high energy density, superior power density, and exceptional mechanical flexibility. These properties make FMSCs ideal for dynamic, contoured surfaces of wearables and the limited spaces in implants, enhancing design, comfort, and user experience. As these devices evolve, incorporating advanced biosensors, communication technologies, and sophisticated processors, the demand for compact, potent energy storage solutions grows. FMSCs address this need by providing efficient energy storage and delivery, significantly extending operational times. Their rapid charging and discharging capabilities are especially beneficial in healthcare applications, where timely data acquisition and transmission are crucial for effective patient monitoring and intervention. Advances in material science, fabrication techniques, and integration methods continuously improve FMSC performance, broadening their applications and ensuring they remain at the technology forefront. This review explores the current state of FMSC research, focusing on recent advancements, identifying ongoing challenges, and examining potential future directions. It underscores the critical role of FMSCs in enhancing the functionality and autonomy of contemporary and future portable and wearable electronics, paving the way for seamless electronic integration into daily life.

Flexible micro-supercapacitors (FMSCs) offer ultrahigh energy and power density, long life cycle and good reproducibility. This comprehensive review explores the latest advancements in FMSCs designed for integration into wearable and implantable devices, providing insights into current critical challenges (i.e. scalability, biocompatibility, and power density) and applications in healthcare and consumer electronics.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: The surge in demand for portable and flexible electronic devices is a notable trend, fuelled by technological advancements, evolving consumer preferences, and the quest for convenient and versatile gadgets[1]. Wearable devices like smartwatches, fitness trackers, and healthcare monitors have become immensely popular, providing easy access to information, health monitoring, and communication [2]. The appeal of flexible displays and electronics, as well as device interconnectivity (with Internet of Things (IoT)) lies in their ability to introduce innovative features, allowing devices to be curved, rolled, or folded as exemplified in most current smartphones, tablets, and e-readers with flexible display[2,3].Consumers are increasingly seeking convenience and mobility in their electronic gadgets[4].Various applications, including smart home devices, remote sensing, and healthcare monitoring, necessitate miniaturized, flexible electronics seamlessly integrated into objects such as textiles[5,6]. To power these portable devices efficiently and reliably, there is a growing need for flexible energy storage solutions. Flexible batteries and capacitors play a crucial role in energizing wearable electronics and IoT devices. Miniaturized electronics offer the flexibility to carry devices comfortably, use them on the go, and adapt to different environments as well as ensure highly integrated devices like flexible micro-supercapacitors (FMSC) in manufacturing processes[7].

Traditional energy storage devices (i.e. batteries and capacitors) have undeniably played crucial roles in various applications. However, these devices come with inherent limitations, such as Limited Energy Density[8], Slow Charging and Discharging Rates[9], Limited Cycle Life[10], Rigid Form Factor[11], Environmental Impact[12], Limited Scalability and Manufacturing[13]and Safety Concerns[14] Analysing these limitations in depth reveals the obstacles they present for particular applications, which can be resolved by adopting flexible micro-supercapacitors. A FMSC is a miniaturized energy storage device, typically range in size from the micrometer scale to the millimeter scale that amalgamates the benefits of supercapacitors and flexible substrates. These devices are designed to be much smaller than conventional supercapacitors, with dimensions generally falling within the following ranges:

Length and Width: Typically between a few micrometers (µm) to a few millimeters (mm). For example, a flexible MSC might have a footprint of a few square millimeters, such as 1 mm² to 10 mm². Thickness: Often less than a millimeter, usually in the range of tens to hundreds of micrometers (µm). This thin profile is crucial for integration into flexible and wearable electronics. These dimensions make MSCs ideal for applications where space is limited, such as in microelectronics, wearable devices, and biomedical implants. Their small size allows them to be integrated directly onto chips or within flexible substrates, providing localized energy storage without significantly increasing the overall size or weight of the device.

Supercapacitors, also known as ultracapacitors or electrochemical capacitors, exhibit high power density and rapid charge/discharge capabilities, harnessing reversible electrochemical processes at the electrode-electrolyte interface to provide quick bursts of power[15]. On the other hand, flexible substrates, such as polymers or thin metal foils, contribute mechanical flexibility and conformability, allowing the micro-supercapacitor to bend, twist, and adapt to irregular shapes [16]. Recent research by Zhe Yan, et al. [17] underscores the importance of flexibility in micro-supercapacitors and highlights their potential for applications in wearable and flexible electronics. This research emphasizes the necessity for adaptable energy storage solutions in emerging technologies[18]. The crucial aspect of flexibility enhances their utility across a broad spectrum of applications, including Conformal Integration[19], Wearable Electronics[20], Bendable and Foldable Electronics[21], Miniaturization and Form Factor [22], Energy Harvesting and Autonomous Systems[23], Mechanical Robustness [24], Scalability and Manufacturing [25], Design Freedom and Versatility. FMSCs emerge as a comprehensive solution for overcoming the limitations of conventional energy storage devices in several ways. Firstly, FMSCs exhibit high power density, allowing them to rapidly store and release energy. This feature is particularly advantageous for applications requiring quick bursts of power, such as wearable devices or sensor networks. The incorporation of FMSCs ensures efficient and reliable operation, even in scenarios with limited energy storage. Traditional batteries face the constraint of a limited number of charge-discharge cycles before significant performance degradation occurs. In contrast, FMSCs boast a considerably longer cycle life, often ranging from thousands to millions of cycles[26,27] .This extended cycle life not only ensures prolonged functionality but also diminishes the need for frequent replacements, contributing to an overall extended lifespan for the device. The flexibility inherent in micro-supercapacitors enables them to conform to irregular shapes and integrate into various form factors. This adaptability facilitates their incorporation into wearable electronics, smart clothing, or bendable displays. By efficiently utilizing available space and embracing unconventional design possibilities, FMSCs maximize energy storage capacity in devices with limited physical dimensions. Furthermore, the integration of FMSCs with energy harvesting technologies allows the scavenging of energy from ambient sources such as solar, thermal, or mechanical energy[28–30]. This capability enables the capture and storage of environmental energy, reducing reliance on external power sources. In applications where energy sources are limited or intermittent, such as remote monitoring systems or IoT devices, FMSCs provide a reliable and sustainable energy storage solution. The compact size and lightweight nature of FMSCs make them ideal for portable devices. These devices offer energy storage without adding significant weight or bulk, enhancing mobility and convenience. Applications like wearable medical devices, electronic textiles, or portable electronics stand to benefit significantly from the use of FMSCs, enabling longer operation times and improving the overall user experience.

The advancement of FMSCs scales with progress in nanomaterials and microfabrication techniques. Researchers have explored a range of materials and fabrication methods to achieve concurrent high performance and flexibility. Commonly used materials for FMSCs electrodes encompass carbon-based materials (such as graphene and carbon nanotubes)[31–35], metal oxides (like manganese oxide and ruthenium oxide)[36–40]and conducting polymers (including polyaniline and polypyrrole)[41–44] .These materials boast excellent electrical conductivity, high surface area, and electrochemical stability, rendering them ideal for energy storage applications. In recent times, there has been a notable surge of interest in pioneering materials, such as 2D materials (Phosphorene, MXenes)[45–49], Organic Materials (Conjugated Polymers, Organic Redox Materials)[50–54],Nanocomposites (Hybrid Materials, Nanostructured Materials)[55–63], Quantum Dots[64–66], Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs)[67–69],Biomaterials[70–73] and Self-Healing Materials[74,75]. The fabrication process of flexible micro-supercapacitors typically involves deposition techniques such as chemical vapor deposition, Layer-by-Layer Assembly, screen printing, inkjet printing, 3D Printing and Additive Manufacturing, Roll-to-Roll Manufacturing, Template-Assisted Assembly, Electrospinning, Hybrid Fabrication Approaches, Template-Free Synthesis and Laser Direct Writing. These methods facilitate precise control over material thickness and electrode patterns, enabling the creation of microscale devices[76].

Furthermore, the careful selection of current collectors and electrolytes is pivotal in augmenting the performance and flexibility of micro-supercapacitors. The distinctive amalgamation of high energy density, high power density, and mechanical flexibility positions flexible micro-supercapacitors as highly appealing for diverse applications [77]. Their integration into wearable electronics, smart textiles, flexible displays, biomedical devices, and IoT devices demonstrates their versatility. For example, FMSCs can serve as power sources for energy harvesting, wearable health monitoring devices, facilitating long-term continuous monitoring without the need for frequent battery replacements. In structural health monitoring, they find application in self-powered and multi-functional sensors due to their flexibility, allowing conformal attachment to various surfaces[52,76,78]. Consequently, flexible micro-supercapacitors emerge as a promising solution to meet the escalating demand for portable and flexible energy storage devices. With the continuous refinement of advanced nanomaterials and microfabrication techniques, current studies are actively enhancing the key performance indicators of micro-supercapacitors. As the field progresses, it is anticipated that FMSCs will play a pivotal role in powering the next generation of portable and wearable electronic devices [79,80]. Fig. 1 illustrates schematic of the integration of FMSCs with smart wearable functional devices.

The planar FMSCs, with their remarkable attributes of high- power density, rapid charge/discharge rates, and extended operational lifetimes, are well-positioned to seamlessly integrate into the future of flexible, integrated, and downsized electronic systems. This adaptability makes them particularly well-suited for a diverse range of applications, reflecting their potential to revolutionize electronic technologies. As we stand at the nexus of innovation, the insights provided herein serve as a roadmap for researchers, engineers, and industry pioneers to navigate the evolving landscape of flexible micro-supercapacitors, fostering advancements that will shape the future of energy storage in the realm of miniaturized electronics. The current review unfolds through a structured exploration across six distinct sections, each dedicated to a fundamental aspect of the technology. Section 1 : Introduction establishes the context by underscoring the critical need for efficient, adaptable energy storage solutions in the realm of wearable and integrated electronics, setting the stage for a detailed discussion on the innovations and challenges in this field. Section 2 : Architecture and energy storage mechanism delves into the architectural fundamentals and operational principles governing these devices, focusing on their charge storage mechanisms and how their performance is influenced by mechanical flexibility. Moving forward, Section 3 : Electrode Materials and Electrolytes offers an in-depth analysis of the materials employed in these devices, detailing their impacts on the efficiency and stability of the supercapacitors. Section 4 : Fabrication Techniques examines the diverse manufacturing processes available, from traditional methods to cutting-edge techniques like 3D printing, and how they affect the scalability and durability of the final products. Finally, Section 5 : Applications and Integration discusses the practical deployment of flexible micro-supercapacitors in various industries, emphasizing their revolutionary potential in wearable technology and other flexible electronic formats, while considering integration challenges and market trajectories. In wrapping up Section 6, the review offers a Summary and Outlook, highlighting ongoing research challenges for the development of future wearable and portable electronic devices. This structured review aims to illuminate the comprehensive landscape of flexible micro-supercapacitor technology, showcasing current innovations and identifying future research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Architecture and energy storage mechanism of flexible micro-supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: Flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) are crucial for energy storage in modern flexible electronics, wearables, and portable devices. The architecture of these MSCs plays a vital role in determining their performance, flexibility, and potential applications [85]: The illustration of different architectures of FMSCs depicted in the Fig. 2 .

Fiber-type MSCs are designed around fibrous substrates, allowing integration into textiles or fiber-based electronic devices (Fig. 2a). This architecture is particularly suited for wearable electronics due to its ability to conform to complex shapes while maintaining high flexibility. The core of a fiber-type MSC typically consists of a conductive fiber, which serves as the current collector. This fiber can be made from materials like carbon fibers, metal wires, or conductive polymer fibers[86]. Active electrode materials, such as metal oxides, carbon-based materials, or conductive polymers, are deposited onto the fiber core using methods like chemical vapor deposition, electrochemical deposition, or direct coating. Following electrode deposition, a solid or gel electrolyte is applied, accompanied by a separator to prevent short circuits and ensure efficient ion transport between the electrodes. An outer encapsulating layer protects the MSC from environmental degradation, enhancing the device's longevity and reliability. These MSCs are highly flexible and can be woven into fabrics, making them ideal for smart textiles, wearable devices, and flexible sensors. They exhibit excellent mechanical stability under bending, twisting, and stretching, suitable for applications where the energy storage device needs to adapt to dynamic and complex shapes.

The island-bridge architecture enhances the flexibility of MSCs by separating rigid electrode areas, known as islands, with flexible interconnections, or bridges (Fig. 2b). This design allows the MSC to maintain electrical connectivity even under significant mechanical deformation, such as stretching, bending, and twisting[87]. The islands are composed of rigid, high-performance electrode materials that offer high energy and power densities, while the flexible bridges, made from soft materials like elastomers or thin metal traces, are designed in patterns that optimize stretchability and mechanical resilience. Active materials are deposited onto the islands, with the bridges serving as electrical pathways to ensure continuous conductivity during deformation. This architecture is ideal for applications in stretchable electronics, such as electronic skin, stretchable displays, and bio-integrated devices, where significant mechanical deformation is expected without compromising functionality.

Kirigami- and origami-inspired architectures in flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) draw from traditional Japanese arts to enhance flexibility and adaptability in energy storage devices. Kirigami, which involves cutting patterns into the electrode or substrate, creates movable structures like hinges and tabs that can stretch, bend, and twist while maintaining electrical performance (Fig. 2c). These strategically placed cuts, often made using techniques like laser cutting or photolithography, allow the device to conform to complex shapes and endure significant mechanical deformation, making them ideal for foldable devices, wearable sensors, and conformal biomedical applications[88,89]. On the other hand, origami-inspired FMSCs use folding techniques to achieve similar flexibility. Instead of cutting, origami relies on folding patterns that allow the device to compress or expand while retaining its structural and electrical integrity. These folds enable the creation of compact, adaptable energy storage solutions that can be integrated into space-limited applications like small wearable devices or expandable sensors. Both kirigami and origami designs offer unique approaches to enhancing the mechanical flexibility of FMSCs, making them suitable for various advanced flexible electronic applications.

Interdigital MSCs are one of the most widely used designs for flexible energy storage devices, featuring interdigitated fingers of electrodes deposited onto a flexible substrate[90]. The substrate, often made of flexible materials like polyimide, PET, or PDMS, is chosen for its flexibility, durability, and compatibility with various electrode deposition techniques. The electrodes are arranged in a comb-like, interdigitated pattern with alternating fingers of anode and cathode, deposited using microfabrication techniques like photolithography, screen printing, or laser scribing. Thin conductive layers, such as gold, silver, or conductive carbon, serve as current collectors, connecting the electrode fingers to external circuits (Fig. 2d). A gel or solid-state electrolyte fills the gaps between the interdigitated fingers, ensuring effective ionic conductivity while preventing electrical shorting between the electrodes. An encapsulating layer is added to protect the device from environmental factors, providing flexibility and impermeability[91]. The interdigitated design maximizes the electrode surface area, enhancing the capacitance and energy density of the MSC, while the small separation between the electrode fingers allows for rapid ion transport, resulting in high power density and fast charge/discharge rates. The planar configuration on a flexible substrate enables the MSC to bend and twist without losing performance, making it ideal for wearable electronics and flexible displays. This design is highly scalable, suitable for various applications from microscale devices to larger energy storage systems. Challenges in this architecture include ensuring uniform electrolyte distribution across the interdigitated fingers, maintaining mechanical integrity under repeated deformation, and ongoing research to improve the flexibility and durability of these devices. Future innovations in materials science, such as the development of stretchable and self-healing materials, are expected to further enhance the performance and longevity of interdigital MSCs.

Each of these flexible microsupercapacitor architectures offers unique advantages tailored to specific applications, whether in wearable electronics, stretchable devices, or highly deformable sensors. The choice of architecture depends on the specific requirements of the application, including flexibility, energy density, power density, and mechanical durability. The interdigital architecture is the most commonly used design in flexible microsupercapacitors (MSCs) due to its simplicity, efficiency, and adaptability across various applications. This architecture, characterized by its interdigitated electrode pattern, offers a robust platform for understanding the basic design principles and energy storage mechanisms in MSCs[92]. The interdigital architecture is favored for its ability to offer high surface area and efficient ion transport, which are critical for achieving high capacitance and power density. Additionally, the planar nature of this design on a flexible substrate allows the MSC to be integrated into various flexible electronic systems, including wearable devices, portable electronics, and energy harvesting systems. Interdigital MSCs are also scalable, making them suitable for applications ranging from microscale devices, like microelectromechanical systems (MEMS), to larger flexible energy storage systems. Their versatility, combined with the ability to customize the electrode materials and electrolyte, makes them a cornerstone in the development of advanced energy storage solutions for the next generation of electronics.

To better understand the energy storage mechanisms of the interdigital flexible microsupercapacitor (MSC), it's helpful to compare it with the traditional sandwich-structured flexible MSC, which is another common design used in energy storage devices. The classic sandwich-structure MSCs and the interdigital-structure MSCs. The traditional sandwich MSC follows a vertical arrangement, featuring two electrodes with an electrolyte sandwiched in between. Its performance is primarily influenced by the thickness of the separator and the electrode layers. The most effective way to enhance electrochemical performance is by increasing the load of active materials. However, due to structural constraints, this requirement can only be met by increasing the electrode thickness. Consequently, this extension of the ion diffusion channel results in lower power density. In contrast, MSCs with in-plane interdigital electrode designs have electrodes separated by an insulated gap (Fig. 3a ), offering several advantages. Firstly, the need for separators is eliminated, enabling ions to achieve higher diffusion efficiency. Secondly, owing to the distinctive finger electrode shape, electrolyte ion transport occurs predominantly in the transverse direction. Consequently, increasing the electrode thickness enhances the capacitance and energy density of MSCs without compromising power density. Thirdly, the in-plane integration is advantageous for system application and industrial production[93,94]. Notably, FMSCs, when designed with the traditional approach of two planar electrodes facing each other separated by an electrolyte, often fall short of meeting microelectronics criteria[95]. In contrast to standard supercapacitors (SCs), which comprise current collectors, electrode materials, a separator, and an electrolyte sandwiched together, FMSCs adopt a different structure. They feature interdigitated micro-electrode arrays on a substrate with the electrolyte deposited on top. Commonly, flexible films like polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), and cellulose fiber sheets serve as substrates. Unlike traditional SCs, FMSCs lack a separator, and the electrolyte filling the gap may operate as a separator. Notably, the open side edges of active materials are exposed to the electrolyte, facilitating electrolyte diffusion into electrodes[96] (Fig. 3b). Recent studies have explored FMSCs with an interdigitated arrangement of active electrodes to enhance device performance by fostering greater interaction between the electrolyte and electrode materials.

This design enhances the ion-accessible surface area between the electrolyte and the electrode materials, facilitating easier infiltration of ions from the electrolyte into the electrode materials. In contrast to the conventional sandwiched architecture, this innovative design allows for improved energy and power densities within a smaller space by controlling the size and arrangement of the interdigitated micro fingers[97]. Moreover, this architecture enables the planar integration of multiple devices without the need for complex wire connections. However, the current FMSCs with interdigitated electrode designs require careful selection of electrode materials, and recent investigations have focused on sophisticated fabrication techniques for electrode patterning to enhance the interaction between the electrolyte and electrode materials. FMSCs employing in-plane interdigitated electrode designs offer versatility and easy integration with other miniature electronic components. This configuration boasts advantages such as straightforward fabrication with a limited number of technological steps, compatibility with various electrolyte types (including liquid), ease of adjusting patterns to optimize areal energy and power densities, and increased accessibility to the active electrode material due to the exposed walls of the interdigitated electrode fingers. Nevertheless, depositing mechanically robust 3D electrode materials onto micro-sized patterns without causing short circuits between the positive and negative electrodes remains a significant challenge in realizing an interdigitated architecture. The key advantages of flexible micro-supercapacitors with in-plane interdigitated designs over flexible supercapacitors with sandwiched architecture are manifold. Firstly, the reduced gaps between electrodes may decrease ion diffusion paths in electrolytes, resulting in higher power density.

Secondly, the interdigitated design of FMSCs facilitates seamless integration with other microelectronics on a chip. MSCs can be closely merged on a chip to enhance output potential or current, thereby increasing MSC unit density by reducing complex connections. The unique interdigitated design of MSCs may reduce the risk of electrode short circuits when subjected to stresses. Additionally, the in-plane construction, as opposed to the sandwiched architecture, has the potential to eliminate sliding or dislocation between electrodes and separators[98] MSCs can be categorized into two types: symmetric and asymmetric ( Fig. 3c). Symmetric MSCs typically utilize active materials with high surface area and good conductivity, such as graphene and activated carbon. These materials store energy in efficient electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) through the fast and reversible adsorption and desorption of electrolyte ions at the activated surface (Fig. 3d). Carbon-based materials with numerous microspores and macrospores are highly sought for symmetric MSCs, as the holes serve as electrolyte reservoirs for ion diffusion, significantly shortening the ion transit length and thereby increasing specific capacitance. Furthermore, heteroatom doping (B, F, P, N) on the carbon matrix can significantly boost capacitance by initiating Faradic reactions, increasing the density around the Fermi level to enhance quantum capacitance, and enhancing affinity to electrolyte ions. For example, F-doped graphene and B-doped graphene MSCs demonstrated outstanding areal capacitances of 17.4 mF cm2 and 16.5 mF cm2, respectively, which were about ten times greater than undoped graphene MSCs. Notably, symmetric MSCs exhibited a high-power density of up to 1191 W cm3. However, their energy densities remain challenging. To address this, pseudocapacitive nanomaterials such as MXene, conductive polymers, and transition metal oxides have been utilized to create asymmetric MSCs and enhance energy storage capacity [99]. Furthermore, asymmetric MSCs with broader voltage output and higher energy density than symmetric MSCs have recently garnered attention in research [89,100,101] .Therefore, the interdigital structure holds significant promise in meeting the evolving demands of social development and daily life. Consequently, considerable attention has been directed towards the research and development of interdigital-structure MSCs. The performance of an interdigital-structure MSC is predominantly influenced by the electrode width, thickness, and gap size, as elucidated in various studies.

For instance, Li et al. [100,102] observed that, under identical conditions, the current density based on the overall device area increased as the finger width expanded from 300 to 600 μm. However, when considering only the electrode area, the areal capacitance decreased when the fingers exceeded 500 μm in width. Furthermore, Eunji Kim et al [100]. demonstrated the production of symmetric micro supercapacitors with a narrow 500 nm gap between electrode fingers, exhibiting a high areal capacitance of approximately 317 mF cm−2 at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. These devices retained over 30 % of their capacitance (∼104 mF cm−2) even at a high scan rate of 100 V s−1. The narrow 500 nm gap significantly enhanced electrochemical performance, and the use of electron beam lithography and photolithography allowed for the creation of nearly invisible devices with ultra-small footprints (0.04 mm2 device areas). This improvement in electrochemical performance is attributed to the dominance of increased diffusion length over enhanced conductivity when the finger width is excessively high. Additionally, an increase in thickness consistently led to a mounting areal capacitance.

Hence, through the thoughtful design of device structures, it is possible to achieve the highest areal capacitance of approximately 0.7 mF cm−2. Similarly, Chih et al. [101]demonstrated outstanding performance in an all-solid-state MSC by optimizing the device's structure and size. By adjusting parameters such as electrode width and gap width, the optimized MSC exhibited remarkable areal (7.7 mF cm−2) and volumetric (77.3 mF cm−3) capacitances. The rationale behind these findings is as follows: a larger electrode interspacing in the insulated gap reduces ion diffusion efficiency, impacting power density negatively. Additionally, an electrode with a width that is either too small or too large can lead to a decrease in capacitance due to insufficient active area or a long diffusion length, respectively. To enhance performance, narrowing the electrode interspacing and selecting an appropriate electrode width are crucial. Generally, increasing the ratio of electrode width to gap width (We/Wg) effectively reduces equivalent series resistance (ESR) and improves power density and energy density. Furthermore, to meet the flexibility requirements of wearable devices and intelligent systems, MSCs with superior tensile properties have been developed. Leveraging Kirigami patterning, Jiao et al [89]. successfully created all-solid-state MSC arrays (MSCAs) with excellent mechanical properties using MXene/bacterial cellulose (BC) composite papers and a simple laser-cutting process. The resulting MSCAs exhibited an aerial capacitance of 111.5 mF cm−2 and demonstrated good tensile, bending, and twisting properties, allowing them to adapt to various complex application scenarios. Hence, the meticulous design of MSCs, inspired by those with outstanding performances, along with an understanding of the relationships between performance and structural parameters, is crucial for enhancing electrochemical performance and expanding practical applications. These designs involve adjusting intrinsic parameters (such as electrode width, thickness, gap width, etc.) and the overall configuration of MSCs (such as Kirigami superstructure, etc.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode materials and electrolytes for flexible micro-supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: Flexible micro-supercapacitors (FMSCs) represent a cutting-edge technology in energy storage, offering high power density, rapid charge-discharge capabilities, and mechanical flexibility, making them ideal for integration into modern wearable and portable electronics. The performance of FMSCs is largely determined by the choice of electrode materials and electrolytes, which together govern the device's energy storage capacity, charge transfer efficiency, and mechanical durability. A variety of electrode materials have been explored to enhance the performance of FMSCs, including traditional carbon-based materials like activated carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, which are valued for their high surface area and excellent electrical conductivity. Additionally, novel two-dimensional (2D) materials such as MXenes, Metal Organic Frame works (MoFs) and black phosphorus have emerged as promising candidates due to their unique layered structures, which provide a large surface area and facilitate efficient ion intercalation, significantly boosting capacitance and energy density. These 2D materials also offer tunable electronic properties and mechanical flexibility, making them highly suitable for use in FMSCs. Furthermore, metal oxides (e.g., MnO₂, RuO₂), conducting polymers (e.g., polyaniline, polypyrrole), and hybrid materials that combine carbon with metal oxides or polymers have shown potential in enhancing both capacitance and mechanical flexibility. On the other hand, electrolytes, which can be liquid, solid-state, or gel-based, play a pivotal role in facilitating ion transport between electrodes and influencing the overall electrochemical stability and operational voltage window of the device. This section explores the latest advancements and challenges in electrode materials, including these novel 2D materials and electrolytes for FMSCs, highlighting their impact on the performance, flexibility, and application potential of these next-generation energy storage devices.

Carbon-based electrode materials are crucial for the development of flexible micro supercapacitors (FMSCs) due to their unique combination of properties that meet the demands of advanced energy storage devices. These materials, such as activated carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, offer a high specific surface area, which maximizes the electrode-electrolyte interface, leading to greater charge storage capacity. Their excellent electrical conductivity ensures efficient charge transport, enabling rapid charging and discharging, which is essential for high-power applications in portable electronics. Additionally, the mechanical flexibility of carbon-based materials allows FMSCs to endure bending and stretching without performance degradation, making them ideal for wearable devices. Furthermore, carbon materials are chemically stable, ensuring long-term reliability, and their versatility allows for easy processing into various forms, enhancing their applicability. Importantly, many carbon-based materials, particularly those derived from biomass, are sustainable and cost-effective, supporting large-scale production and aligning with environmental goals. These combined attributes make carbon-based electrodes indispensable in the advancement of FMSCs for use in flexible, wearable, and portable electronics.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are widely recognized for their significant role in enhancing the performance of various devices across diverse applications. Within the carbon family, CNTs can be classified into two main types: single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The accidental synthesis of SWCNTs occurred in 1991 through the arc-discharged method by Iijima and Ichihashi [103]. SWCNTs possess a cylindrical structure formed by folding a single layer of graphene from edge to edge, with an effective diameter of approximately 1 nm and lengths reaching up to 1 mm. Despite their impressive properties, the production cost of SWCNTs is relatively higher compared to MWCNTs [104]. In contrast, MWCNTs consist of concentric tubes made of graphene sheets, and their diameter varies due to multiple folds of these sheets. The interplanar distance between the concentric cylinders is maintained by internal Coulombic forces, typically ranging from 0.32 nm to 0.35 nm, which is similar to the interplanar distance between successive graphene layers in graphite [105]. MWCNTs are preferred in electronic device applications over SWCNTs due to their stronger adhesion profile with the substrate surface. This enhanced adhesion is attributed to the availability of more active sites at the end of the MWCNT tube [106], contributing to improved substrate interaction [107]. In certain instances, MWCNTs can be considered as double-walled carbon nanotubes (DWCNTs), resembling cylinders of graphene layers with an interlayer space of 0.335 nm. The physical and chemical properties of DWCNTs closely resemble those of SWCNTs. Despite the similarities, MWCNTs, especially in their extreme form as DWCNTs, offer advantages in electronic device applications, leveraging their enhanced adhesion properties and the presence of multiple active sites. This distinction makes MWCNTs, including DWCNTs, a preferred choice for various technological advancements.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) stand out as highly promising materials for applications in flexible micro-supercapacitors (FMSCs), playing a crucial role as electrodes to significantly enhance both the energy and power densities of these energy storage devices. One of the key attributes of CNTs is their remarkable mechanical property[108]. CNTs exhibit exceptional strength and flexibility, allowing them to withstand mechanical stress and deformation. This mechanical resilience is particularly advantageous in flexible supercapacitors, where the electrode material needs to endure bending, stretching, and other deformations without compromising performance. The flexibility of CNTs enables the development of robust and durable flexible supercapacitors suitable for various applications. Another essential characteristic of CNTs is their high electrical conductivity[109]. CNTs possess excellent electron transport properties, facilitating efficient charge and discharge processes in supercapacitors. The high electrical conductivity of CNTs ensures rapid electron mobility within the electrode material, leading to enhanced overall performance and reduced internal resistance. This property is crucial for achieving high power densities in supercapacitors, making them suitable for applications requiring quick bursts of energy. CNTs also boast a large surface area, providing ample active sites for charge storage. This increased surface area allows for a higher amount of electrochemically active sites, contributing to the overall capacitance of the supercapacitor. The large surface area of CNTs is particularly beneficial in storing electrical energy through the electrostatic adsorption of ions at the electrode-electrolyte interface [81]. Furthermore, CNTs are known for their ease of functionalization. The surface chemistry of CNTs can be modified by adding functional groups or incorporating other materials, allowing for tailored properties and improved interactions with electrolytes in supercapacitors. This functionalization capability enhances the overall electrochemical performance and stability of CNT-based FMSCs. In a recent study led by Lyu and colleagues [110], an innovative method was employed, utilizing highly conductive single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) and hydrophilic cellulose nanofibers (CNF) to intercalate and disperse Ti3C2Tx, resulting in the creation of stable and uniform hybrid hydrogels (Fig. 4a
). These hydrogels were employed as film electrodes in the fabrication of symmetrical all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors and interdigitated micro-supercapacitors. The incorporation of SWCNT into the aerogel film electrode significantly enhanced its conductivity, while the inclusion of CNF imparted exceptional wettability and flexibility. The resultant fibrous conductive network structure, comprising Ti3C2Tx/SWCNT/CNF within the aerogel film electrode, provided an increased number of active sites, improved ion transport, and enhanced diffusion paths. The symmetrical all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor electrode demonstrated an impressive area-specific capacitance of 746.68 mF cm2, accompanied by outstanding cycle stability (91.23 % capacity retention after 10,000 cycles) and favorable bending stability. Additionally, the interdigitated micro-supercapacitor electrode exhibited an area-specific capacitance of 244.50 mFcm2. This research introduces a novel approach for harnessing the properties of Ti3C2Tx in conjunction with SWCNT and CNF, showcasing the potential for developing high-performance, flexible supercapacitors with applications in advanced electronics and microdevices. Naik and his research team [111] have successfully developed ternary nanocomposites comprising multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), silver (Ag), and sulfonated polyaniline (SPANI) using a straightforward method. These nanocomposites were integrated into a flexible membrane, with the hybrid substrate formed through TEOS cross-linked polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). The resulting ternary nanocomposite membrane demonstrated exceptional electrochemical performance, revealing a specific capacitance of 572.8 Fg⁻¹ at 1 mAg⁻¹ in a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. Impressively, it exhibited a cycle-life stability of 83.5 % even after 2500 cycles. The remarkable electrochemical properties indicate that the MWCNTs-Ag-SPANI ternary nanocomposite, incorporated into the cross-linked PVA, holds promise as a highly efficient electrode material for supercapacitors. The fabricated device showcased a specific capacitance of 60.95 Fg⁻¹, an energy density of 7.46 Whkg⁻¹, and a power density of 67.81 Wkg⁻¹ at a current density of 5.0 mAg⁻¹. The higher current density observed in the ternary nanocomposite membrane is attributed to its elevated electrical conductivity, rapid ion transport, and expansive surface area resulting from the synergistic effects of MWCNTs, Ag, and sulfonated PANI. Based on its outstanding electrochemical performance, the newly designed and fabricated micro-supercapacitor holds great promise for various supercapacitor applications.

Hu and collaborators [114]introduced a novel design involving the in-situ growth of active materials on a substrate. In this method, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) containing oxides were grown on the surface of nickel foam through a metal-induced deoxidation process. Subsequently, NiCoO2 nanosheets were developed on the CNT films using a hydrothermal reaction, resulting in a meticulously structured hierarchical 3D network. This innovative architecture ensures robust bonding of active materials to the substrate, reducing contact resistance, improving electrode stability, facilitating rapid ion transport through well-organized conductivity pathways, and providing an abundance of active sites. These features endowed the NiCoO2@CNT@NF electrode with exceptional properties, including a high specific capacitance of 929 F g⁻¹ (or an aerial capacitance of 3350 mF cm²) at 5 mA cm⁻², superior rate capability with 71.5 % retention from 5 to 50 mA cm⁻², and durable stability exceeding 90 % after 5000 cycles. To further showcase the electrode's capabilities, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device was assembled, pairing the remarkable NiCoO2@CNT@NF electrode with a universal activated carbon@nickel foam (AC@NF) electrode. This innovative design resulted in an impressive specific capacitance of 151 F g⁻¹ at 5 mA cm⁻², outstanding rate capability of 83.8 %, and a high energy density of 56.0 Wh kg⁻¹ at a power density of 500.3 W kg⁻¹. Son et.al [113] reported a twistable and hydrophilic coiled CNT yarn produced by the facile electrochemical oxidation (ECO) method (Fig. 4c). The resulting yarns are effectively functionalized with oxygen-containing groups, showcasing enhanced extrinsic hydrophilicity and a substantial improvement in capacitance, approximately 17 times higher than before (Fig. 4d). The underlying reasons for this capacitance enhancement are quantitatively elucidated through a combination of theoretical simulations and experimental observations. Stretchable supercapacitors, constructed with coiled carbon nanotube (CNT) yarns treated with extracellular oxidase (ECO), exhibit remarkable capacitance values (12.48 mF/cm and 172.93 mF/cm2, respectively) and exceptional stretchability (80 %). Furthermore, these ECO-treated coiled CNT yarns demonstrate robust mechanical strength, allowing them to be woven into a wearable mask functioning as a supercapacitor. Hence, carbon nanotubes serve as a linchpin in the advancement of FMSCs, bringing together a unique combination of electrical, mechanical, and structural properties. Their integration enables the creation of energy storage solutions that align with the demands of emerging technologies, ranging from wearable electronics to compact portable devices. As research and development in this field progress, carbon nanotubes continue to play a central role in pushing the boundaries of energy storage innovation. Zhang et al. [112]utilizing a hierarchical Na-MnOx@NCF/CNTF as the positive electrode and VN NSAs/CNTF as the negative electrode, employing a facile cut-and-transfer method. The positive and negative electrode materials were chosen for their high specific capacitance and wide electrochemical potential window. The achieved results demonstrated an outstanding specific capacitance of 109.5 mF cm−2 and an exceptional energy density of 87.62 mW h cm−2 for our AMSC device (Fig. 4b). Notably, the device exhibited negligible capacitance decay even after undergoing 3000 bending cycles, underscoring its excellent mechanical stability. The proposed method offers a relatively simple and cost-effective strategy for the rational design and fabrication of prototypical high-voltage AMSCs with superior electrochemical performance. This work represents a significant advancement in the development of flexible energy storage devices with enhanced capacitance, energy density, and mechanical stability. Hence, carbon nanotubes serve as a key component in the development of flexible micro-supercapacitors, enabling the construction of compact, flexible, and high-performance energy storage devices for emerging technologies.

Graphene-based electrode materials have become pivotal in advancing flexible micro supercapacitors (FMSCs) due to their outstanding physical and chemical properties, which significantly enhance the performance and adaptability of these energy storage devices. Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice, offers an extraordinarily high surface area of up to 2630 m²/g. This extensive surface area is crucial for increasing the electrode's capacitance, as it provides ample space for ion adsorption and storage, directly improving the energy storage capability of FMSCs [115,116].. Moreover, graphene's exceptional electrical conductivity facilitates efficient charge transport, enabling rapid charge-discharge cycles essential for high-power applications. This efficiency is crucial in applications where quick energy delivery is needed [117]. Additionally, graphene's intrinsic mechanical flexibility and strength make it an ideal material for FMSCs, particularly in scenarios where the electrodes must withstand bending, stretching, and folding without losing functionality. This mechanical resilience allows graphene-based supercapacitors to be seamlessly integrated into wearable and portable electronic devices, where durability and flexibility are paramount [118].. Graphene's chemical stability under various environmental conditions further ensures the long-term reliability and durability of FMSCs, which is critical for devices expected to operate over extended periods. This stability, coupled with the material's versatility, allows graphene to be easily modified or combined with other materials, such as metal oxides, conducting polymers, or other carbon allotropes. Such hybrid electrodes exhibit enhanced electrochemical properties, including increased capacitance, energy density, and mechanical robustness, broadening the applicability of graphene-based FMSCs across various cutting-edge technologies. Significant advances in graphene-based flexible MSCs have been made, as demonstrated by various innovative approaches. For example, Xiaoyu Shi and colleagues [116] have reported a novel, cost-effective method for the rapid and scalable fabrication of patterned laser-induced graphene (LIG) used in all-solid-state planar integrated micro supercapacitors (LIG-MSCs). This approach allows for the creation of LIG-MSCs with various customizable shapes, exceptional flexibility, uniform performance, superior modularity, and high-temperature stability. The conductive and porous LIG patterns, made up of randomly stacked graphene nanosheets, function both as microelectrodes and interconnects. These LIG-MSCs exhibit a remarkable areal capacitance of 0.62 mF/cm² and demonstrate robust electrical double-layer capacitive behavior, maintaining stable performance with no capacitance degradation after 10,000 cycles. Additionally, the LIG-MSCs offer outstanding mechanical flexibility and the ability to adjust voltage and capacitance by configuring cells in series or parallel, allowing for customizable performance. When operated with an ionogel electrolyte, these all-solid-state LIG-MSCs maintain high stability even at elevated temperatures of 100 °C, with 90 % capacitance retention after 3000 cycles, highlighting their exceptional reliability. Consequently, LIG-MSCs present significant potential for integration into miniature power sources within advanced microelectronics. Another significant development comes from Truong-Son Dinh Le et al [119]. who introduced an innovative method for fabricating conductive graphene micropatterns on fallen leaves using ultrashort laser pulses depicted in Fig. 5a . This technique leverages the heat accumulation from repetitive laser pulses to precisely control the base temperature, enabling effective carbonization of the leaves. The use of ultra-intense laser pulses generates extremely high temperatures, which convert amorphous carbon into graphitic carbon. The resulting green flexible FsLIG-MSCs, produced on leaves, exhibit impressive electrochemical performance, with an areal capacitance of 34.68 mF/cm² at a scan rate of 5 mV/s and approximately 99 % capacitance retention after 50,000 charge-discharge cycles. Furthermore, a single leaf-based FsLIG-MSC is capable of powering small devices such as LEDs or a table clock. Unlike conventional continuous wave (CW) and long-pulsed lasers, which struggle with thin and heat-sensitive substrates, ultrashort-pulsed lasers present a promising advancement for creating LIG microelectrodes from various carbon precursors. This method holds great potential for applications in green wearable electronics, smart homes, and the Internet of Things. Yongjiu Yuan et al [120]. have further advanced the field by developing a flexible, customizable micro-supercapacitor that can be fabricated using a single-pulse laser photonic-reduction stamping technique shown in Fig. 5b. This method is remarkably efficient, capable of generating a thousand spatially shaped laser pulses per second, resulting in the production of over 30,000 micro-supercapacitors within just 10 min. The micro-supercapacitors created have narrow gaps down to 500 nm, with the micro-supercapacitor structures measuring only a few dozen microns. Thanks to the unique three-dimensional structure of the laser-induced graphene-based electrodes, a single micro-supercapacitor exhibits an ultra-high energy density of 0.23 Wh/cm³, an exceptionally small time constant of 0.01 ms, outstanding specific capacitance of 128 mF/cm² and 426.7 F/cm³, along with excellent long-term cyclability. This innovative technique is highly desirable for a wide range of applications, overcoming the current challenges of high-throughput fabrication and low energy density in micro-supercapacitors. Sujit Deshmukh et al. [121] describe a single-step roll-to-roll manufacturing technique for producing laser-induced MXene-derived Ti/O-rich nanoparticles that are covalently bound to graphene. This process results in a stable nanoarchitecture formed at elevated lasing temperatures around 2000 K, with molecular dynamics (MD) simulations confirming the formation of a TiOC covalent bond at the MXene-graphene interface depicted in Fig. 5c. The resulting capacitor electrode demonstrates a specific areal capacity of approximately 373 mF/cm² at a current density of 2 mA/cm² in a 1 M H₂SO₄ electrolyte (three-electrode cell), and around 88 mF/cm² at 0.25 mA/cm² in a PVA/H₂SO₄ electrolyte (two-electrode cell). It also boasts an ultra-high energy density of 21.16 × 10⁻³ mWh/cm², which is about 10 times greater than that of commercially available supercapacitors and is even comparable to a 4 V/500 μAh thin-film lithium battery. Additionally, the O-LIM-LIG micro-supercapacitor exhibits excellent cyclic stability, with 91 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles an essential feature compared to microbatteries, which often face significant lifetime issues when integrated into biomedical implants, RFID tags, or microsensors where replacement or maintenance is challenging. These micro-supercapacitors can be directly integrated into such biomedical devices, contributing to more efficient self-powered real-time health monitoring systems. When operating in an actual application, the substrates and electrode materials of the FMSCs are often subjected to various mechanical damage from the outside. These failures constrained the reliability and stability of the supercapacitor and led to whole scale breakdown of the device. Therefore, self-healing materials are expected to further develop powerful and long-lived functional devices. In the realm of self-healing materials, Yang Yue et al. [122]developed a self-healable 3D micro-supercapacitor (MSC) using a composite aerogel electrode made of MXene (Ti₃C₂Tₓ) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), encased in a self-healing polyurethane outer shell. The MXene-rGO composite aerogel combines the high specific surface area of rGO with the excellent conductivity of MXene, preventing the self-restacking of the lamella structure and partially mitigating MXene oxidation (Fig. 5d).

This 3D MSC achieves a high area-specific capacitance of 34.6 mF/cm² at a scan rate of 1 mV/s and demonstrates impressive cycling stability with 91 % capacitance retention over 15,000 cycles. The 3D self-healing micro-supercapacitor (MSC) maintained its approximate rectangular shape even after the fifth healing cycle. Following the fifth healing, the device exhibited a capacitance retention rate of approximately 81.7 %. During the cutting–healing process, the MSC undergoes vigorous operation, which can cause misalignment in the reconnection of the disconnected electrodes. However, the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) results confirm the effective recovery of the 3D MSCs. Besides these studies, 2D graphene has been widely used in printable flexible MSCs. Very recently, Jiaxin Ma and his team[116] have developed 2D heterostructures featuring a high specific surface area, ultrathin in-plane structure, and exceptional conductivity. These innovations have been utilized to create high-capacitance microelectrodes for flexible solid-state FZG-MSCs, as well as highly sensitive sensor materials for planar mPPy/G sensors aimed at NH3 detection in an all-flexible, self-sustained sensor system. The printable flexible FZG-MSCs demonstrated an impressive areal capacitance of 25.2 mF/cm², along with an excellent areal energy density of 9.46 μWh/cm², and exhibited enhanced cycling stability. Therefore, graphene-based electrodes offer immense potential for advancing flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) due to their remarkable surface area, electrical conductivity, and mechanical flexibility. However, challenges such as aggregation, low volumetric capacitance, scalability issues, chemical instability, and interface limitations with electrolytes need to be addressed to fully harness these advantages. Future developments should focus on mitigating restacking, enhancing volumetric capacitance, improving chemical stability, optimizing the electrode-electrolyte interface, and developing cost-effective, scalable production methods. Additionally, integrating graphene with hybrid materials and self-healing technologies could further elevate the performance and durability of FMSCs, paving the way for their widespread adoption in advanced energy storage applications.

Activated carbon-based electrodes are highly valued in the development of flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) due to their favorable properties that enhance the performance and versatility of these energy storage devices. Derived from carbon-rich materials through activation processes, activated carbon features a highly porous structure with an extensive surface area, often exceeding 1000 m²/g. This porous nature provides ample sites for ion adsorption, which translates into higher capacitance and energy density, making activated carbon ideal for supercapacitors[123]. Additionally, its porous structure facilitates rapid ion diffusion, improving charge and discharge rates. Although activated carbon does not inherently possess high electrical conductivity compared to materials like graphene or metals, its conductivity can be enhanced through chemical activation or by incorporating conductive additives. This adjustment is crucial for efficient charge transport and optimal device performance. Activated carbon electrodes can be fabricated into flexible forms, making them suitable for integration into FMSCs that need to endure bending, stretching, and folding, which is essential for applications in wearable electronics and other flexible devices. Cost-effectiveness is another significant advantage of activated carbon, as it is relatively inexpensive and widely available. This affordability makes it an attractive option for large-scale production of supercapacitors. Furthermore, activated carbon's chemical stability ensures long-term reliability and performance, maintaining effectiveness over extended periods and under various environmental conditions. In practical applications, activated carbon can be combined with flexible substrates such as polymer films or textiles. Techniques like screen printing, spray coating, or casting are commonly used to apply activated carbon onto these substrates, creating robust and adaptable electrodes. For instance, Qin Wang et al [124]. demonstrated the use of hierarchical oxygen-enriched porous carbons synthesized from Trachycarpus fortunei silk waste. By treating the carbon with dilute sulfuric acid, they increased the specific surface area and mesopore fraction, resulting in an electrode with an impressive specific capacitance of 469 F/g. This electrode maintained 95.06 % capacitance retention after 20,000 cycles and was used in a flexible supercapacitor with a high specific capacitance and energy density, showing excellent mechanical flexibility. Similarly, Dongming Liu's[125] research on biomass-derived carbons prepared by KOH activation highlights the advantages of a three-dimensional hierarchical porous structure. These carbons achieved a specific capacitance of 433 F/g and exhibited remarkable cyclability with no capacitance loss after 50,000 cycles. The resulting flexible micro-supercapacitors demonstrated a high areal capacitance and energy density, maintaining almost 100 % capacitance retention even under extreme bending conditions. Graphene-based electrodes, while widely studied, face limitations due to their low specific surface area and ion transport challenges caused by the restacking of graphene sheets. Huaxin Ma and colleagues[126] addressed these issues by developing 2D porous carbon nano-flakes from MXene through chlorine etching. These porous flakes exhibited a high specific surface area and ion transport channels, resulting in enhanced electrochemical performance compared to traditional graphene electrodes. In another approach, He Wang et al. [127]utilized polymer waste to produce activated carbon and MnO2-RAC electrodes, forming an all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor. This device showed a high specific capacitance and energy density, with excellent flexibility and cycle life. Fan Zeng's team[128] introduced a three-dimensional porous carbon fiber wrapped with metal oxides and interconnected with graphene sheets. This multi-conductive ink approach improved electron and ion conduction, resulting in high capacitance and energy density. The supercapacitor also demonstrated excellent flexibility and durability, capable of powering devices such as LEDs and timers. Yide Qiao and colleagues[129] developed an alga-based carbon sieve with an interconnected hierarchical porous structure. This innovative design facilitated electrolyte diffusion and ion transport, achieving a high specific capacitance and energy density for their supercapacitors. Jothi Ramalingam Rajabathar's [130]work with cow dung-based activated carbon showed that hierarchical structures could be created at various temperatures, resulting in high surface area and good electrochemical performance. The fabricated supercapacitor exhibited significant capacitance and stability. Xinrui Ding et al. [131]proposed a novel packaging strategy for activated carbon using a binder-free approach with composite films and selective laser carving. This method enhanced the flexibility and performance of the supercapacitors, achieving high areal capacitance and energy density while maintaining excellent flexibility after numerous bending cycles. To further enhance performance, activated carbon electrodes can be hybridized with materials like conductive polymers, metal oxides, or graphene. This hybrid approach leverages the high surface area of activated carbon while addressing its conductivity limitations. Activated carbon-based electrodes are well-suited for various applications, including wearable electronics, flexible displays, and portable energy storage devices. Their flexibility and cost-effectiveness make them particularly appealing for integration into consumer electronics and smart textiles. Future research could focus on optimizing the activation processes to improve surface area and conductivity, exploring new methods for combining activated carbon with advanced materials, and developing scalable fabrication techniques. Overall, activated carbon remains a valuable material in the development of flexible microsupercapacitors, balancing performance, cost, and versatility.

MXenes are a class of 2D nanomaterials that have gained significant attention and are often referred to as "wonder materials" due to their remarkable properties and potential applications[132], discovered in 2011[133], MXenes have been extensively researched and developed over the past decade, with various synthesis methods contributing to their versatile nature [134] . While the initial method involved HF etching, novel approaches such as water-free etching and molten salts etching have been introduced, expanding the possibilities for MXene applications[135]. One of the key features of MXenes is their multifunctional surface chemistry and unique structure, which contribute to their coveted properties. This versatility opens up a wide range of potential applications for MXenes, with a particular focus on their excellent electrochemical performance[136]. This outstanding performance makes MXenes promising materials for energy devices, particularly in the realm of flexible electronic devices. The unique properties of MXenes that make them suitable for energy applications include high electrical conductivity, large surface area, and excellent electrochemical stability. These characteristics make MXenes ideal candidates for various energy storage and conversion devices, such as batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells [137]. Their flexibility is especially advantageous in the development of flexible electronic devices, where traditional materials may fall short. In energy storage applications, MXenes have shown great promise in enhancing the performance of batteries and supercapacitors. Their ability to accommodate ions and provide efficient charge storage contributes to improved energy density and cycling stability [138]. Additionally, MXenes' excellent conductivity facilitates rapid charge and discharge processes, further enhancing the overall efficiency of energy storage devices. MXenes' potential extends to the field of electrocatalysis, where they can play a crucial role in fuel cells [139]. Their unique surface chemistry and structure make them effective catalysts for various electrochemical reactions, contributing to the development of efficient and sustainable energy conversion technologies. MXenes with slit-shaped ion diffusion channels as promising candidates. However, the widespread production of MSCs using MXene-based films still poses a considerable challenge. Xu et al. [140] the successful fabrication of large-area flexible freestanding double-MXenes (Ti3C2Tx/V2CTx) hybrid films through a straightforward one-step rolling process. Subsequently, these large-area hybrid films were efficiently processed into micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) using a laser-cutting method shown in Fig. 6a . The strategic stacking of Ti3C2Tx with V2CTx nanosheets in hetero-structured architectures proved effective in mitigating the self-restacking tendencies of MXene nanosheets. This stacking approach facilitated the construction of a highly interconnected conductive network, contributing to expanded interlayer spacing and accelerated charge transport processes. The resulting double-MXenes hybrid films demonstrated a substantial enhancement in gravimetric capacitance, reaching up to 496 F g⁻¹ (2.3 F cm⁻²), a notable improvement compared to pure MXene films. Even under a high scan rate of 200 mV s⁻¹, the gravimetric capacitances of the hybrid films exhibited a remarkable 72.5 % retention of the initial value at 2 mV s⁻¹, highlighting excellent high-rate performance. Furthermore, the all-solid-state MSCs derived from these hybrid films exhibited a high energy density of 4.9 mWh g⁻¹ at 350 mW g⁻¹. The MSCs displayed exceptional durability, with 95 % capacitance retention after undergoing 10,000 charge–discharge cycles. This work signifies a significant advancement in the development of high-performance flexible MSCs based on double-MXenes hybrid films.

Zhitan Wu et al. [49]present a novel assembly strategy for the production of MXene film electrodes using a combination of microgels and individual nanosheets. While freestanding MXene films exhibit promise for compact energy storage due to their high pseudocapacitance and density, the rate capability is impeded by sluggish ion transport resulting from the densely packed structure. In response, the authors propose a reassembly strategy to construct freestanding and flexible MXene-based film electrodes with a tunable porous structure. In this approach, Ti3C2Tx microgels, disassembled from a 3D structured hydrogel, are reassembled alongside individual Ti3C2Tx nanosheets in varying mass ratios. This results in a densely packed 3D network at the microscale and a film morphology at the macroscale. The optimized balance between density and porosity maximizes the space utilization of the produced film, leading to a high volumetric capacitance of 736 F cm⁻³ at an ultrahigh scan rate of 2000 mV s⁻¹. The fabricated supercapacitor demonstrates superior energy density, reaching 40 Wh L⁻¹ at a power density of 0.83 kW L⁻¹. Even at higher power densities, such as 41.5 kW L⁻¹, an energy density of 21 Wh L⁻¹ is maintained.
