These values represent the highest reported to date for symmetric supercapacitors in aqueous electrolytes. Moreover, the reassembled films exhibit excellent flexibility and integrability, making them promising candidates for flexible supercapacitor electrodes.

Zhang et al., [141] have successfully demonstrated the development of additive-free MXene inks and the direct printing of high-performance, all-MXene micro-supercapacitors with exceptional resolution. The printed flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) exhibit outstanding electrochemical performance, showcasing a volumetric capacitance of up to 562 F cm⁻³ and an energy density as high as 0.32 µW h cm⁻². These results surpass all other known printed MSCs, marking a significant advancement in the field. Extrusion printing utilizing MXene aqueous ink exploits its favorable fluidic properties, characterized by its viscous nature (Fig. 6b). This viscosity facilitates precise deposition of ink, as demonstrated by the diverse fine-printed patterns depicted in Fig. 6b. Notably, printing two paths yields Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) with a line gap of approximately 120 μm (Fig. 6c), a width of around 438 μm, and spatial uniformity within approximately 5.6 %. The resulting printed lines consist of interconnected nanosheets, forming a continuous metallic network. The preservation of characteristic Raman peaks of MXene along different directions (Fig. 6c and insets) signifies the absence of oxidation during extrusion printing. While the precise thickness measurement of lines printed on paper is hindered by the rough substrate surface. The direct printing of MXene ink holds fundamental importance not only in energy storage and harvesting but also extends to diverse fields such as electronics, circuits, packaging, and sensors, where cost-effective and easily integrable components are crucial. Additionally, the scalable, facile, and low-cost route for formulating MXene ink, which is additive- and binary-solvent-free, enables new possibilities for applications in smart electronics, sensors, electromagnetic shielding, antennas, and various other technological domains. This innovative low-temperature printing technique opens avenues for the development of advanced materials in a cost-effective and environmentally friendly manner, promising widespread applications in diverse electronic and sensing technologies.

Duan and his team [142]have pioneered a remarkable advancement in on-chip technology by creating an all-MXene multifunctional system seamlessly integrated on a single chip. Through a streamlined one-step laser scribing process, they ingeniously combined three key components: a MXene wireless charging coil (WCC), a MXene zinc-ion micro-supercapacitor (MSC), and a DCTES-MXene photodetector depicted in Fig. 6d. Despite its compact size of only 1.78 cm2, the system's capabilities are vast. The WCC, crafted from MXene material, serves as a receiver for wireless energy transmission, efficiently captured and stored in the MSC. This stored energy powers the photodetector, showcasing the system's self-sustaining functionality. Impressively, the integrated system boasts rapid charging, achieving full capacity within a mere 2 min, and maintains exceptional performance over 100 wireless charging and discharging cycles, retaining 90 % of its capacity. This ground-breaking achievement not only highlights the efficacy of the one-step laser scribing technique but also unveils the extensive potential for flexible integrated systems. From IoT applications to intelligent healthcare and environmental monitoring, the possibilities are boundless, promising a transformative impact across various domain. Cao and his team[143] have introduced a ground-breaking development in high-energy-density and ultra-stretchable energy storage with their creation of a ZIH-MPC (zinc-ion hybrid micro-supercapacitor). This innovative device utilizes an in situ reconstructed Ag nanoparticles on MXene nanosheets (Ag-NP-MXene) flexible film cathode, complemented by a matching metallic zinc anode and a polyacrylamide (PAM)/ZnCl2 hydrogel electrolyte. The incorporation of in situ reconstructed Ag nanoparticles on MXene nanosheets serves a dual purpose: enhancing electronic conductivity and participating in the redox-conversion of Ag0/Ag+, alongside ion adsorption/desorption processes. Ex situ XRD analysis further elucidated the dual mechanism of ion adsorption/desorption and Ag-redox within the Ag-NP-MXene cathode. Assembled ZIH-MPCs, based on the Ag-NP-MXene cathode, demonstrate exceptional voltage output stability and achieve energy densities comparable to traditional batteries. Moreover, the team has pioneered the design and fabrication of a novel stretchable ZIH-MPC array, featuring a liquid metal bridge-island architecture. This array maintains stable voltage and energy output even when subjected to a 100 % elongation state (Fig. 6e). This work represents a significant advancement in the field, offering an efficient strategy for constructing super-stretchable and high-energy electrochemical energy storage devices with diverse applications in flexible electronics and wearable technologies.

Black phosphorus (BP) has emerged as a promising candidate for supercapacitors (SCs)[144,145]. BP, being a two-dimensional material, exhibits an orthorhombic crystalline structure where each phosphorus atom is covalently bonded to three neighboring phosphorus atoms, forming a hexagonal structure with adjacent layers held together by weak van der Waals forces[146]. Its recent discovery has attracted considerable attention due to its ability to tune its bandgap within the range of 0.30 to 2.2 eV, high carrier mobility of 1000 cm−2 V−1 s−1 for electronic applications, remarkable chemical activity, and extensive surface area, making it highly suitable for energy storage devices and catalytic reactions. BP offers numerous advantages for SCs, including a high packing density of 2.69 g cm−3 compared to graphene's 1.1 g cm−3 and MoS2's 1.35 g cm−3. This high packing density enables it to store a substantial volumetric capacitance, along with rapid ion transport characteristics and enhanced electrical conductivity of approximately 300 S m−1 [147]. Many 2D materials exhibit exceptional physical, chemical, and electrochemical properties. By comparing certain properties of BP to other 2D materials, we can highlight its advantages. Electrode materials with a high surface area typically demonstrate excellent EDLC capacitance. Furthermore, the out-of-layer spacing of BP, measured at 5.3 Å, indicates weaker van der Waals spacing compared to graphene's 3.6 Å, thereby facilitating ion intercalation between the sheets[148]. In their recent work, Yang et al. [149] have presented a scalable fabrication method for producing free-standing black phosphorus (BP) thin films shown in Fig. 7a . These films are derived from high-quality few-layer BP nanoflakes using a modified electrochemical exfoliation process shown in Fig. 7b . The fabricated BP thin films are utilized for flexible quasi-solid-state micro-supercapacitors (QMSCs). The resulting QMSCs exhibit excellent and stable electrochemical performance, even at high scan rates of up to 100 V s−1. Notably, the QMSC devices demonstrate outstanding energy density, reaching 3.63 mWh cm−3, along with remarkable power density of 10.1 W cm−3. Moreover, they exhibit superior cycle stability, with a capacity retention of 94.3 % even after 50,000 cycles. Additionally, the devices demonstrate excellent mechanical flexibility, retaining 91.3 % of their capacity after 500 mechanical bending cycles. Yadav et al. [150] introduced a novel V2CTx MXene-Red/Black phosphorus nanohybrid (MXRBP) as an electroactive material tailored for supercapacitors. The research showcased the design and synthesis of a V2CTx MXene-RBP composite through a straightforward and sustainable sono-chemical method. The resulting composite exhibited a three-dimensional (3D) network structure characterized by high porosity, facilitating rapid transportation and adsorption of electrolyte ions. The integration of RBP within the MXene nanosheets effectively prevented their restacking, creating channels between the nanosheets for enhanced transportation and diffusion of electrolyte ions. The hybrid electrode comprised of V2CTx MXene and RBP (Fig. 7c) demonstrates improved active sites due to the intercalation of RBP between MXene nanosheets, which reduces restacking. This enhancement is evident in the maximum electrochemical surface area (ECSA) exhibited by MXRBP (2125 m2 g−1) compared to RBP and MXene alone, indicating better utilization of the interlayer distance in MXRBP. The value of charge stored at the electrode's outer surface (qc) can be determined from the y-intercept of qt vs v1/2 plots (Fig. 6c). The contribution of charge stored at the inner (qd) and outer surface (qc) of the electrode at different scan rates. Processes governed by diffusion-limited behavior are characterized by b values of 0.5, while surface-controlled capacitive behavior is indicated by b values of 1.0. The inset of Fig. 7d presents the b values at different potentials for the MXRBP electrode. In performance evaluation, the MXRBP composite electrode demonstrated a maximum capacitance of 738 F g−1 at 2 A g−1, representing a ∼1.6-fold increase compared to V2CTx MXene and ∼9 times higher than that of the RBP hybrid alone. Furthermore, the fabricated device exhibited exceptional cycle stability, retaining approximately 86 % of its capacitance after 5000 GCD cycles. The symmetric cell configuration yielded an energy density of 55 Wh kg−1 at 452 W kg−1. Overall, the study presents a promising strategy for introducing desired levels of porosity in V2CTx MXene while preserving its structural flexibility through the incorporation of RBP between MXene layers. Wu et al. [151] developed a chemically bridged hetero-structured BP-CNT material. This material was then utilized to fabricate a flexible supercapacitor (SC) device capable of withstanding bending, rotating, twisting, and folding, using a micro-fluidic spinning technique (MST). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis revealed a sheet/wire-like structure of the BP-CNT material. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) indicated lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.26 nm corresponding to the (040) lattice plane of BP crystal and a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm resembling the (002) plane of CNT. The material exhibited an average pore size of about 6.28 nm, with a sharp peak at 0.26 nm, and pore size distribution ranging from 2.4 nm to 190.5 nm. The electrochemical performance of the fabricated SC was impressive, including a high specific volumetric capacitance of 308.7 F cm−3, an energy density of 96.5 mWh cm−3, and remarkable cyclic stability of 90.2 % over 10,000 cycles. Moreover, the device demonstrated long-term bending durability, with cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) performances remaining consistent under different bending conditions.

The research group demonstrated the practical applications of the fabricated device by lighting LEDs under various bending angles, powering a watch, and displaying functions. Overall, the combination of tunable band gap, high charge carrier mobility, large surface area, flexibility, and chemical stability makes black phosphorus a highly attractive material for flexible energy storage applications, paving the way for the development of advanced and efficient flexible electronics. Research on the safety and environmental impact of using black phosphorus (BP) as an electrode material for energy storage applications is still in its early stages. Similarly, the exfoliation and utilization of 2D phosphorene nanosheets for energy storage applications are also in the initial phases of development. However, we anticipate that phosphorene-based materials will undergo significant advancements in the near future. These advancements are expected to contribute to the development of reliable, eco-friendly, and high-performance energy storage devices

The concept of quantum dots (QDs) or artificial atoms originated from efforts to reduce the dimensions of semiconductors[153]. Nanocrystals of semiconductors exhibit quantum size effects on their electronic and optical properties[154] . Through photochemical methods, stable core-shell structures of QDs can be synthesized, showcasing effective photoluminescence with emissions within a narrow range[65]. Beyond compounds in Groups IV and I-III-VI, the concept of QDs has been extended to perovskites, carbon-based materials, and transition metal dichalcogenides. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) refer to nanomaterials composed of graphene nanoplatelets or other small carbon aggregates [155]. CDs are characterized by water solubility, biocompatibility, and stability, along with notable features such as ease of functionalization, strong fluorescence, and low toxicity [156]. They exhibit exceptional photoluminescence properties, which can be tuned by adjusting size, synthesis methods, and the introduction of various functional groups at the edges of graphite sheets [157]. Graphene nanostructures with zero dimensions, consisting of single or multilayer graphene sheets, are known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [64]. GQDs possess quantum dot-like properties within a nanometer scale, featuring abundant active sites, a large specific surface area, and edges that offer accessible interfaces for ion adsorption and desorption. These unique characteristics make GQDs suitable for applications in fluorescence sensing and bioimaging, owing to their good chemical stability, low toxicity, high quantum confinement effects, and superior photoluminescence [158].Due to their copious active sites, large surface areas, and excellent electrical properties, graphene sheets incorporated with GQDs are ideal materials for supercapacitor applications. Moreover, the utilization of GQDs can significantly enhance the capacitive performance by improving the interface between electrolytes and electrodes. Fu et al [159]. demonstrated the fabrication and integration of flexible planar supercapacitors based on GO-BPQD composites using the direct laser writing (DLW) method. The combination of graphene, known for its high conductivity, with BPQDs, which offer abundant active sites, proves beneficial for enhancing device performance. The resulting supercapacitors, utilizing these nanocomposites, exhibit significantly improved specific capacity and superior rate performance compared to those based solely on reduced graphene oxide (RGO), highlighting the contribution of BPQDs. Moreover, the fabricated device exhibits outstanding flexibility, with no noticeable performance degradation observed even after bending for 1000 cycles. Additionally, the DLW technology enables seamless integration of such supercapacitors. The integrated device demonstrates reasonable performance uniformity with a voltage extension of 3 V, making it capable of easily powering a LED. Recently, Bian et al. [160] fabricated flexible pseudocapacitive micro-supercapacitors (PMSCs) with interdigital electrodes and a gap width of 6 µm/2 µm using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NO-GQDs) via photolithography. The electrochemical performance of these flexible PMSCs was comprehensively evaluated under bending angles of 75° and 0° at scan rates ranging from 0.01 to 10,000 V/s. This assessment included cyclic voltammetry (CV), area capacitance analysis, discharge current measurement, energy density calculation, power density estimation, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic stability examination, and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) testing. The results demonstrated that the prepared flexible PMSCs, based on a polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate, exhibited excellent bending ability and superior performance. Huijie Zhou and colleagues[161] explored how integrating 0D Co3O4 quantum dots (QDs) with pseudocapacitive properties can enhance the electrochemical performance of electrodes. By embedding these QDs into interconnected porous structures without altering the ink's rheological properties, they effectively mitigated surface-related issues. Their study delved into the influence of Co3O4 QDs and carbon dots (CDs) on the ink's liquid-crystal properties, electrode structure, rheology, and electrochemical behavior. By adjusting the intercalation amount of Co3O4 QDs, they fine-tuned the pore structure of the layered electrode, optimizing surface pores and oxidation sites to enhance electrochemical performance. Compared to other 0D non-metallic oxide carbon electrodes, the Co3O4 QD-infused layered structure demonstrated superior electrochemical activity, thanks to enhanced capacitance. A high-resolution interdigital electrode is fabricated through extrusion 3D printing, utilizing a pseudo plastic nanocomposite ink comprising graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), vanadium pentoxide nanowires (V2O5 NWs), and Co3O4 quantum dots (QDs) ( Fig. 8
a). The electrode's inner layer is crafted using ink with liquid-crystal properties. By varying the amount of Co3O4 QDs, the pore structure and active oxidation sites of the layered electrode are tailored. For the micro-supercapacitor (MSC) cathode material, V2O5 NWs and Co3O4 QDs serve as active components, while CNTs and GO act as conductive fillers. Additionally, MXene is employed to construct a counter electrode for an asymmetric MSC configuration. This MSC architecture exhibits an area specific capacitance of 306.13 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.24 mA cm−2 and an energy density of 34.44 µWh cm−2 at a power density of 0.108 mW cm−2. To further validate the efficacy of Co3O4 QDs in enhancing electrochemical performance, we introduce carbon dots (CDs) into the material and conduct a comprehensive characterization. This optimized structural design paves the way for the development of printed electrode materials and configurations, offering novel insights for high-performance microelectrode electrochemical energy storage devices. Moreover, precise control over Co3O4 QD insertion and layer density improved conductivity in micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) by facilitating charge increase and ion transport between electrodes. Furthermore, QD intercalation allowed for pore structure adjustment, mitigating adverse surface effects on MSC capacitance and energy density, thus boosting electrochemical performance.

This enhanced performance was attributed to the advantages offered by NO-GQDs, the narrow electrode/gap width, and the effective and practical film processing technology employed. Overall, this work serves as a significant reference for advancing research on high-performance flexible PMSCs. In their study, Zhang et al. [162] employed a novel top-down strategy to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through sonication crush and strong acid cutting, effectively breaking the CC bonds of graphene oxide (GO). The resulting GQDs, with a uniform nanoscale size of <5 nm, effectively prevent the restacking of graphene nanosheets. Moreover, the presence of abundant edges and defects serves as active sites, promoting ion diffusion within the material. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was utilized to monitor the stage of reaction for GO shearing. Fig. 8 b illustrates the degree of exfoliation and size of the GO sheets, and confirms that the average thickness of GO particles after ultrasonication is approximately 1.5 nm, indicating the existence of single-layer graphene sheets. Further analyses of GQDs are presented in Fig. 8 b, revealing that 88.8 % of GQDs have dimensions between 1 and 3 nm, demonstrating a uniform size distribution. Two typical Nyquist plots (Fig. 8 b), depicting GQD and rGO electrode samples exhibit a semicircle at high frequency, a short 45° district at intermediate frequency, and a straight line at low frequency. The phase angle response to frequency for GQDs and rGO can be observed in the Bode plots. Additionally, a series of electrochemical tests highlight the remarkable properties of GQDs as an electrode material. They exhibit a specific capacitance value of 307.6 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, a high energy density of 41.2 Wh kg−1, and excellent capacitance retention after 5000 cycles. These results confirm the ideal electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) nature of GQDs and underscore their potential applications in energy storage devices. Liu et al. [163] reported promising results in their study, where they developed supercapacitors (MSCs) based on defect-rich nitrogen-doped Ti3C2Tx quantum dots (QDs). These MSCs exhibited excellent electrochemical performance, including a large operating voltage (3.0 V in ionic liquid and 1.0 V in aqueous electrolyte), perfect rectangular cyclic voltammetry (CV) shape even at 1000 V⋅s−1, high volumetric capacitance of 33.1 F⋅cm−3, and superior cycling stability after 10,000 cycles. Through experimental characterizations and density functional theory calculations, the exceptional performance of the MSCs was attributed to the unique chemical states, surface features, and structural characteristics of Ti3C2Tx QDs. These features provide abundant active sites, shorten ion diffusion pathways, promote ion/electron transports, and enhance capacitance.

The Ti3C2Tx QDs solution exhibited a faint yellow color and displayed blue luminescence under UV light, indicative of their surface functional groups and quantum size effects (Fig. 8 c). Compared with Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, the Ti3C2Tx QDs showed the presence of Ti, C, N, and O elements, with Ti 2p spectrum well-fitted with six peaks. The absorption edge energy of Ti3C2Tx QDs, as shown by Ti K-edge XANES curves, indicated partial oxidation, likely occurring at their surface and edges with terminal groups. The Ti3C2Tx QDs-based MSCs demonstrated potential as energy power sources, with the output voltage doubling when two MSCs were connected in series, and the current nearly doubling when connected in parallel (Fig. 8 d). This work presents a novel strategy for designing high-performance MSCs and provides valuable insights for utilizing MXene QDs in other energy-related applications. The quantum capacitance effect of quantum dots (QDs) facilitates the storage of additional charges, leveraging large energy density bands. Further research is necessary to deepen our understanding of quantum capacitance and optimize material design for the development of high-performance QD-based supercapacitors. Additionally, there is a need for sustainable fabrication processes to ensure the eco-friendliness of flexible supercapacitors. The precise control of parameters such as thickness, chemical functionalization, and sizes of quantum dots (QDs) remains an area with limited research. Furthermore, the choice of precursors plays a pivotal role in fine-tuning QD properties, necessitating the development of advanced synthetic approaches. There is a pressing need for standardized synthetic methods capable of producing electrochemically and chemically stable QDs in high yields. Such methods would enable consistent and reliable production of QDs for a wide range of applications.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), also known as porous coordination polymers (PCPs), represent a class of hybrid materials combining inorganic and organic components with high porosity[164]. Originating from the pioneering work of Yaghi and colleagues in 1995 [165], MOFs consist of metal ions coordinated with organic linkers, forming a porous network. These structures incorporate heteroatoms like phosphorus and nitrogen, alongside carbon-rich organic ligands, offering tunability in dimensions ranging from 1D to 3D[166]. The versatility of MOFs lies in their ability to adjust pore size, surface area, and functional groups, garnering attention across diverse fields such as chemical sensing, catalysis, batteries, and supercapacitors. In the realm of supercapacitors, MOFs find application in three primary categories. Firstly, pristine MOFs store energy through physisorption of electrolyte ions onto their surfaces or via reversible redox reactions at metal centers. Secondly, MOF-derived metal oxide-based electrodes facilitate energy storage via charge transfer between electrolyte ions and the electrodes [167]. However, both pristine MOF electrodes and MOF-derived metal oxides suffer from poor electronic conductivity, which can be addressed by integrating electrode-active materials to enhance overall conductivity[168]. These hybrid electrodes, incorporating MOF composites, exhibit improved performance due to synergistic effects, offering enhanced physical and chemical properties. The third category involves the utilization of MOF-derived porous carbons, obtained through the pyrolysis of MOF templates [169]. These carbons possess high electronic conductivity, significant surface area, and large pore volumes, rendering them ideal candidates for electrical double-layer capacitor (EDLC) charge storage [170]. Li and colleagues[171] introduced a straightforward and reliable method for fabricating micro-supercapacitors utilizing nanoporous carbon electrodes generated via a solvent-free MOF-CVD (Metal-Organic Framework Chemical Vapor Deposition) approach. They found that electrodeposition is an effective method for producing the initial ZnO seed layer, as it is cost-effective and yields porous films capable of accommodating significant volume expansion during MOF-CVD, thus reducing delamination from the current collector. They observed that the morphology and elemental composition of the ZIF-8 (Zeolitic Imidazolate Framework-8) films are influenced by the thickness of the electrodeposited ZnO, and successful carbonization of the ZIF-8 films depends on factors such as the flow velocity of the N2 protecting atmosphere and temperature ramping. Their micro-supercapacitors, featuring interdigitated electrodes made of carbon materials, exhibited excellent capacitance retention, as well as high power and energy density, making them promising candidates for potential power sources in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) applications. Notably, when the micro-supercapacitor was patterned with 16 interdigitated electrodes, it demonstrated an ultrahigh stack power of 232.8 W/cm3 at a scan rate of 1000 V/s, while maintaining stable stack energy. The authors suggest that advancements in reticular chemistry may lead to the discovery of MOFs that outperform ZIF-8 as precursors, resulting in nanoporous carbon electrodes with even higher porosities and conductivities. Consequently, the simplicity and cost-effectiveness of the material processing steps outlined in their study could facilitate the development of next-generation miniaturized power supplies for MEMS. Recently, Wang et al. [172] achieved efficient synthesis and delamination of phthalocyanine-based 2D conjugated metal-organic frameworks (2D c-MOFs), specifically Ni2[CuPc(NH)8], into ultrathin nanosheets through top-down ball milling exfoliation. This breakthrough addresses the challenge of developing flexible thin-film electrodes from 2D c-MOFs, which were previously limited due to rigid powder samples produced by solvothermal synthesis. The bulk Ni2[CuPc(NH)8] was synthesized by coordinating 2,3,9,10,16,17,23,24-octaaminophthalocyaninato copper [II] (OAPcCu) ligand (Fig. 9a ) with Ni(NO3)2⋅6H2O in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at 60 °C in the presence of NH3⋅H2O. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) of the prepared bulk Ni2[CuPc(NH)8] revealed peaks at 2θ = 4.91°, 7.01°, 9.89°, 14.81°, 15.63°, 19.80°, 22.17°, and 27.51°, corresponding to (100), (110), (200), (210), (300), (310), (400), (420), and (002) lattice planes, respectively. These peaks signify long-range order within the ab-plane, with a center-to-center distance between neighboring Cu⋯Cu of 18.0 Å . Moreover, structural modeling using density functional theory (DFT) methods explored various interlayer arrangements (Fig. 9a) Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed stacked sheet-like morphologies, with sheet sizes estimated at 100–300 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging displayed layer-stacked crystallites, while XRD patterns confirmed the integrity of the crystalline structure even after 24 h of immersion at room temperature (Fig. 9a). Ni2[CuPc(NH)8] nanosheets were combined with electrochemically exfoliated graphene (EG) nanosheets, serving as a conductive platform, in different mass ratios. The mixture was then filtered through a homemade mask to fabricate interdigital electrodes for flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) (Fig.9b). Comparative analysis revealed that the integral area of the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve for the Ni2[CuPc(NH)8]/EG-2-based MSC (referred to as Ni2[CuPc(NH)8]EG-2) reached the maximum value compared to other fabricated devices. CV profiles of Ni2[CuPc(NH)8]/EG-2 exhibited a nearly rectangular shape across scan rates ranging from 2 to 50 mV s−1 (Fig. 9b), attributed to the high specific surface area and well-defined structure of Ni2[CuPc(NH)8]. Additionally, the capacitive behavior of Ni2[CuPc(NH)8]/EG-2 was investigated through galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements at various current densities ranging from 0.04 to 0.4 mA cm−2. The resulting ultrathin Ni2[CuPc(NH)8] nanosheets exhibit intrinsic conductivity, porosity, high crystallinity, and excellent chemical stability. In their study, the Ni2[CuPc(NH)8] 2D c-MOF-based micro-supercapacitor (MSC) device demonstrated remarkable mechanical flexibility, cycling stability, and a high areal capacitance of 18.9 mF cm−2, showcasing its potential for energy storage applications. This work not only provides a guideline for the top-down exfoliation of 2D c-MOF nanosheets but also highlights their functionality in flexible MSCs. Furthermore, the authors suggest that by further modifying the chemical structures to incorporate redox-active sites, improving crystallinity, porosity, and conductivity, 2D c-MOFs hold significant promise for the development of flexible electronic devices. Naderi et al [144]. designed and synthesized 2D-3D S-doped CoZnNi-OH/CuCoP nanosheet arrays on Cu wire using bubble hydrogen and MOF templates. They utilized CuCoP foam with a 3D nano-dendritic architecture as a porous substrate to support nanostructured materials on the surface of 1D metal fibers. Compared to CoZnNiS co-electrodeposited on 3D CuCoP foam, the MOF-derived sulfide nanoarrays exhibited significantly higher specific capacitance, approximately 18 times greater (2.9 F cm−2, 290.07 F cm−3, 348 mF cm−1 at 4 mA cm−2), and outstanding rate capability. The rational morphology of the MOF-assisted interconnected nanosheet arrays, combined with the synergy between ternary metal sulfide and binary metal phosphide, played a crucial role in enhancing the capacitive performance. The symmetrical GCD curves highlight the prominent coulombic efficiency and high reversibility of the as-made micro-device. Fig. 9c presents the volumetric and areal capacitances of the S-doped CoZnNi-OH/CuCoP@CW//V2O5-PPy/rGO-NC@CF as a function of current density. To evaluate the practical applicability of the assembled fiber-shaped micro-supercapacitor (micro-SC), energy density and power density were calculated and depicted in the Ragone plot based on their geometric parameters (Fig. 9c). Furthermore, cyclic stability was assessed for the S-doped CoZnNi-OH/CuCoP@CW//V2O5-PPy/rGO-NC@CF over consecutive 5000 GCD cycles at a current density of 9 mA cm−2. To demonstrate the flexibility of the micro-SC, CV curves were measured at various bending angles (0°, 60°, 90°, 120°, and 180°). Additionally, after charging, the fabricated micro-device was connected to a red and green light-emitting diode (LED) for 5 min (Fig. 9d), showcasing the practical applicability of S-doped CoZnNi-OH/CuCoP/CW//V2O5-PPy/rGO-NC@CF as an efficient energy storage micro-device.

Furthermore, they synthesized a negative microelectrode of V2O5-PPy growth on rGO-NC@CF using an electrodeposition method to construct a wire-like micro-supercapacitor (micro-SC). The V2O5-PPy/rGO-NC electrode exhibited a high areal capacitance of 1279.24 mF cm−2 along with excellent cycle stability of 97.15 % after 5000 cycles and good rate capability. Combining the high specific capacitance and large working potential window of the positive and negative microelectrodes, the fabricated solid-stated flexible fibrous micro-SC demonstrated a high areal energy density of 127.62 µWh cm−2 at a power density of 1.83 mW cm−2, with a capacitance retention of 93.7 % after 5000 cycles and high mechanical stability. These results underscore the excellent potential of the fabricated micro-device for applications in next-generation energy supply microelectronics. Hence, the unique combination of properties offered by MOFs makes them promising candidates for flexible micro-supercapacitors, offering opportunities for the development of efficient and versatile energy storage devices for future wearable and portable electronics.

Composite hybrids for flexible micro-supercapacitors represent an innovative approach in the development of energy storage devices that seamlessly integrate with flexible and wearable electronics[174]. These composites leverage the unique properties of multiple materials to overcome individual limitations and achieve superior performance in terms of energy storage capacity, flexibility, and durability. One prevalent class of composite hybrids involves the combination of carbon-based materials with various functional components [175]. Carbon-based materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and activated carbon offer high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and mechanical flexibility. By incorporating these carbon materials into flexible polymers such as polyurethane or polyimide, composite electrodes with enhanced structural integrity and electrochemical performance are realized[176]. These composites provide a stable and conductive framework for efficient charge transfer and ion diffusion, thereby maximizing the capacitance and energy density of the FMSCs. Another promising approach is the integration of metal oxides into carbon-based composites. Metal oxides, such as manganese dioxide (MnO2), ruthenium oxide (RuO2), and nickel oxide (NiO), exhibit high pseudocapacitance, making them ideal candidates for increasing the overall capacitance of micro-supercapacitor electrodes [[177]. By combining metal oxides with carbon materials, synergistic effects are achieved, resulting in electrodes with improved specific capacitance, rate capability, and cyclic stability. Additionally, metal oxide/carbon composites offer enhanced mechanical flexibility, crucial for applications in flexible electronics. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have also emerged as promising components in composite hybrids for FMSCs. MOFs possess high porosity, tunable functionality, and large surface area, making them attractive for enhancing the electrochemical performance of electrodes. When incorporated into carbon-based composites, MOFs provide additional active sites for charge storage and facilitate ion diffusion, resulting in electrodes with superior capacitance and energy storage capability [178]. Moreover, MOF/carbon composites offer excellent flexibility, enabling the fabrication of FMSCs that can conform to the contours of wearable devices. Conductive polymers, such as polyaniline (PANI) and polypyrrole (PPy), represent another class of materials commonly used in composite hybrids for FMSCs[68]. These polymers offer high specific capacitance and facile synthesis, making them suitable for integration with carbon-based materials to enhance electrical conductivity and electrochemical performance [179]. Composite electrodes based on conductive polymer/carbon hybrids exhibit excellent flexibility, mechanical robustness, and electrochemical stability, enabling the development of FMSCs for diverse applications in wearable and portable electronics[180]. In their study, Cho et al [181]. demonstrated the fabrication of all-solid-state flexible high-energy-density micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) using 3D printing technology to create porous carbon-based composite electrodes integrated with a solid-state ionogel electrolyte. The composite electrodes were engineered to possess large mesopores, facilitating efficient ion diffusion and enhancing the supercapacitive energy storage per unit area. By vertically integrating the 3D-printed composite electrode within a confined space, the active material volume was increased, further boosting the device's energy storage capacity. The fabricated 3D-printed MSCs exhibited an impressive specific capacitance of 110.4 mF cm−2 over an operating voltage of 2 V, translating to a remarkably high energy density of 60.6 μWh cm−2. Furthermore, the MSCs demonstrated excellent power density, reaching 0.89 mW cm−2, while maintaining an energy density of 31.5 μWh cm−2. The capacitive retention remained high at 96.2 % even after 2000 charge-discharge cycles, indicating excellent cycling stability. Moreover, the devices exhibited outstanding mechanical stability, enduring 1000 successive 90° bending stresses without performance degradation. Liu and colleagues [182] developed uniform MXene/Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) composites through a coprecipitation reaction. This process involved metal ions cross-linking with the surface termination groups of Ti3C2TX nanosheets, disrupting the electrostatic repulsion between the nanosheets. Subsequently, the metal ions served as nucleation sites for the in-situ growth of MOF particles on the Ti3C2TX nanosheets. In MXene/MOF systems, three-dimensional (3D) MOFs played a crucial role in preventing the aggregation of MXene nanosheets and facilitating the transport of electrolytes. Meanwhile, MXene nanosheets enhanced conductivity and contributed to pseudocapacitive behavior. Moreover, an "ion-conversion-exchange" strategy was employed to fabricate hollow Ti3C2TX/ZIF-67/CoV2O6 composites shown in Fig. 10a . This strategy addressed the poor conductivity of MOFs and further improved electrolyte transport. The electron/electrolyte transport mechanism as shown in Fig. 10b. By compensating for these limitations, the hollow composite structures enhanced the overall performance of the material system. Wang et al [183]. developed a flexible CN-MX hybrid film with a well-structured framework through a two-step strategy involving facile hydrothermal synthesis followed by filtration. In this process, rhombic g-C3N4 nanoparticles were intentionally incorporated to adjust the layered-MXene skeleton, creating a frame-structured construction. This design facilitated the establishment of a conductive network within the MXene interspace, promoting efficient charge transfer and ion diffusion.

Fig. 10c illustrates the schematic diagram depicting the mask-assisted preparation process of flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), digital images showcase the interdigitated symmetrical electrodes and the assembled flexible MSCs. Furthermore, top-view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images, reveal that the surface of the CN-MX electrode appears relatively flat with minimal fluctuation. The interface between the CN-MX electrode and the PET substrate is discernible in the cross-section SEM image presented in Fig. 10c presents typical cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves obtained at a potential window of 0–0.5 V. Even at a high scan rate of 200 mVs−1, the CV curves maintain their highly symmetrical shape, indicating excellent reversibility and high coulombic efficiency. The symmetric flexible micro-supercapacitor (MSC) device demonstrates a high capacitance of 2.63.2 mFcm−2 at 0.3 mA cm−2. The electrochemical performance of the flexible MSC device at various bending angles is illustrated in Fig. 10d. Remarkably, the flexible MSC device exhibits superior cyclic stability, with 90.4 % retention of capacitance after 700 cycles of alternating 90° bending and releasing states. The resulting CN-MX hybrid film electrode demonstrated remarkable electrochemical performance, with an ultra-high area capacitance of 1932.8 mF cm−2 (equivalent to 386.5 F g−1) at 2 mA cm−2.

Additionally, it exhibited excellent rate capability, retaining 79.4 % of its capacitance even at a high current density of 40 mA cm−2. Moreover, symmetrical micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) assembled using the CN-MX electrode displayed comparable area energy-power density and outstanding cyclic stability, highlighting the potential of the hybrid film for practical energy storage applications. Recently, Shekar et al [184]. designed and fabricated a composite electrode consisting of MoS2 and graphene. Remarkably, at a discharge density of 5 A g−1, the MoS2/graphene (1:9) composite electrode exhibited an exceptional specific capacitance of 248 F g−1, with a capacitance retention of 97.3 % after 8000 cycles. Furthermore, when incorporated into an all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor, the MoS2/graphene (1:9) composite electrode demonstrated excellent cycle performance, retaining 93.0 % of its capacitance after 8000 cycles. This indicates promising prospects for utilizing such composite electrodes in the development of advanced energy storage solutions. Additionally, using too many electrode materials necessitates additional processing stages, increasing complexity and total manufacturing costs. The relative mass ratio of various components is crucial for achieving optimal electrochemical performance and mechanical flexibility[185,186] as the relative mass ratio of each precursor determines the porosity, electrical conductivity, mechanical characteristics, and other final electrode physicochemical properties. Binders and conducting agents are often used in conjunction with active materials to create the electrode. However, their addition can lead to a smaller electroactive surface area and fewer ionic transports, potentially reducing capacitance performance while increasing device costs.

Exploring novel electrode materials for flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) involves delving into alternatives that offer significant improvements in performance, flexibility, and stability beyond traditional choices. Here are some promising materials:

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) is a significant member of the transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) family. Structurally, MoS₂ is composed of molybdenum atoms that are sandwiched between layers of sulfur atoms, forming a hexagonal lattice. This layered structure allows MoS₂ to be exfoliated into monolayers, which greatly increases its surface area—a critical factor for effective charge storage in energy devices. The material's excellent electrical conductivity further supports high power density and rapid charge-discharge cycles, making it ideal for energy storage applications. Moreover, the layered nature of MoS₂ imparts mechanical flexibility, making it suitable for devices that require bending or stretching, such as flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs). MoS₂ is also known for its pseudocapacitive behavior, which arises from the rich redox chemistry of its layers. This pseudocapacitive behavior enhances the energy density of FMSCs, making MoS₂ an attractive material for high-performance energy storage devices.

However, there are challenges that need to be addressed before the full potential of MoS₂ can be realized. These include the scalability of monolayer production and ensuring long-term stability under varying environmental conditions. Recent studies have explored innovative approaches to harness MoS₂'s potential. For instance, Bin Li et. al[187] fabricated integrated inkjet-printed electrodes based on MoS2 nanosheets have been demonstrated for low-cost and planar MSCs with high capacitance via a simple desktop inkjetprinter. These planar MSCs not only possess any collapsible shape through the computer design but also exhibit excellent electrochemical performance. Another study by S. S. Nardekar and team[188] involved the preparation of MoS₂ quantum sheets (QSs) using a salt-assisted ball milling method, marking the first use of MoS₂ QSs as supercapacitor electrodes. The MoS₂ QSs exhibited superior capacitive properties compared to the bulk material due to several factors: (i) reduced lateral size, (ii) fewer stacking layers, (iii) increased ion-transport channels (quantum channels), and (iv) the presence of the metallic 1T phase in the MoS₂ QSs. An asymmetric supercapacitor fabricated using MoS₂ QSs electrodes showed an impressive energy density of 14.4 Wh kg⁻¹, surpassing the performance of symmetric supercapacitors using MoS₂. Additionally, Jiang Zhao et. al[189] engineered MoS₂ into laser-induced graphene to fabricate a flexible microsupercapacitor, demonstrating the versatility and potential of MoS₂ in advanced energy storage applications. Two-dimensional tellurites represent another exciting class of materials. These materials are based on tellurium and oxygen and can be structured into nanosheets or other two-dimensional forms. Tellurites offer high theoretical specific capacities because they can undergo multiple redox reactions, which can significantly enhance the energy storage capabilities of FMSCs. The layered structure of tellurites provides a high surface area for charge storage, and their electrochemical stability is generally good, which is essential for maintaining consistent performance over many charge-discharge cycles[190] . Nonetheless, challenges related to the synthesis of high-quality two-dimensional tellurites and ensuring their compatibility with other components in FMSCs must be addressed. Scaling up production while maintaining material quality and optimizing material interfaces are critical for practical applications. Nickel-cobalt sulfide (NCS) is another promising material. NCS combines nickel and cobalt sulfides, offering enhanced pseudocapacitance and stability. These materials exhibit high specific capacitance and good cycling stability due to their high conductivity and redox activity[191]. The combination of nickel and cobalt in the sulfide form provides synergistic effects that improve the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitor. However, the complexity of synthesizing NCS materials and optimizing their performance for various electrolytes and operating conditions poses significant challenges. Vanadium oxide (VO₂) is a transition metal oxide with unique properties that include variable oxidation states and good electrical conductivity. VO₂ can undergo reversible redox reactions, which contributes to its high pseudocapacitance and makes it suitable for energy storage applications. Its ability to provide high energy storage capacity is balanced by challenges such as poor cycle life and limited flexibility compared to other materials. To address these issues, careful material engineering and optimization are required.

Silicene, a 2D allotrope of silicon, also shows promise as an electrode material. Like graphene, silicene has a high surface area and flexibility, coupled with good electronic conductivity[192]. This makes it an attractive candidate for flexible electronics. However, silicene is less mature compared to graphene, and its production and integration into flexible devices are still under development. Tellurene, another 2D material based on tellurium, shares similarities with graphene but offers unique electronic properties and high electrical conductivity. Its layered structure supports high charge storage capacities, and it demonstrates potential for flexibility and mechanical stability. The main challenges include the synthesis and scalability of tellurene, as well as validating its long-term stability and performance in flexible applications. Therefore, materials such as MoS₂, two-dimensional tellurites, NCS, VO₂, silicene, and tellurene offer novel approaches for enhancing the performance of flexible microsupercapacitors. Each of these materials brings unique properties that can potentially address the limitations of traditional electrode materials. Advances in synthesis techniques, material integration, and long-term stability will be crucial in unlocking their full potential for next-generation flexible energy storage devices.

Electrolytes play a crucial role in the performance of supercapacitors (SCs), influencing capacitance, power density, rate performance, cyclability, and safety. The physical and chemical properties of electrolytes are as vital as the choice of electrode materials for optimal SC functionality[193]. Achieving an optimal electrolyte concentration is a constant pursuit, as elevated concentrations facilitate ion transport within the electrode layers, enhancing the double-layer build-up. However, excessively high concentrations can diminish ion activity due to reduced water hydration, leading to decreased ion mobility. An ideal electrolyte exhibits a broad voltage range, excellent electrochemical stability, high ionic concentration and conductivity, low viscosity, and low toxicity[194]. Consideration of key electrolyte parameters is essential in proper cell design, including sufficiently high ion conductivity, electrochemical stability on the anode and cathode surfaces, good wetting in contact with electrode materials, suitable thermal properties, cost-effectiveness, and adequate mechanical properties. There are three primary types of electrolytes: aqueous, organic liquid, and ionic liquid (IL) and Polymer electrolytes. Among all the electrolytes, Polymer-based electrolytes have garnered significant interest in FMSC manufacturing due to their safer and more manageable construction, greater design flexibility, a broader operational temperature range, and enhanced electrochemical stability [195–197]. Consequently, polymer electrolytes are considered optimal candidates for replacing liquid electrolytes in advanced energy-storage devices (ESDs), given their wide electrochemical window, robust thermal stability, and reduced risk of electrolyte solution leakage. Polymer electrolytes typically comprise a polymer matrix, a lithium salt or other ionic compound, and sometimes additives to enhance conductivity or mechanical properties[198]. The polymer matrix can be selected from a wide range of materials, including polyethylene oxide (PEO), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), among others. Lithium salts commonly used in polymer electrolytes include lithium perchlorate (LiClO4), lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), and lithium trifluoromethanesulfonate (LiCF3SO3), although other salts may also be employed [199]. The ionic conductivity of polymer electrolytes arises from the mobility of ions within the polymer matrix, facilitated by segmental motion of polymer chains and/or ion hopping between polymer chains. Polymer electrolytes are categorized into three main types based on their composition and structure:

A solid polymer electrolyte, also known as a polymer-salt complex electrolyte with ion-conducting properties, is formed by dissolving inorganic salts in a polar functional polymer. This process results in the creation of electrostatic forces through interactions between metal ions and polar groups within the polymers, facilitated by the formation of coordinating bonds[200]. Due to the relatively weak coordination of cations along the polymer chain, the cations in the electrolyte can migrate from one coordinated site to another when an electric field is applied[201].Various polymers, including polycarbonate (PC), poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA), poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO), and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), are utilized to produce dry and solid polymer electrolytes. Metal ions, such as lithium (Li), sodium (Na), and potassium (K), can be dissolved in these polymers through various soluble substances. However, the room ionic conductivities of these solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) typically range from 10−8 to 10−5 S cm−1, falling below the necessary conductivity threshold of 10−3 S cm−1 for practical applications. This limitation restricts their widespread applicability in real-world scenarios [202]. Developing solid-state polymer electrolytes with both excellent mechanical properties and high ionic conductivity remains a significant challenge. Typically, as the ionic conductivity increases, the mechanical properties of polymer electrolytes tend to deteriorate significantly. Therefore, achieving a balance between these conflicting requirements poses a formidable task. Moreover, supercapacitors (SCs) are frequently deployed in demanding environments, including high temperatures. Consequently, a polymer electrolyte designed for SCs must possess attributes such as robust ionic conductivity, elevated thermal stability, and mechanical strength to withstand such conditions effectively. Mao et al [203]. have successfully fabricated a series of solid-state polymer electrolytes using porous polybenzimidazole (pPBI) and functionalized ionic liquid (IL). Among these, the pPBI-IL-15 % composite film stands out, exhibiting high conductivity of 0.103 S cm⁻¹ at 170 °C, coupled with excellent mechanical properties and thermal stability. Notably, the specific capacitance of the associated all-solid-state supercapacitor (ASSC-15) reaches 85.5 Fg⁻¹ at 120 °C, which is three times higher than that at room temperature. Furthermore, the prepared supercapacitors demonstrate stable electrochemical performance even under various bending conditions. Remarkably, even after 10,000 galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) cycles, the all-solid-state supercapacitor retains an impressive capacitance retention rate of 91.0 %, with a high coulomb efficiency of 95.8 %. These findings highlight the potential of the developed porous PBI composite film for high-temperature supercapacitor applications and suggest its feasibility for integration into other energy storage devices. Recently, Liu et al. [204] devised a composite solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) incorporating a reinforced polypropylene (PP) membrane. A novel solid polymer electrolyte composite membrane was created by impregnating PP separators with a PLI(X) electrolyte solution, as illustrated in Fig. 11a . This method differs from the conventional approach of dispersing polymers and inorganic nanoparticles. The PEO matrix within the developed PLI(X)@PP membrane facilitated ion transport within the separator pores, while the PP separators provided structural support. The PLI(70)@PP membrane displayed notable characteristics, including a high ionic conductivity of 2.9 × 10−3 Scm−1 at 25 °C, alongside minimal resistance at the electrolyte-electrode interface and exceptional mechanical strength. Utilizing CE/PLI(70)@PP/CE devices, boasting a specific capacity of 158 Fg−1, they showcased superior energy and power densities, along with consistent cycling performance. Furthermore, the utilization of dry SPEs in flexible micro supercapacitors has led to the creation of devices with impressive performance metrics. These include high specific capacitance, excellent rate capability, and prolonged cycling stability, essential for applications requiring reliable energy storage solutions in compact and flexible form factors. Moving forward, continued research efforts aimed at optimizing SPE fabrication processes, enhancing electrolyte properties, and integrating SPEs with advanced electrode materials will further propel the development of high-performance and durable flexible micro supercapacitors for diverse applications.

In recent times, gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) have emerged as a prominent category of polymer electrolytes suitable for flexible solid-state supercapacitors[205]. GPEs are favored for their versatility, lightweight characteristics, straightforward manufacturing process, strong adhesive properties, and wide operational window. These physical gels, formed through diverse mechanisms such as hydrogen bonds, crystallization, ionic bonds, self-assembly of small molecules, or mechanical dispersion, offer distinct advantages as they are simpler to produce and do not necessitate the use of cross-linking agents. Moreover, this gel materials exhibit reversibility, characterized by transient interactions among polymer chains that can adapt in response to variations in temperature, pH, or solvent composition. Various polymers, including poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), poly(acrylonitrile) (PAN), poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), and poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO), have undergone extensive exploration for their potential in developing high-performance GPEs. Typically, polymer matrices should possess rapid segmental mobility, the capacity to dissociate salt ions, a low glass transition temperature, and demonstrate good electrochemical and thermal stability. However, each of these polymers has inherent limitations that hinder their practical application[206,207]. For example, gel electrolytes based on PEO suffer from limited ionic conductivity due to the high crystallinity of the polymer and restricted motion of the polymer chains. Similarly, PMMA-based gel electrolytes, despite exhibiting good electrochemical stability, experience reduced ionic conductivity. PAN and PVDF matrices, while facilitating the formation of homogeneous gel films, encounter challenges in their ionic transport mechanisms, limiting their effectiveness. PVDF, known for its high dielectric constant, exhibits limited ionic conductivity attributed to its crystalline nature and subpar interfacial chemical stability[207]. Among host polymers, PVA has emerged as a leading candidate for GPEs owing to its biodegradability, biocompatibility, affordability, and non-toxicity. However, there remains a critical need to enhance both the mechanical properties and electrochemical performance of existing solid-state electrolytes. New solid-state electrolytes must simultaneously fulfill multiple requirements, including mechanical strength, excellent ionic conductivity, minimal interfacial resistance, and improved ion transference efficiency. In response to this challenge, Alipoori and colleagues[208] have developed a novel PVA/KCl/H2O gel electrolyte, demonstrating a significant enhancement in ionic conductivity. This particular combination has not been previously explored in this context. Their study focuses on optimizing key properties of these gel electrolytes, including ionic conductivity, film formation, thermal characteristics, and biocompatibility. The findings establish a strong foundation that could potentially be extended to a wide range of polymer systems, thereby laying the groundwork for the next generation of flexible solid-state supercapacitors.

Gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) exhibit superior ionic conductivity at normal temperatures, drawing significant interest from researchers. This heightened attention is a result of GPEs combining the advantageous characteristics of liquid-based electrolytes, such as robust ionic conductivity, with the stability observed in solid-based electrolytes. The unique strength of GPEs lies in the robust ionic conductivity of the liquid phase coupled with the favorable mechanical properties of the solid component, offering a promising synergy. One of the compelling features of GPEs is their enhanced safety profile compared to traditional liquid-based electrolytes GPEs manage to maintain exceptional ionic conductivity on the order of 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature, a factor that significantly contributes to enhancing the overall electrochemical performance of devices incorporating GPEs The combination of these attributes positions GPEs as a preferred choice in the fabrication of modern energy storage devices (ESDs), offering not only heightened safety but also increased flexibility. This advancement marks a crucial step forward in the pursuit of more efficient and secure energy storage solutions. Nguyen et al. [209] have successfully developed a high-conductivity all-solid-state sodium ionic gel polymer using a low-cost sodium salt through a simple process illustrated in Fig. 11b. This advancement holds promise for the future of next-generation energy storage devices. At room temperature, the ionic conductivity of the material reaches 0.54 mS/cm, with a capacitance of 5.7 μF/cm2 and a dielectric constant of 3.2 × 106 F/m. Crucially, the researchers designed an all-solid-state micro-supercapacitor and observed stable electrochemical performance, achieving a high energy density of approximately 0.7 Wh/kg and a high-power density of up to approximately 2500 W/kg. The successful formation of a solid electrolyte interface is attributed to the perfect contact between the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) electrode and the electrolyte, as well as the excellent combination of each material in the electrolyte. These factors contribute to a minimal loss of capacitance, with only a 1.1 % decrease observed after 20,000 cycles of continuous operation at a current density of 50 mA/g within an operating voltage window from 0.0 to 1.0 V, without any observable peaks from chemical reactions. Very recently, Duan et al [210]. have introduced a novel PAM/KC/LiNO3 gel polymer electrolyte (GPE), demonstrating the efficacy of the DN (double-network) structure in enhancing mechanical properties. The combination of the rigid KC network and the soft PAM network results in outstanding tensile, compressive, and self-recovery properties for the GPE. These physical cross-linked networks feature reversible interactions, including KC's thermos-reversible transition and dynamic hydrogen bonds, contributing to remarkable self-healing capabilities. Utilizing the PAM/KC/LiNO3 GPE, the researchers fabricated a self-healing integrated supercapacitor by depositing PANI on both sides of the GPE, ensuring seamless connection between the electrolyte and electrodes. Compared to traditional sandwich-like supercapacitors, this integrated device exhibits superior specific capacitance, impressive energy and power densities, attributed to lower interface resistance. Furthermore, the integrated supercapacitor demonstrates exceptional self-healing performance, even at −10 °C, and remarkable flexibility.

It remains safe under various deformations and external stimuli without compromising electrochemical performance. These findings highlight the potential of DN GPE structures and integrated supercapacitor designs in flexible energy storage devices, offering enhanced mechanical properties, seamless integration, and improved overall performance. the development of GPEs represents a promising direction for the future of energy storage technology. Further research and innovation in GPE design and fabrication processes hold the potential to unlock even greater advancements in flexible and solid-state energy storage devices, paving the way for widespread adoption in diverse applications ranging from wearable electronics to electric vehicles and beyond.

Plasticized polymer electrolytes (PPEs) are crafted from host polymers with lower molecular weights, including poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG), polycarbonate (PC), and ethylene carbonate. The mechanical and thermomechanical properties of these polymers undergo alterations, leading to a reduction in the stiffness of the polymer structure[212]. Simultaneously, the glass transition temperature of the specific polymer electrolyte system is lowered. This transformation is attributed to an increased capability for salt dissociation and a decrease in crystallinity, ultimately resulting in an enhanced conveyance of charge carriers [213,214]. It has been observed that polymer electrolytes exhibit heightened ionic conductivity at higher concentrations of plasticizers, albeit at the expense of mechanical stability. This trade-off highlights the intricate balance between achieving optimal ionic conductivity for efficient charge transport and maintaining the necessary mechanical integrity for practical applications. The concentration of plasticizers becomes a critical factor influencing the dynamic interplay between these two crucial aspects in plasticized polymer electrolytes. Recently, Zou and colleagues [215] have conducted an experimental investigation using advanced Cryo-TEM to uncover the surface chemistry and morphology evolution of Na0 in succinonitrile-plasticized polymer electrolytes. Their findings reveal the presence of whiskery Na0 nucleation/growth accompanied by the formation of a double-layered thick solid electrolyte interface (SEI), which contributes to rapid degradation. Building upon these insights, the researchers have developed a novel polymer electrolyte embedded with fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) to deliberately control SEI formation and regulate Na0 nucleation/growth. The incorporation of additives in the electrolyte facilitates the formation of a thin, amorphous, and fluorine-rich SEI, resulting in compact Na0 deposits resembling domes. Utilizing the FEC-embedded polymer electrolyte, the team successfully demonstrated ultra-stable solid-state sodium metal batteries in both coin cells and pouch cells. These findings provide valuable insights into understanding the failure mechanisms of plasticized polymer electrolytes and pave the way for the development of more stable and efficient energy storage devices. Ghorbanzadeh and colleagues[216] investigated the combination of plasticization with (Li)salt-doping as a method to enhance the ionic conductivity of solid polymer electrolytes (SICs). They found that solely improving polymer flexibility through the addition of amorphous PPO does not significantly increase ionic conductivity, likely due to the reduction in charge carrier concentration. However, this limitation can be effectively addressed by doping with LiTFSI, resulting in flexible ternary electrolytes with increased ionic conductivities. Their approach and choice of materials prove to be a viable strategy for simultaneously enhancing the ionic conductivity and flexibility of the polymer matrix without inducing crystalline phases, as seen in PEO doping. Although the achieved ionic conductivities are lower compared to standard SPEs, they fall within the range of similar SICs. However, it's crucial to consider the complex trade-offs between polymer flexibility, mechanical properties, and Li+ transport. plasticized polymer electrolytes (PPEs) represent a promising avenue for enhancing the performance of various energy storage and conversion devices, particularly in the realm of lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors. By incorporating plasticizers into polymer matrices, PPEs offer improved flexibility, mechanical strength, and ionic conductivity compared to traditional solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs). Additionally, the flexibility imparted by plasticizers enables the electrolyte to conform to irregular electrode surfaces, promoting better electrode-electrolyte contact and improving device efficiency. Moreover, the versatility of plasticized polymer electrolytes allows for tailored design and optimization to meet specific performance requirements, such as high ionic conductivity, wide electrochemical stability window, and excellent mechanical integrity. This flexibility in design enables the development of PPEs suitable for various applications, including flexible and wearable electronics, electric vehicles, and grid-scale energy storage systems. While significant progress has been made in the field of plasticized polymer electrolytes, challenges such as maintaining stability over extended cycling, mitigating plasticizer migration, and optimizing electrolyte-electrode interfaces remain areas of active research. Overcoming these challenges will be essential for realizing the full potential of PPEs in next-generation energy storage and conversion technologies. Nonetheless, the continued exploration and refinement of plasticized polymer electrolytes hold great promise for advancing the performance and safety of energy storage devices in the pursuit of a more sustainable and efficient energy future.

Composite polymer electrolytes (CPEs) have emerged as promising candidates for flexible micro-supercapacitors, offering a combination of mechanical flexibility and high ionic conductivity. By incorporating various types of fillers or additives into polymer matrices, CPEs can achieve enhanced electrochemical performance suitable for micro-supercapacitor applications. The incorporation of inorganic fillers into polymer electrolytes serves to enhance mechanical strength and interfacial stability, giving rise to a distinct category known as composite polymer electrolytes (CPEs)[217]. This addition of high dielectric constant fillers, particularly inorganic inert fillers, into the polymer matrix offers an avenue to augment the electrical characteristics of polymer electrolytes. Ceramic materials emerge as frequently employed inorganic dopants, chosen for their propensity to break easily and possess low dielectric strength. The amalgamation of these inorganic dopants polymers results in a new composite electrolytic material boasting higher relative permittivity [218].These composite electrolytes, composed of ceramic particles, can be envisioned as heterogeneously disordered systems wherein electrical properties are significantly influenced by the relative permittivity and conductivity of the dopants. Furthermore, the electrical properties of these composite electrolytic materials are intricately shaped by the size, shape, and volume percentage of the incorporated dopants[219–222]. The deliberate manipulation of these factors allows for tailoring the electrical characteristics of composite polymer electrolytes to meet specific performance requirements. Kim et al. [223] developed cross-linked composite gel polymer electrolytes (CGPEs) using an H-shaped PEO-PPO tetra block copolymer precursor with cross-linkable triethoxysilane end groups, SiO2 nanoparticles, and an ionic liquid (IL) EMIM-TFSI. The CGPE formed with 0.1 wt % SiO2 demonstrated balanced electrolyte properties, with high ionic conductivity (2.22 × 10−3 S cm−1) at room temperature and good mechanical stability. Assembled with CGPE-S0.1, the supercapacitor (SC_CGPE-S0.1) exhibited a high specific capacitance (109.24 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1), excellent rate capability (60.49 % capacity retention at 10 A g−1), and good durability (94 % capacitance retention over 10,000 charge/discharge cycles). These findings suggest that SC_CGPE-S0.1 holds promise as a suitable candidate for all solid-state supercapacitor applications. Nitish Yadav et al [224]. successfully developed flexible and durable porous membranes made from PVdF-HFP using a simple, one-step immersion precipitation phase inversion technique, which is environmentally sustainable. They utilized a water: MP (80:20 v/v) solution as the non-solvent, enhancing the process's simplicity and eco-friendliness. The optimized nanocomposite membrane, containing 3 % ZrO2 nanofiller, exhibited remarkable conductivity of 4.4 S cm−1 at room temperature upon activation with NaClO4-EC: PC electrolyte solution. With a porosity of 71.7 % (V/V), this membrane demonstrated outstanding performance. In a symmetric EDLC cell employing commercial activated carbon and the optimized electrolyte, low bulk resistance (3.8 Ω cm2) was observed. This configuration yielded impressive maximum power, effective energy densities of 14.2 kW kg−1, and 10.2 Whkg−1, respectively, along with a capacitance of 84 Fg−1. Notably, the cell exhibited exceptional cyclic stability, enduring 50,000 charge-discharge cycles with negligible capacitance decrease and almost complete retention of coulombic efficiency. Thus, the porous electrolyte emerges as a highly promising option for solid-state, flexible electrochemical energy storage devices. Ji et al. [225] have devised an interfacial crosslinking approach to produce integrated energy storage devices featuring stable electrode-electrolyte interfaces. The areal capacitance of these all-in-one supercapacitors reaches an impressive 806 mF.cm−2 (with a mass-specific capacitance of 403 F. g−1), maintaining 92 % of its original capacity after 1000 bending cycles. This performance is comparable to that of PANI-based gel electrolyte supercapacitors. Through the interfacial crosslinking strategy, each electrode can function as a component within a complex series-parallel system. Leveraging the autologous series crosslinking structure, both series and parallel configurations of 3D all-in-one supercapacitors are realized, reducing the number of current collectors and resulting in a more compact device volume. Moreover, the interfacial crosslinking strategy can be extended to fabricate compact and high-performance all-in-one energy storage devices by crosslinking various hydrogels like PVA and PAM. This approach holds significant potential for applications in flexible, wearable, and adaptable power sources. Hyang et al. [211] have recently pioneered the development of hydrogel polymer electrolytes featuring triple-network structures for supercapacitors (Fig. 11c), introducing multifunctional properties and performance enhancements. Through a combination of chemical cross-linking, hydrophobic association, and ion coordination, the resulting TNPE exhibited exceptional characteristics such as high ionic conductivity, remarkable ductility and recovery, and outstanding adhesion strength (55 J/m2) with AC electrodes.

These unique properties facilitated superior electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors, with nearly 100 % capacitance retention even under mechanical deformation, including bending and folding. The ion/water complexation within TNPE, supported by water structural analysis from FTIR and strong binding energy from DFT calculation, endowed TNPE with remarkable anti-freezing and anti-dehydration properties. Moreover, the supercapacitors demonstrated consistent and stable operation across a wide voltage range (up to 2.25 V), a broad temperature range (from −30 to 30 °C), and over extended operating cycles. Consequently, the TNPE-based supercapacitor achieved impressive values of energy density (17 Wh/kg) and power density (671 W/kg) while maintaining 90 % of the specific capacitance even after a rigorous 10,000-cycle retention test, highlighting its long-term durability and high performance have been depicted in Fig. 11d. Overall, this ground-breaking study presents new opportunities for advancing next-generation flexible and high-voltage supercapacitors with wide temperature durability. Looking ahead, further research efforts should focus on enhancing the performance and reliability of CPE-based micro-supercapacitors by exploring novel polymer matrices, optimizing filler loading and distribution, and investigating advanced fabrication techniques. Additionally, the integration of CPEs into multifunctional devices and the development of sustainable and eco-friendly electrolyte systems will be critical for realizing the full potential of FMSCs in various real-world applications. Overall, CPE-based FMSCs represent a promising technology that paves the way for the continued advancement of flexible and wearable electronics.

The electrochemical performance of finger electrodes and devices can exhibit significant variations depending on the manufacturing processes employed. This suggests that the preparation process itself plays a crucial role in shaping device attributes, likely for the following reasons. Firstly, even slight differences in the manufacturing procedure for a specific electrode material can lead to distinct microstructures, thereby influencing the device attributes in terms of ion and electron transport capabilities. Secondly, it is well-established that the electrochemical performance of micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) is influenced by both electrode width and the interspace between two electrodes[226,227]. Moreover, the precision of these characteristics is subject to the effects of fabrication processes, contributing to the divergence in electrochemical performance. In light of these considerations, this section undertakes a comprehensive review of some of the most common fabrication methods documented for patterning electrode finger arrays. Additionally, a comparative analysis of the advantages and drawbacks associated with each technique will be conducted. The aim is to provide valuable insights and guidelines for manufacturing high-performance MSCs by understanding the nuanced impact of various fabrication methods on device attributes.

Inkjet printing, as an innovative additive manufacturing technique, holds tremendous potential for fabricating electronic devices due to its numerous advantages, including a straightforward process, cost-effectiveness, excellent patterning capability, and the ability for large-area fabrication [228,229] . Despite its great potential, inkjet-printed micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) are still in the early stages, and the resulting electrochemical performances are relatively modest. This is primarily attributed to the small surface area and low ion diffusion efficiency of the printed electroactive materials[230].

Delekta et al. [229] have demonstrated a comprehensive inkjet printing method for creating micro-supercapacitors entirely from graphene. These devices consist of inkjet-printed graphene films, serving as both current collectors and active electrodes, and inkjet-printed nano-graphene oxide (nGO) acting as the electrolyte. Leveraging nGO's high ionic conductivity and hygroscopic properties, we achieved impressive areal capacitances of up to 313 μF cm−2 at 5 mV s−1 with an overall device thickness of approximately 1 μm. To optimize performance across device volume, we systematically investigated the impact of thickness on performance. Consequently, our ultrathin devices achieved power and energy densities comparable to those with thicker or liquid electrolytes. This fully inkjet-printed approach offers scalability, cost-effectiveness, and eliminates manual assembly steps and bulky inactive components, making it highly attractive for leveraging graphene's potential. With its outstanding performance and compact design, our devices hold promise as power sources for miniaturized electronics. Li et.al [187] successfully demonstrated fabrication of MoS2 nanosheets based low-cost and planar Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) using a conventional desktop inkjet printer using a conventional desktop inkjet printer. Achieving a high areal capacitance of 175 μF cm−2 at 4 μA cm−2, these MSCs demonstrate maximum energy and power density values of 0.215 mWh cm−3 and 0.022 W cm−3, respectively. Furthermore, even under a high-power density of 0.079 W cm−3, the volumetric energy density of MSCs remains at 0.137 mWh cm−3. The devices also showcase a prolonged cycling lifetime, retaining 85.6 % capacitance even after 10,000 cycles, and negligible capacitance changes under various bending radii. This underscores their outstanding mechanical flexibility, robust cycle stability, and ease of scalability. Wang and team [231] addressed this challenge by preparing aqueous MXene inks for inkjet-printing micro-supercapacitors, The processes involved in assembling and printing the MXene/SA-Fe ink for Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) are illustrated in Fig. 12a . The aqueous MXene/SA-Fe ink exhibits excellent solution processability, possesses suitable viscosity, and can be effortlessly printed directly onto A4 paper, allowing for customizable patterns (Fig. 12b).
