Importantly, the MXene/SA-Fe ink demonstrates a well-matched surface energy with various substrates, as evidenced by its successful printing of interdigitated electrodes on glass and PET. Despite the significant developments in inkjet-printing technology for scalable and adaptable energy storage devices, the quest for additive-free and environmentally friendly aqueous inks remains a significant challenge. Therefore, an aqueous MXene/sodium alginate-Fe2+ hybrid ink (denoted as MXene/SA-Fe) with solution processability and suitable viscosity was developed for direct inkjet printing of MSCs.

Sodium alginate (SA) molecules were adsorbed on the surface of MXene nanosheets, constructing three-dimensional (3D) structures that effectively alleviated the issues of oxidation and self-restacking of MXene. Simultaneously, Fe2+ ions compressed the ineffective macropore volume, making the 3D structure more compact. Hydrogen and covalent bonding formed between MXene nanosheets, SA, and Fe2+, protecting against the oxidation of MXene and increasing its stability. The MXene/SA-Fe ink provided abundant active sites for ion storage and a highly conductive network for electron transfer. Inkjet-printed MSCs with an electrode spacing of 310 μm exhibited remarkable capacitances of 123.8 mF cm−2 (@5 mV s−1), good rate capability, an extraordinary energy density of 8.44 μWh cm−2 at a power density of 33.70 μW cm−2, long-term cycling stability with 91.4 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles, and impressive mechanical durability with 90.0 % of the initial capacitance retained after 10,000 bending cycles (Fig. 12c). Three MXene/SA-Fe Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) were connected in series to supply power to a digital timer with an operating voltage of 1.6 V or a red light-emitting diode (LED) with an operating voltage of 1.8 V. This configuration demonstrates the practical applicability of the setup, as depicted in Fig. 12d. MXene/SA-Fe inks are anticipated to open up various opportunities for printable electronics. Flexible MXene/graphene composite electrodes were successfully produced through an inkjet printing process using printable graphene-modified MXene-based ink, as detailed by Wen and his co-workers[232]. Their analysis revealed that the interlayer spacing in the printed MXene-based composite films is increased through the insertion of graphene nanosheets, effectively reducing the self-stacking effect of MXene. The printed MXene/1 wt% graphene composite electrodes demonstrated outstanding stability, with 89 % resistance retention at a 180° bending angle. They also exhibited a high volumetric capacitance of 183.5 F cm−3 at a scanning speed of 5 mVs−1 and long cycle life, retaining 75 % capacitance after 3000 charge-discharge cycles. Furthermore, a flexible supercapacitor based on these electrodes demonstrated competitive energy density (0.53 μWh cm−2) at a power density of 10 μW cm−2, offering prospects for the design of next-generation flexible and wearable energy storage devices.

In summary, although inkjet printing may have lower production efficiency compared to screen printing, its high resolution stands out as a significant advantage. Additionally, inkjet printing allows for a diverse range of substrates and electrode materials, opening the door to the fabrication of high-performance flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs). By printing large-scale integrated MSCs on flexible substrates, it becomes possible to achieve a high voltage window and energy density, crucial for the practical application of MSCs in high voltage systems. Inkjet printing holds the potential for further enhancement, and it is expected to elevate the manufacturing of high-performance MSCs by precisely controlling electrode size and spacing while improving ink quality.

Screen printing is a technique that utilizes ink to create specific patterns on substrates using a screen. It offers advantages such as speed, simplicity, and low cost, and is adaptable for direct printing on various substrates. Consequently, this method is well-suited for the manufacturing of Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs), particularly in large-scale production [233–235]. For example, D. Jiang et al. [234] have successfully utilized a straightforward vulcanization method to create 3D-networked G/CoS2/Ni3S4 nanosheets. This unique architecture significantly enhances the active sites of the battery-like electrode material and facilitates ion and electron transportation, resulting in a high specific capacitance of 1739 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1. Printable G/CoS2/Ni3S4 inks have been developed, and a rapid and scalable screen-printing technique has been employed to fabricate arbitrary-shaped film electrodes shown in Fig. 13b for flexible G/CoS2/Ni3S4-based hybrid supercapacitor devices (HSCDs). In a two-electrode system, the G/CoS2/Ni3S4 hybrid supercapacitor demonstrates a high energy density of 44.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 224.8 W kg−1. Impressively, 79.6 % of the capacitance is retained after 10,000 charge/discharge cycles, indicating excellent cycling stability. Additionally, the electrochemical performance of the fabricated G/CoS2/Ni3S4-based HSCDs remains largely unaffected when subjected to bending at various angles or over 1000 bending cycles.

The significant outcomes of this study lie in the simple and abundant formation processes of the electrode materials and the utilization of screen printing for the production of arbitrary-shaped film electrodes on diverse substrates. Furthermore, the flexibility of the resulting G/CoS2/Ni3S4-based HSCDs enables their industrial-level printing, laying the groundwork for fully printable fabrication of portable and wearable energy storage devices, as well as other electronic applications in the future.

The conventional screen-printing approach involves printing active electrode materials and current collectors separately. Notably, a recent advancement by Lin et al. [236] showcases the use of an environmentally friendly water-based conductive ink. They successfully prepared and developed a CO-CNT-MnO2-based micro-electrode on stretchable cotton/spandex fabric through screen printing, as illustrated in Fig. 13a. The CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs), featuring a forked finger structure, are further assembled in combination with PVA/LiCl. The assembled device demonstrates high electrochemical performance attributed to the synergy of double-layer capacitance and pseudocapacitor capacitance storage. It achieves an area capacitance of 8.479 mF/cm2 at 10 mV/s and attains a maximum energy density of 0.132 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 0.176 mW/cm2. Moreover, the assembled MSCs exhibit notable capacitance retention even under stretching, folding, bending, and friction conditions. The CV curves for the CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based Micro-Supercapacitor (MSC) before and after stretching are depicted in Fig. 13d. The inset provides a schematic diagram illustrating the CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based microelectrode being stretched multiple times in both the warp and weft directions. Examining the CV curve in Fig. 13d, it reveals that the specific capacitance of the CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based MSC after folding is 79.87 % of that before folding, the inset presents a schematic diagram showing the CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based microelectrode attached to the inside of the elbow, folded several times, indicating satisfactory retention rate and fold resistance. Moving on to the CV curves of the CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based micro-electrode in Fig. 13d, and the bending schematic in the inset, it is evident that there is no significant change in the curve's shape before and after bending. The specific capacitance of the MSC based on CO-CNT-MnO2/F can maintain 98.05 % of that before bending, highlighting the device's good bending resistance. The inset in Fig. 13d is a schematic diagram illustrating the rubbing of a 1.5 kg weight wrapped in white cloth on the surface of the CO-CNT-MnO2/F-based microelectrode several times. This study underscores the significant potential of the new water-based conductive ink for stretchable MSC applications based on screen printing. In a related study, Li and his group [237] have developed high volumetric energy density micro-supercapacitors using a high-resolution thixotropic-ternary hybrid interdigital micro-electrodes approach via screen printing technique. Their study introduces a general and scalable screen-printing technique for the one-step construction of high-performance MSCs. This involves utilizing a thixotropic hybrid ink composed of hydrous ruthenium oxide (RuO2·xH2O) nanoparticles, silver nanowire (AgNW), and graphene oxide (GO) as interdigital in-plane microelectrodes.

It's noteworthy that the electrode inks' excellent printability, along with the remarkable conductivity and flexibility of the printed electrodes, facilitate reliable mass production of flexible MSCs on paper substrates through a fully printed method, eliminating the need for additional metal current collectors, polymer binders, or conductive additives. along with optical images and optical microscopy images, demonstrate that the interdigital electrodes in RArG-4 MSCs can be printed with various widths (RArG-4–200, RArG-4–150, RArG-4–100, and RArG-4–50, with the number representing the widths in microns). Furthermore, the well-defined shape and uniform edges of the printed MSCs help minimize the potential for short circuits and enhance printing reliability.

Benefiting from the synergistic effects of ternary nanocomponents, high-resolution microelectrode fingers reaching up to 50 μm, and an exceptional electrical conductivity exceeding 5000 S cm−1, the fully-printed MSCs produced achieve a ground breaking volumetric capacitance of 338 F cm−3, a landmark volumetric energy density of 18.8 mW h cm−3, and a power density of 40.9 W cm−3. These values surpass those reported for previously printed MSCs. Additionally, the printed MSCs demonstrate long-term cycling stability, retaining 91.6 % capacitance after 8000 cycles, and remarkable mechanical flexibility, maintaining 88.6 % of the initial capacitance after 2000 bending cycles. In a related development. Lindsay E. Chaney and colleagues[238] have developed fully and rapidly screen-printed MSC arrays using a high-temperature hBN ionogel electrolyte that boasts robust mechanical properties and superior ionic conductivity. The entire device production leverages screen printing, a high-throughput additive manufacturing technique commonly utilized in the production of printed electronics. This method not only enhances efficiency but also reduces waste, making the process scalable and environmentally friendly. The devices achieve impressive areal capacitances close to 1 mF cm−2, facilitated by the use of screen-printed graphene IDEs combined with the hBN ionogel. The high electrical conductivity of screen-printed graphene films has led to their use in printing conductive interconnects with the same graphene ink. Various integrated device configurations are displayed, such as two devices connected in series (2S), two devices connected in parallel (2P), and a combined 2S×2P array of devices as seen in Fig. 13c. The corresponding cyclic voltammograms and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves demonstrate expected behavior: devices in series extend the operating voltage window, while devices in parallel show increased discharge current (Fig. 13c).

Screen printing excels over drop-on-demand printing by enabling the rapid patterning of large-area device arrays. For instance, an array of 100 devices (10S × 10P) is patterned with minimal additional effort compared to producing a single device. These arrays exhibit robust electrochemical performance, characterized by rectangular cyclic voltammograms and triangular galvanostatic curves within an extended 10 V window. Furthermore, the resilience of these arrays to mechanical stress is notable; the 10S × 10P array's performance remains stable even when bent to a radius of 2.5 cm. This demonstrates the potential of screen-printed graphene in creating flexible, large-scale, and durable integrated micro-supercapacitor arrays for various applications. By incorporating hBN as the solid matrix, the team has successfully eliminated the polymers that typically hinder high-temperature performance in traditional gel electrolytes. Consequently, these screen-printed MSC arrays exhibit outstanding cyclability at temperatures up to 180 °C and enhanced power density, thanks to the increased ionic conductivity of the hBN ionogel under high temperatures. This pioneering work lays the groundwork for an additive manufacturing platform that could advance on-chip energy storage solutions for applications in extreme environments, such as underground exploration, aviation, and electric vehicles. Yang et al [239]. successfully demonstrated the fabrication of high-performance planar MoS2/EEG (Expanded Graphene) MSCs using the convenient and widely adopted screen-printing process. Leveraging high-quality EEG and pseudocapacitive MoS2 nanoparticles, the assembled MoS2/EEG MSCs exhibit notable capacitance characteristics, including a remarkable areal capacitance of 13.6 mF cm–2 and a high energy density of 1.478 mWh cm–2 at an outstanding power density of 0.08 W cm–2. This highlights their enormous potential and versatility in energy supply applications. Furthermore, the MoS2/EEG MSCs maintain stable electrochemical properties when bent to varying degrees, indicating their suitability for diverse flexible scenarios. The remarkable capacitance retention over 10,000 cycles ensures the reliability and continuous energy supply of the novel screen-printed planar MSCs.

Tian et al [240]. demonstrated the creation of an Implantable and Biodegradable MSC based on a super-assembled three-dimensional network Zn@PPy hybrid electrode using screen printing and electrodeposition techniques. The resulting super-assembled interdigital pseudocapacitor exhibits superior electrochemical performance attributed to the high capacitances and excellent rate performances of the patterned Zn@PPy electrode and NaCl/agarose electrolyte. The optimized biodegradable supercapacitor achieves a maximum energy density of 0.394 mWh cm−2 and can fully degrade in vivo within 30 days without adverse effects on the host organism. This breakthrough offers a new platform for transient electronic technology in various implantable electronic applications. Screen printing's ability for large-batch production in an extremely short time is one of its significant advantages, challenging to achieve with other fabrication methods. Furthermore, the step-by-step printing of both electrode materials and electrolytes facilitated by screen printing may pave the way for the fabrication of all-solid-state MSCs. However, it's crucial to note that challenges such as the preparation of electrode material ink and its low resolution still need addressing to fully unlock the application potential of screen printing.

Three-dimensional (3D) printing, also referred to as additive manufacturing (AM), stands out as a transformative technology offering diverse applications[241]. The ability to precisely deposit materials layer by layer allows for the creation of complex structures with tailored properties, making 3D printing a key technology in the development of advanced flexible energy storage devices. This technology allows for the accurate fabrication of electrode and electrolyte materials in three dimensions using computer-aided design. There are 4 main types of 3D printing techniques as fallows.
(a) Direct Ink Writing (DIW)

Direct Ink Writing (DIW) is one of the most commonly used 3D printing techniques for fabricating flexible microsupercapacitors. In DIW, a viscoelastic ink is extruded through a nozzle onto a substrate in a continuous manner, forming patterns or structures layer by layer. DIW can utilize a wide range of materials, including conductive polymers, graphene-based inks, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and metal oxides. The ink's viscosity is crucial, as it must be high enough to retain its shape after extrusion but low enough to flow through the nozzle. The process involves preparing an ink that contains the active materials dispersed in a solvent or polymer matrix. This ink is then extruded through a fine nozzle under pressure to create the desired pattern on a flexible substrate. After deposition, the printed structures are usually cured through thermal treatment or UV irradiation to solidify the material. DIW allows for precise control over the geometry of the printed structure, enabling the fabrication of complex architectures like interdigitated electrodes, porous structures, or hierarchical designs. The technique is compatible with a wide range of materials and substrates, allowing for the creation of customized flexible MSCs with tailored properties[242,243]. DIW is particularly suitable for fabricating microsupercapacitors with high surface area electrodes, which are essential for achieving high capacitance. This technique has been used to create wearable energy storage devices, integrated power sources for flexible electronics, and stretchable energy storage systems.
(b) Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)

Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) is another 3D printing technique that can be used to fabricate flexible microsupercapacitors. In FDM, a thermoplastic filament is heated and extruded through a nozzle, layer by layer, to create a 3D object. FDM typically uses thermoplastic materials like polylactic acid (PLA), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS), and thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). For MSCs, these materials are often blended or coated with conductive fillers like graphene, CNTs, or metal particles to create conductive composites. The thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated nozzle, where it is melted and extruded onto a build platform[244]. The material solidifies upon cooling, and the process is repeated layer by layer to build up the desired structure. In the case of MSCs, the printed structure can be further processed, such as by incorporating electrodes, electrolytes, and separators. FDM is widely accessible and cost-effective, making it a popular choice for prototyping and small-scale production. The technique allows for the creation of lightweight, flexible structures with moderate mechanical strength. The use of conductive composites enables the fabrication of all-in-one devices where the MSC is integrated into a flexible structure. FDM is used to fabricate structural components of MSCs, such as casings, substrates, or integrated current collectors. It is particularly useful for creating devices with specific mechanical properties, such as flexibility, stretchability, or impact resistance.
(c) Stereolithography (SLA)

Stereolithography (SLA) is a 3D printing technique that uses a photopolymerization process to solidify liquid resin into solid structures. It is known for its high resolution and ability to create intricate details. SLA uses photosensitive resins that harden when exposed to light, typically from a laser or a digital light projector (DLP). To fabricate MSCs, the resin can be mixed with conductive materials like graphene, carbon black, or metal nanoparticles. The process begins with a liquid resin that is selectively exposed to a light source, causing it to solidify in a layer-by-layer fashion. The build platform is incrementally lowered or raised, and successive layers are added until the entire structure is formed. The printed structure is then washed to remove excess resin and cured under UV light to fully solidify the material. SLA offers very high resolution, allowing for the fabrication of intricate microsupercapacitor designs with fine features. It also enables the creation of smooth surfaces and complex geometries that are difficult to achieve with other 3D printing techniques [245].SLA is particularly suitable for fabricating microstructured electrodes, intricate current collectors, and other fine features in MSCs. It is often used in applications where high precision and detail are required, such as in microfluidic energy storage devices or on-chip power sources.
(d) Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)

Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a 3D printing technique that uses a laser to sinter powdered material into solid structures. This method can be adapted for the fabrication of flexible microsupercapacitors by using conductive and flexible powders. SLS typically uses powdered materials, including polymers, metals, and ceramics. For MSCs, conductive powders like carbon black, metal oxides, or composites are used, sometimes mixed with polymer powders to enhance flexibility[243]. In SLS, a thin layer of powder is spread over the build platform, and a laser selectively fuses the powder based on the desired pattern. The build platform is lowered, and another layer of powder is added and fused until the entire object is formed. The unfused powder is then removed, leaving the solid structure[246]. SLS allows for the creation of complex, high-strength structures without the need for support materials. The use of powdered materials also enables the incorporation of various functional materials into the structure, such as conductive pathways or electrode materials. SLS is used to fabricate components of MSCs that require high mechanical strength and complex geometries, such as casings or supports. It can also be used to create conductive frameworks for electrodes or current collectors within flexible MSCs.

Among the various 3D printing processes, Direct Ink Writing (DIW) has gained popularity, relying on the extrusion of ink through a nozzle and the subsequent layer-by-layer deposition to create 3D patterned structures. Particularly in miniaturized energy storage applications, DIW is favored for its cost-effectiveness and the versatility of materials it accommodates. Research endeavors in the realm of 3D printed Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) represent a burgeoning frontier. Despite being in the early stages, significant efforts have been invested in optimizing energy storage effectiveness. A crucial aspect of this exploration involves tailoring electrode materials to further enhance the electrochemical behaviors of 3D printed MSCs.

A notable example by Li et al. [247] showcases the use of 3D printing and electrochemical polymerization to craft Polypyrrole (PPy)@Ag-wall interdigitated electrode micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs), as depicted in Fig. 14a . This innovative approach exemplifies the evolving landscape of 3D printing technology for advanced energy storage solutions. The Ag-wall featured a tightly wrapped uniform film of PPy (Fig. 14a). The operational mechanism of the Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) could be elucidated through the redox reaction occurring between adjacent PPy@Ag-wall electrodes. Furthermore, the design offered several advantages: 1) enhanced conductivity, reducing cycle time; 2) the thin PPy film minimized internal structural changes during charging and discharging, contributing to a prolonged cycle life. To assess the adhesion between PPy and Ag, a 3 M tape test was conducted, revealing no surface cracks and almost constant resistance after 200 tests. In practical application, the manufactured PPy@Ag-wall interdigitated electrode MSCs were connected in series to power light-emitting diodes (LEDs), under severe deformation, the PPy@Ag-wall interdigitated electrodes MSCs demonstrated resilience. This robust performance under deformation underscores the potential durability and versatility of these 3D-printed micro-supercapacitors for various applications. The MSCs presented in this study showcase exceptional area capacitance, cycle-life, and flexibility. The Ag-wall serves as a highly conductive conduit, facilitating rapid charge transfer, while the PPy coating on its surface creates numerous active sites. In comparison to conventional PPy-based MSCs, our flexible MSCs demonstrate superior electrochemical performance, boasting an area capacitance of up to 90 mF cm−2 and an energy density as high as 12.5 Wh cm−2. The unique combination of the Ag-wall's conductivity and the thin PPy coating enables faster electron transit, increased flexibility, and an extended cycle life.

This innovative PPy@Ag-wall structure offers an alternative approach to enhancing device performance, differing significantly from previously reported designs. We believe that this distinctive configuration can inspire novel ideas for future small energy storage devices. Notably, recent advancements in cost-efficient 3D printing of MSCs, utilizing Fe2O3/graphene/Ag electrodes by Tang et al. [248], have further expanded the possibilities. Their electrode ink exhibited desirable printability and enabled excellent extrusion processes. Tuning the ink formulation resulted in optimal 3D printed MSCs with high device areal capacitance (412.3 mF cm−2 at 2 mA cm−2) and a corresponding high energy density (65.4 μWh cm−2). The MSCs demonstrated an impressive capacitance retention of 89 % over 5000 charge and discharge cycles, showcasing excellent flexibility and stable charge storage behaviors during bending cycles. Additionally, the series and parallel connections of MSCs achieved higher operation voltage and current. These advancements underscore the versatility and potential of 3D printing for the development of high-performance, flexible energy storage devices. The bending angle, as defined in the inset of Fig. 14b, serves as a clear illustration of the outstanding mechanical flexibility exhibited by the Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) throughout the charge and discharge processes. devices is evident. Notably, a remarkable capacitance retention of 90.2 % was observed even after 500 bending cycles, underscoring the robust attachment of the 3D printed MSCs to the PET substrate and their consistent flexibility. Furthermore, the delivered voltage from MSC units connected in series exhibited a doubling effect compared to a single MSC, as evident in the CV curves and GCD curves. This outcome highlights the synergistic performance. Macroscopic image and SEM images of PPy@Ag-wall interdigitated electrode depicted in Fig. 14d. The 3D printing of hybrid-dimensional MSC electrodes holds immense promise for applications in next-generation miniaturized electronics. Yu Liu and colleagues[249] have introduced an all-3D-printing approach for constructing interdigital micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), leveraging an aqueous activated carbon (AC)-based electrode ink and a LiCl/SiO2 water-in-salt (WIS) gel electrolyte ink with superior rheological properties and electrochemical performance shown in Fig. 14c. In this strategy, interconnect expanded graphite (EG) sheets act as 3D scaffolds, accommodating both the active materials of AC for electron transport and forming self-supported AC/EG electrodes with a porous architecture, facilitating rapid ion transport and enhancing electrochemical performance, including rate capability and capacitance.

As a result, the aqueous all-printed planar AC-MSCs with a stable operating voltage of 2 V exhibited an ultrahigh areal capacitance of 2381 mF cm−2 and an energy density of 331 μWh cm−2, surpassing those of previously reported aqueous planar MSCs. This study underscores the feasibility and practicality of extrusion-based 3D printing for fabricating high-performance electrochemical energy storage devices. In a notable study by Tagliaferri and team [250], they demonstrated the 3D printing of micro-supercapacitor electrodes using aqueous inks of pristine graphene, eliminating the need for high-temperature processing and functional additives. With an intrinsic electrical conductivity of approximately 1370 S m−1 and thoughtfully designed architectures, the symmetric micro-supercapacitors exhibited exceptional areal capacitance, measuring 1.57 F cm−2 at 2 mA cm−2, with retention over 72 % after repeated voltage holding tests. The achieved areal power density was 0.968 mW cm−2, and the areal energy density reached 51.2 μWh cm−2. The increasing importance of Three-dimensional (3D) printing as a sustainable approach for crafting high-performance energy storage. This method allows the efficient production of devices with programmable geometry at various length scales, even down to micron-sized dimensions. In the context of on-chip technologies requiring energy autonomy, miniaturized energy storage devices play a fundamental role. The use of printed electrodes enhances the rapid penetration of the electrolyte into the active material, boosting capacitance and rate performance. Furthermore, 3D printing facilitates the creation of porous micron-sized electrodes, offering shorter ion diffusion paths and enhancing the rate capability at higher currents. Among various 3D printing techniques, extrusion-based 3D printing stands out for its ability to provide precise structural control in manufacturing electrodes for energy devices. This allows tailored fabrication of macropores and uninterrupted pathways, promoting efficient electrolyte infiltration and charge transport. Hence, the convergence of 3D printing and flexible micro-supercapacitors heralds a transformative era in energy storage innovation. The versatility afforded by 3D printing techniques empowers researchers to engineer intricate designs and tailored architectures, optimizing performance metrics such as energy density, power density, and mechanical flexibility. This synergy enables the development of next-generation micro-supercapacitors capable of meeting the evolving demands of modern electronics, from bendable wearables to conformable medical implants. Moreover, the additive manufacturing approach facilitates rapid prototyping and iteration, accelerating the pace of discovery and commercialization in this burgeoning field. As interdisciplinary collaboration continues to flourish and materials science advances, the trajectory of FMSC propelled by 3D printing holds boundless potential, promising to reshape the landscape of energy storage technologies and empower a myriad of emerging applications in the interconnected world of tomorrow.

The mask-assisted fabrication technique for flexible micro-supercapacitors involves a methodical process utilizing masks to precisely define electrode patterns and structures on flexible substrates. This technique enables the creation of intricate electrode configurations necessary for high-performance micro-supercapacitors.

By strategically placing masks during deposition or etching processes, specific areas of the substrate are exposed or protected, allowing for the selective deposition or removal of electrode materials. This approach ensures precise control over electrode dimensions, spacing, and alignment, critical for optimizing electrochemical performance and device functionality. Moreover, mask-assisted fabrication offers scalability and repeatability, making it suitable for large-scale production of flexible micro-supercapacitors with consistent performance. Additionally, it allows for the integration of multiple electrode materials and architectures, enabling the tailoring of device properties to meet diverse application requirements. A mask-assisted fabrication technique has enabled the creation of solid-state planar micro-supercapacitors with high energy density. In a recent development, Sen Wang and team [251] demonstrated a straightforward, flexible, and high-energy Potential Hydrogen Micro-Supercapacitor (PHMS) using a mask-assisted approach. This innovative design incorporated porous VN nanosheets as the negative electrode and Co(OH)2 nanoflowers as the positive electrode on a single substrate. The resulting PHMSs harnessed the synergies of porous VN nanosheets, Co(OH)2 nanoflowers, strong interfacial interaction with EG nanosheets, a simplified electrode manufacturing technique, and an advanced in-plane geometry. The VN//Co(OH)2-PHMSs, thus fabricated, exhibited an impressive areal capacitance of 21 mF cm−2, volumetric capacitance of 39.7 F cm−3, and a remarkable energy density of 12.4 mWh cm−3. This breakthrough not only underscores the efficacy of the mask-assisted approach but also highlights the potential of these PHMSs for high-performance energy storage applications. Manning Chen and colleagues[252] have developed an innovative type of high-voltage, anti-freezing hybrid gel electrolytes (HGEs) that are both cost-effective and highly conductive. They achieved this by integrating ethylene glycol (EG) into aqueous LiCl electrolytes. Using an optimal ratio of HGE, they constructed planar micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) with activated carbon (AC) electrodes and highly conductive few-layer graphene (FLG) as current collectors. These components were assembled using a mask-assisted vacuum filtration technique illustrated in Fig. 15a . The addition of EG disrupts the hydrogen bonding network among water molecules within the electrolyte, significantly expanding the voltage window of the HGE to 2.7 V and enhancing its anti-freezing properties. These improvements lead to a significant increase in the energy density and low-temperature stability of the MSCs. Importantly, the microdevices demonstrate excellent mechanical flexibility, superior module integration, uniform performance, and high practicability. Furthermore, this fabrication strategy is versatile and scalable, making it suitable for creating other types of anti-freezing microscale electrochemical energy storage devices, such as microscale lithium-ion batteries, to further enhance energy density. This advancement opens up new possibilities for developing flexible, anti-freezing microscale power sources ideal for smart electronics that operate in subzero conditions. In a related study, Zhu and team [253] employed a simple mask-painting method, to fabricate N-doped hierarchical porous carbon-based interdigitated symmetric micro-supercapacitors (MSCs). This approach further advances the field of micro-supercapacitor manufacturing, offering a straightforward yet effective technique for creating high-performance energy storage devices. Initially, a masking foil paper with an oil paper substrate was precisely cut using a bistoury scalpel in a cutting machine, creating a designed interdigitated logo that formed a hollowed-out mask.

This straightforward method can be extended to string interdigitated symmetrical Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs). The as-prepared MSCs underwent a thorough inspection on both front and back sides, ensuring quality control. Moreover, the technique allows for the preparation of serially connected MSCs without the need for conducting wires, enhancing the miniaturization. The versatility of this fabrication method opens up possibilities for tailoring MSC designs to meet specific application requirements and encourages innovation in the development of advanced micro-supercapacitors.

The symmetrical Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) demonstrated impressive electrochemical performance, boasting a notable areal capacitance of 36.5 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.25 mAcm−2 and a high energy density of 5.07 mW h cm−2 at a power density of 0.25 mWcm−2. The ultrahigh electrochemical stability of these MSCs was underscored by achieving over 98 % retention after 10,000 cycles and maintaining over 80 % of the initial capacitance after 500 cycles. In a separate advancement, Yuan and colleagues [254] introduced a facile and cost-effective mask-assisted drop-casting method for crafting adhesive MSCs on medical tape, utilizing a dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) doped poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) aqueous solution. The droplet of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) doped poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) aqueous solution did not immediately wet the medical tape, resulting in a circular PEDOT: PSS pattern with sharp edges and a smaller area than that of the initial droplet after drying, as depicted in Fig. 15b. Remarkably, this method yielded heart-shaped and circular patterns, almost identical to the masks used (Fig. 15b). Notably, the versatility of this technique was confirmed by its successful application to other aqueous solutions, such as MXene solution, showcasing its universality. A pair of parallel PEDOT: PSS electrodes on the medical tape, with a thin and clearly observable layer of PEDOT: PSS. The patterned electrodes were imaged using optical microscopy, revealing a typical width of approximately 1000 μm for a single electrode. A straight gap of about 950 μm wide was observed between two electrodes. This method, as demonstrated, provides a simple yet effective way to achieve precise and varied patterns for electrode fabrication on medical tape, offering flexibility and adaptability for diverse applications. The resulting MSC exhibited a high areal capacitance of 10.96 mF cm−2 and exceptional flexibility, retaining 96.2 % of its capacitance after 500 bending cycles. Leveraging the stickiness of the medical tape, the MSC could be conveniently affixed to various substrates such as gloves, cloth, paper, and walls, making it ideal for applications in wearable electronics and the Internet of Things. Furthermore, integration with a solar cell and a flex sensor allowed the development of a self-powered strain sensing system, enabling real-time detection of finger movements. This study lays the foundation for the development of adhesive energy storage devices tailored for wearable electronics, offering a strategic approach to advancing this field. In their study, Xiao et al. [255] showcased a streamlined manufacturing approach for high-energy Phosphorene-Graphene Micro-Supercapacitors (PG-MSCs) in an ionic liquid environment. The fabrication process involved mask-assisted directly-patterned interdigital electrodes composed of high-quality phosphorene and graphene. The resulting PG-MSCs exhibited exceptional electrochemical performance, boasting an impressive areal capacitance of 9.8 mF cm−2 and a remarkable energy density of approximately 11.6 mWh cm−3. This performance surpassed that of many previously reported nanocarbon-based Micro-Supercapacitors, emphasizing the efficacy of their simplified manufacturing technique and the potential of PG-MSCs for high-energy applications. To highlight the exceptional mechanical flexibility of planar Phosphorene-Graphene Micro-Supercapacitors (PG-MSCs), they fabricated traditional sandwich supercapacitors with a separator (Celgard 3501) positioned between two identical PG films on PET substrates, denoted as PG-SSCs. The electrochemical performance of both PG-MSCs and PG-SSCs was compared under various bending states, including 0, 90, 180 °, and a highly folded configuration, as depicted in Fig. 15c. Remarkably, all the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of PG-MSCs exhibited significant overlap even under highly folded conditions, underscoring their superior flexibility and electrochemical stability. In contrast, the CV shapes of PG-SSCs experienced substantial decline with increasing bending angle. This comparison underscores the distinct advantage of PG-MSCs in maintaining consistent electrochemical performance even under extreme bending conditions, emphasizing their potential for applications that demand exceptional mechanical flexibility and stability. In Fig. 15d, porous VN nanosheets function as the negative electrode, while Co(OH)2 nanoflowers serve as the positive electrode, operating at an optimized working voltage of 1.5 V. Cross-section scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and CV curves showcase the performance of VN//Co(OH)2-PHMSs (VN/Co(OH)2 ratio: 2.5) at different scan rates ranging from 2 to 50 mV s−1. The pseudocapacitive nature of VN//Co(OH)2-PHMSs is further affirmed through galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements, as illustrated in Fig. 15d. The volumetric capacitance of the device, measured at a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2, is quantified at 63 F cm−3, providing additional insight into the impressive electrochemical performance of VN//Co(OH)2-PHMSs.

The mask-assisted method has proven to be excellent for fabricating MSCs due to its simplicity, efficiency in depositing active materials and current collectors, and the elimination of the need for additional ink preparation or expensive equipment. However, challenges such as time-consuming mask preparation and limitations on substrate selection and transfer methods should be addressed for broader applicability. The mask-assisted method emerges as an excellent technique for MSC fabrication, offering ease of use and efficiency not only in depositing active materials but also in integrating current collectors. Notably, this method doesn't demand additional ink preparation or involve expensive equipment, providing a straightforward route to high-performance MSCs. However, it's important to acknowledge that the mask preparation itself can be a time-consuming process, posing a limitation on its development and applicability to some extent. Additionally, the substrate of MSCs obtained through mask-assisted filtration is confined to the filter membrane. To extend the substrate selection range (such as PET, PI, etc.), additional transfer methods are typically required. This limitation not only constrains the variety of substrates but also adds to the overall preparation time.

Photolithography stands out as a prevalent technique in the fabrication process of supercapacitors. Its widespread adoption in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) and Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (NEMS) stems from its simplicity, affordability, high precision, and established technology. Through the utilization of masks, photolithography enables the creation of intricate in-plane patterns in photoresists, making it a promising avenue for initiating the patterning of electrochemically active materials in micro-supercapacitors. One strategy involves depositing layers of active materials and current collectors separately onto the photoresist in two sequential steps. Research in this field primarily focuses on energy conversion through programmable materials and the integration of nano-bio interfaces. Following the removal of the photoresist, electrode finger arrays can be employed to achieve the desired patterns. Photolithography is a precise pattern-making technique that not only delivers high-resolution products but also intricate plane patterns, making it a frequent choice for creating templates for micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) electrode materials. This well-developed technology excels in producing metal conductive electrodes with patterned, high-resolution, and minimal-size features, making it suitable for on-chip MSC fabrication. L. Sun et al [256]. introduced a pioneering application of photolithography for the fabrication of single-wall carbon nanotube (SWNT)/carbon current collectors. They employed a carbonization process that transformed photoresist into amorphous carbon on SWNT surfaces. Through subsequent growth of MnO2 nanostructures, they achieved the development of an in-plane micro-supercapacitor (MSC) exhibiting an impressive stack capacitance of 20.4 F cm−3. This innovative approach underscores the versatility and effectiveness of photolithography in the creation of advanced energy storage devices with high performance characteristics. In the study conducted by Tian et al. [257], they employed a synergistic approach that integrated photolithography with physical vapor deposition (PVD) techniques to fabricate interdigital patterns and Ti/Au layers on a SiO2 substrate. Following this step, they deposited a holey graphene oxide intermediate layer onto the current collectors using a drop dry technique. Subsequently, they proceeded to electropolymerize the uppermost layer with polyaniline. This unique combination of methodologies yielded micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) characterized by an impressive stack capacitance of 271.1 F cm−3, a high energy density of 24.1 mWh cm−3, and exceptional rate capability. The integration of photolithography with PVD and subsequent deposition techniques showcases a novel approach towards fabricating MSCs with remarkable performance metrics, underscoring the potential of such multifaceted strategies in advancing energy storage technologies. In their work, Kim and colleagues[258] explored a microfabrication methodology that integrated photolithography with a solution process to generate interdigitated micro-patterns of pristine MXene and 3D interconnected nanoporous MXene electrodes. These electrodes were subsequently transferred onto flexible substrates, as illustrated in Fig. 16a . This innovative approach highlights the adaptability and versatility of photolithography in the design of intricate micro-supercapacitors (MSCs). By leveraging photolithography alongside solution processes, the researchers were able to create precisely defined patterns of MXene electrodes, enabling the fabrication of MSCs with enhanced performance characteristics and tailored functionalities. The highlighted examples indeed underscore the pivotal role of photolithography in driving the development of next-generation energy storage devices, particularly FMSCs, with diverse applications such as flexible electronics and wearable technologies. These instances vividly demonstrate the versatility and effectiveness of photolithography in fabricating MSCs with remarkable performance metrics. By integrating photolithography into the fabrication processes, researchers can achieve precise control over electrode patterns and create intricate structures, thus unlocking new avenues for enhancing the functionality and performance of MSCs. As a result, photolithography emerges as a cornerstone technology driving innovation in the development of high-performance energy storage devices with applications spanning from portable electronics to emerging wearable technologies. FMSCs have emerged as highly promising energy storage solutions suitable for scalable manufacturing, especially in meeting the demands of integrated microelectronics on an 8-inch wafer platform. A notable achievement in this domain involves the fabrication of MSCs totaling 107 chips on an 8-inch wafer, showcasing exceptional properties and performance metrics. These MSCs exhibit an outstanding volumetric capacitance of 1727 F cm−3, which stands as the highest reported value for MXenes, underscoring the efficacy of this technology in energy storage applications. Moreover, these flexible MSCs offer customizable features essential for integrated microelectronics, enabling tailored designs to meet specific application requirements. With a remarkable energy density of 41.9 mWh cm−3, maintained at a high-power density of 26.8 W cm−3, these devices demonstrate the capability to deliver substantial energy output efficiently. Impressively, even after enduring 10,000 bending cycles, the volumetric capacitance increases by approximately 140 %. This observation is particularly significant and highlights the resilience of planar interdigitated electrode micropatterns made of 2D MXene sheets under tensile stress on flexible polymer substrates. The unique combination of high performance, flexibility, and durability observed in these MSCs underscores the potential for scalable and sustainable manufacturing processes. This includes the creation of on-chip and flexible MSC devices that can be seamlessly integrated into electronic systems. With their ability to offer high power density and an extended lifetime, these MSCs hold considerable promise for various applications, including integrated power supplies in electronic devices and systems. Overall, this represents a significant advancement in the field of energy storage, paving the way for the development of next-generation flexible and integrated microelectronics. FMSCs have emerged as highly promising energy storage solutions suitable for scalable manufacturing, especially in meeting the demands of integrated microelectronics on an 8-inch wafer platform. A notable achievement in this domain involves the fabrication of MSCs totaling 107 chips on an 8-inch wafer, showcasing exceptional properties and performance metrics. These MSCs exhibit an outstanding volumetric capacitance of 1727 F cm−3, which stands as the highest reported value for MXenes, underscoring the efficacy of this technology in energy storage applications. Moreover, these flexible MSCs offer customizable features essential for integrated microelectronics, enabling tailored designs to meet specific application requirements. With a remarkable energy density of 41.9 mWh cm−3, maintained at a high-power density of 26.8 W cm−3, these devices demonstrate the capability to deliver substantial energy output efficiently. Impressively, even after enduring 10,000 bending cycles, the volumetric capacitance increases by approximately 140 %. This observation is particularly significant and highlights the resilience of planar interdigitated electrode micropatterns made of 2D MXene sheets under tensile stress on flexible polymer substrates. The unique combination of high performance, flexibility, and durability observed in these MSCs underscores the potential for scalable and sustainable manufacturing processes. This includes the creation of on-chip and flexible MSC devices that can be seamlessly integrated into electronic systems. With their ability to offer high power density and an extended lifetime, these MSCs hold considerable promise for various applications, including integrated power supplies in electronic devices and systems. Overall, this represents a significant advancement in the field of energy storage, paving the way for the development of next-generation flexible and integrated microelectronics. In another study by Zhu et al. [259], a photolithography technique was utilized to fabricate a patterned interdigitated current collector on clean silicon wafers through a lift-off protocol photoresist (see Fig. 16b). In the fabrication process, Cr/Au fingers (with a thickness of 5 nm/50 nm) were sequentially deposited onto silicon wafers through electron beam evaporation (EBV) and patterned using photoresist AZ 5214E, as depicted in Fig. 16b.

The progression of these devices through different fabrication steps is visualized in the film, created by phase inversion with embedded nano-CaCO3 and CNTs (PAN-CaCO3-CNTs), forms a porous structure, specifically a macropore. It's worth noting that a minor content of small pores persists in PIC-CNTs-MnO2, although they are too small to accommodate the MnO6 octahedron nucleus. The detailed morphology of PAN-CaCO3-CNTs composites is further elucidated in Fig. 16c. Crucially, this morphology remains intact without collapsing even after carbonization and the removal of nano-CaCO3 through treatment with aqueous HCl, as evidenced. However, after decoration, the majority of the pores are buried, leaving only a few large pores.

This approach contributes to the development of high-performance energy and power supply systems. High mass loading asymmetric MSCs were designed with positive electrodes comprising porous carbon with brines site coverage and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as conducting additives (PIC-CNTs-MnO2), while negative electrodes were composed of hierarchically porous active carbon mixed with CNTs (PICK-CNTs). The positive and negative electrode materials were tailored to ensure a high content of macro- and mesopores. MSCs with an optimized mass loading of 13.9 mg cm−2 (maximum: 23.6 mg cm−2) demonstrated ultrahigh areal capacitance (1.13 F cm−2), volumetric capacitance (22.6 Fcm−3), energy density (627.8 μWh cm−2), and a maximum power density of 64 mWcm−2. The devices exhibited remarkable capacitance retention (85 %) after 5000 cycles, and shunt and tandem device testing confirmed high uniformity, meeting the requirements of adjustable output currents and voltages in microchips.

Therefore, Photolithography is a key technique in the fabrication of micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), particularly valued for its ability to achieve high precision and resolution in small, integrated systems where these characteristics are crucial. As a mature technology originally developed for the semiconductor industry, photolithography allows for the detailed patterning of materials, which is essential for the intricate electrode structures required in high-performance MSCs. However, while photolithography offers distinct advantages in terms of precision, it is not without its challenges, particularly when considered for the full scope of MSC production. One significant issue is the necessity to integrate photolithography with other fabrication processes to create a complete production workflow. This integration often involves complex multi-step procedures that can introduce inefficiencies and increase production costs. Another notable challenge is the removal of templates used in the photolithography process, which typically requires either immersion in a buffer solution or exposure to high temperatures. These steps can be cumbersome and may risk damaging the microscale structures, thereby affecting the overall performance and yield of the MSCs. Moreover, the scalability of photolithography poses additional hurdles. While excellent for small-scale, high-precision tasks, photolithography can be less efficient and cost-effective when scaled up to larger production volumes. This is partly due to the extensive setup and processing time required for each batch, which does not translate well to high-throughput manufacturing environments. Environmental concerns also accompany the use of photolithography, particularly regarding the chemicals involved in the photoresist process. These chemicals can be toxic, posing health and safety risks during handling and disposal. The environmental impact of disposing of these chemicals, often classified as hazardous waste, further complicates the use of photolithography in MSC fabrication. Given these challenges, there is a clear need for further development and innovation in photolithography techniques for MSC production. This could involve the creation of more environmentally friendly photoresists, streamlined processes that reduce the need for harsh template removal conditions, or new integration methods that enhance compatibility with large-scale manufacturing technologies. By addressing these issues, photolithography could become even more effective for the future production of micro-supercapacitors, maintaining its crucial role in the advancement of integrated energy storage solutions.

Laser scribing has emerged as a cutting-edge technique for the fabrication of flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), offering precise and efficient patterning of electrode materials on flexible substrates. This innovative approach enables the creation of high-performance energy storage devices with enhanced flexibility, scalability, and integration into various electronic applications[260]. The process of laser scribing involves the use of a focused laser beam to selectively remove or modify materials on a substrate, thereby creating desired patterns with high precision. In the context of MSC fabrication, laser scribing is employed to define electrode structures and current collectors on flexible substrates, such as polymers or thin films. This technique offers several advantages over traditional fabrication methods, including High Precision, Flexibility, Scalability, Reduced Waste and Rapid Prototyping. Laser scribing allows for the creation of intricate electrode patterns with sub-micron resolution, enabling precise control over the design and dimensions of the MSCs. This precision is crucial for optimizing the performance and energy density of the devices.

By using flexible substrates, laser scribing enables the fabrication of MSCs that can conform to curved or irregular surfaces, making them ideal for flexible electronics and wearable applications. This flexibility ensures compatibility with a wide range of form factors and design requirements. Laser scribing is a highly scalable process that can be easily automated for mass production. This scalability makes it suitable for large-scale manufacturing of flexible MSCs, meeting the growing demand for high-performance energy storage solutions in various industries. Compared to traditional lithography-based techniques, laser scribing generates minimal waste and requires fewer processing steps. This results in lower production costs and environmental impact, making it a sustainable option for MSC fabrication which offers rapid prototyping capabilities, allowing researchers and engineers to quickly iterate and optimize MSC designs. This accelerates the development cycle and facilitates the exploration of novel materials and configurations. Laser technology has emerged as a leading precision micromachining tool in the fabrication of micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), particularly for creating interdigital patterns directly on current electrodes or active materials. This approach offers several unique advantages, including flexible design capabilities, large-scale manufacturing potential, and environmental friendliness. One of the key techniques in laser-based MSC fabrication is laser scribing, where a high-energy laser beam is precisely directed onto the surface of the workpiece. This process locally melts and gasifies the irradiated area, enabling the creation of intricate patterns with high precision and rapid speed. Laser scribing has gained popularity in MSC fabrication due to its simplicity and effectiveness. In a recent study by Xie et al. [261], Ni foams were patterned into interdigitated architecture current collectors using a direct laser machining method. Subsequently, pseudocapacitive materials were electrodeposited onto these collectors to create all-solid-state 3D MSC devices, as illustrated in Fig. 17
a.

This innovative approach showcases the versatility and effectiveness of laser technology in fabricating advanced MSCs with enhanced performance characteristics. Electrodeposited pseudocapacitive materials, manganese dioxide (MnO2), and polypyrrole (PPy) formed Ni foam-MnO2 and Ni foam-PPy composites. The resulting all-solid-state symmetric Ni foam-MnO2 −MSCs and Ni foam-PPy-MSCs, as well as an asymmetric MSC using Ni foam-MnO2 as a positive electrode and Ni foam-PPy as a negative electrode (Ni foam-MnO2//Ni foam-PPy), exhibited high energy density, good rate performance, and excellent cycling stability. The hybrid Ni foam-based asymmetric MSC (Ni foam-MnO2//Ni foam-PPy) displayed an areal capacitance of 59.29 mF/cm2 at 1 V/s with outstanding capacity retention of 95.6 % after 10,000 cycles. Moreover, this hybrid MSC demonstrated a high areal energy density ranging from 42.99 to 77.94 μWh/cm2, coupled with a corresponding power density range from 0.272 to 6.818 mW/cm2.

In another study Led by Zhang and team [264], a notable study utilized a CO2 laser engraving machine to fabricate a flexible interdigital electrode. Laser engraving proved to be a versatile and efficient method for creating customized electrode patterns, significantly contributing to the advancement of flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs). The incorporation of laser technology in these investigations underscores its effectiveness in precision patterning, crucial for fabricating high-performance MSCs. This approach offers precise control over electrode geometries, enabling tailored designs that enhance energy storage capabilities. Moreover, laser engraving's adaptability to various substrates, including flexible materials commonly used in MSC fabrication, ensures compatibility with diverse electronic applications. As research in this field progresses, laser engraving is poised to continue playing a pivotal role in driving innovation and accelerating the development of next-generation energy storage devices. In the realm of micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), innovative approaches leveraging laser technology have demonstrated remarkable performance improvements.

In one study, a Fork-Finger MSC (FMSC) was assembled with a planar interdigital electrode consisting of Laser-Induced Graphene (LIG) and a PVA/H3PO4 gel electrolyte (see Fig. 17b). The incorporation of a novel fork-finger electrode pattern optimized the structure of planar interdigital electrodes, resulting in excellent supercapacitance performance. FMSCs prepared at 12.5 % power exhibited a specific capacitance of 2.21 mF/cm2 at a voltage window of 0.8 V. In another notable work by Gao and collaborators [262], a simple and efficient laser direct writing method was employed on a GO/Co(CH3COO)2 precursor to process LrGO/Co/CoO nanocomposite interdigital electrodes for high-performance MSCs. Laser writing speed was found to influence the disorder, defect density, and sheet resistance of LrGO/Co/CoO, impacting its electrochemical performance. The resulting MSC, based on PVA/H3PO4 gel electrolyte and LrGO/Co/CoO interdigital electrodes, exhibited exceptional performance with a specific capacitance of 420.23 mF cm−2, power density of 1.97 mW cm−2, and energy density of 58.37 μWh cm−2. After 10,000 cycles, the Coulombic efficiency and capacitance retention rate were 97.97 % and 86.56 %, respectively. A different approach to laser-patterned MSCs was demonstrated by Park and team [263]. They created stretchable micro-supercapacitors integrated with soft electronic circuit components using a highly conductive and stretchable rGO/Au heterostructure.

Femtosecond-laser-induced vertically oriented graphene flakes, coupled with an Au layer, enabled large strain operation (up to 60 %) and improved rate capability and frequency response in the stretched state. These stretchable MSCs, easily connected in series, offer user-customized tuning of the cell voltage and flexible application in diverse soft electronic systems. The stretchability of the MSCs can also be controlled by designing the electrodes in wavy/curved shapes (see Fig. 17c). Laser scribing, as opposed to photolithography, provides a simpler and more straightforward fabrication process for MSCs, avoiding the need for additional templates or complex techniques. Laser scribing can directly convert polymer films into graphene and other active materials, promoting the production of active materials like metal oxides. This method has found extensive application in creating micro energy storage devices, particularly in the production of high-capacitance MSCs with excellent tensile characteristics. Overall, laser scribing represents a cutting-edge technique for the fabrication of flexible micro-supercapacitors, offering unparalleled precision, flexibility, scalability, and sustainability. As research in this field continues to advance, laser scribing is poised to play a key role in the realization of next-generation energy storage devices for a wide range of applications. Researchers in the field of flexible micro-supercapacitors are pioneering several novel fabrication methods that aim to enhance device performance, scalability, and integration into diverse applications, moving beyond traditional techniques like spray coating, electrospinning, 3D printing and laser scribing.

Among these innovations, Roll-to-Roll Assembly stands out for its efficiency in mass production, continuously depositing and patterning materials on flexible substrates, and notably reducing costs while increasing throughput. Self-Assembly Techniques leverage natural processes where materials organize into structured patterns autonomously, improving electrochemical properties through the precise arrangement of nanomaterials. Flash Freeze Processing quickly locks in highly porous structures by cooling electrode materials rapidly, thereby boosting energy storage capacity and maintaining nanostructure integrity. Direct Writing and Aerosol Jet Printing offer advancements in printing technology, allowing for the fabrication of intricate, high-resolution electronic components directly onto substrates. These methods are not only enhancing the manufacturing process but are also setting new standards for the integration of supercapacitors into next-generation flexible and wearable electronics, promising revolutionary changes in the industry. While various methods exist for manufacturing MSCs, each presents opportunities for improvement. Ongoing exploration and research into novel approaches will likely expand the range of MSC preparation techniques and further enhance their development. Table. 1 offers a comprehensive performance comparison of MSCs produced by different methods and electrode materials, highlighting the need for strategies that deliver both high capacitance and good cycle stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced packaging technologies for flexible microsupercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Advanced packaging technologies are vital for the effective deployment of flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs), as they ensure the protection, durability, and functionality of these devices in various applications. Effective packaging not only shields the delicate components of FMSCs but also maintains their mechanical flexibility and provides necessary environmental protection[140]. Encapsulation materials play a central role in protecting FMSCs from environmental factors such as moisture, oxygen, and mechanical stress. Flexible encapsulants, including silicone, polyurethane, and fluoropolymers, are commonly used because they offer robust protection while maintaining the necessary flexibility for wearable and flexible electronics. Additionally, barrier films made from materials like polyethylene terephthalate (PET) or polyimide serve as protective layers that shield the FMSC from contaminants and physical damage, preserving its functionality and extending its lifespan. The selection of encapsulation materials is critical, as they must adhere well to the FMSC components and be compatible with the materials used in the device. This ensures that the encapsulant provides effective protection without causing adverse reactions or degrading the performance of the FMSC. Moreover, the encapsulant must possess mechanical properties that allow it to flex with the device without cracking or delaminating, which is crucial for maintaining the integrity and flexibility of the FMSC. Packaging techniques involve applying these encapsulants and protective layers in ways that integrate seamlessly with the FMSC. Lamination is a common technique where protective films or materials are applied over the FMSC to create a robust barrier. This process enhances the mechanical durability and environmental resistance of the device. Roll-to-roll (R2R) processing is another technique that enables continuous, high-throughput manufacturing of flexible electronics[265]. In R2R processing, materials are fed through rollers for coating and patterning, which is particularly suitable for producing large quantities of FMSCs efficiently and uniformly. UV curing is often used to solidify certain encapsulants by exposing them to ultraviolet light, ensuring optimal adhesion and hardness. However, challenges include achieving a uniform coating across large areas and balancing the need for thorough protection with the efficiency of the manufacturing process.

Integration and alignment are crucial for maintaining the performance of FMSCs. Accurate alignment of packaging materials with the device components ensures that all areas are protected without affecting the device's functionality. Techniques such as optical alignment and automated placement systems are employed to achieve this precision. Additionally, the packaging must accommodate electrical interconnections, ensuring that these connections remain intact and functional while being protected. Ensuring that the packaging process does not compromise the integrity of these connections is essential for the reliable operation of the FMSC. Mechanical flexibility is a significant consideration in the packaging of FMSCs[86,91]. The encapsulants and substrates must be flexible enough to withstand bending, stretching, and other mechanical deformations without impairing the performance of the device. Flexible substrates like polymer films or conductive fabrics provide the necessary flexibility, while soft, stretchable encapsulants conform to the shape of the FMSC. Balancing the need for flexibility with effective protection is challenging, as the encapsulants must stretch and flex along with the device without cracking or peeling. Furthermore, ensuring the long-term durability of flexible encapsulants under various mechanical conditions is essential for the device's longevity. Environmental protection is another critical aspect of FMSC packaging. Effective moisture barriers are necessary to prevent water ingress, which can lead to degradation of the device components. Materials with low water vapor transmission rates are used to provide this protection. Temperature stability is also important, as packaging materials must withstand temperature fluctuations without losing their protective properties. The challenge lies in achieving effective barriers against moisture and temperature while maintaining the flexibility and thinness of the packaging materials. Additionally, ensuring that the packaging remains effective throughout the device's lifespan is crucial for reliable operation. Customization and integration are important as FMSCs are used in a wide range of applications. Packaging solutions must be tailored to fit various shapes and sizes of FMSCs, particularly when devices are embedded in unconventional or irregularly shaped product. Moreover, integrating FMSCs with other electronic components or sensors requires packaging solutions that accommodate these integrations while maintaining device performance. Developing customized packaging solutions adds complexity to the manufacturing process and requires careful design and engineering to ensure seamless integration with other components without compromising the packaging or FMSC performance[87,90]. In conclusion, advanced packaging technologies for flexible microsupercapacitors are essential for ensuring the devices' protection, functionality, and integration into diverse applications. Innovations in encapsulation materials, packaging techniques, alignment and integration methods, and environmental protection are key to overcoming the challenges associated with FMSC packaging. Addressing these challenges effectively will advance the field and broaden the applications of FMSCs in next-generation electronic devices and systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications and integration of flexible micro-supercapacitor

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) are poised to revolutionize the landscape of wearable technology, offering compact, lightweight, and versatile energy storage solutions. These MSCs find diverse applications in wearable electronics, health monitoring systems, biometric sensors, and energy-harvesting devices. By integrating flexible MSCs into wearable devices, such as smartwatches, fitness trackers, and health monitoring patches, continuous power can be provided for sensors and data transmission components. Moreover, flexible MSCs enable the development of self-powered wearable systems that harness energy from body movements or ambient environmental sources, ensuring prolonged operation without the need for external charging. With their seamless integration into wearable textiles and accessories, flexible MSCs pave the way for innovative applications in smart clothing, fabrics, and accessories, enabling advanced functionalities such as gesture-based control, communication, and energy harvesting[266]. Overall, the adoption of flexible MSCs in wearable energy storage opens up new horizons for the development of next-generation wearable electronics and personalized healthcare monitoring systems, offering enhanced functionality, comfort, and autonomy. The growing demand for wearable/flexible electronics has sparked increased interest in wearable energy storage devices, emphasizing not only energy/power density and cycling life but also flexibility, lightweight, and comfort. This is particularly relevant for smart textiles (E-textiles), where the integration of electronics into yarns, fabrics, or garments requires power devices that are both functional and stylish.

Conventional rigid batteries limit design versatility and convenience in smart textiles used for body thermal control, medical diagnosis, and sports monitoring [267]. To address this, an ideal power device for smart textiles should take the form of textile itself. An ground-breaking study led by Li and colleagues[82], a novel design for constructing high-section micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) was demonstrated, employing 3D textiles as the porous skeleton, as depicted in Fig. 18a . This innovative approach leverages the interdigitated structure of PEDOT: PSS supported by fabric fibers, serving as highly conductive conduits for rapid charge transfer. Additionally, pseudocapacitive MnO2 microspheres coated on the fabric's surface provide abundant active sites for enhanced energy storage capabilities. The fabric-based MSCs showcased remarkable performance metrics, including ultra-high area capacitance (135.4 mF cm−2), long-term cycling stability (93 % capacitance retention after 3000 cycles), and exceptional flexibility (94 % capacitance retention after 3000 bending cycles), all attributed to their unique structural properties. Furthermore, these devices demonstrated seamless integration into a sweatshirt, maintaining stable operation even after enduring vigorous activities and cleaning, as illustrated in Fig. 18b. Fabric-based Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) present a novel approach to enhancing energy storage device performance, particularly for applications in wearable textiles. Unlike conventional methods utilizing planar substrates or 3D printing configurations, fabric-based MSCs undergo rigorous durability testing under strain conditions, such as bending or twisting, to ensure resilience in harsh working environments. These soft textile MSC devices exhibit exceptional flexibility, capable of being bent, twisted, and even attached to the curvy skin of a human finger, showcasing their potential for seamless integration with smart textiles and wearable electronics. Pu and his team [83] introduced an innovative method for fabricating textile planar MSCs and integrating them into fashionable garments. This involved a combination of mask-assisted Ni-coating and the self-assembly of hydrothermal reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to design four MSCs on a polyester fabric, forming the letters "BINN" (refer to Fig. 18c). Remarkably, a green light-emitting diode (LED) can be illuminated by a DC current source through two conductive lines on the polyester fabric, as depicted in Fig. 18c. The final solid-state textile Micro-Supercapacitor (MSC) is presented in Fig. 18d, featuring 1.5 mm wide finger electrodes and 0.5 mm wide interstitials. Additionally, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image (Fig. 18e) showcases the Ni-coated interdigitated fabric, revealing a clear gap between finger electrodes while preserving the woven feature of the pristine fabric. Following the reduction graphene oxide (rGO) coating, each finger electrode is uniformly covered with a layer of rGO, as illustrated in Fig. 18f. At higher magnification, the wavy rGO ribbons become apparent (inset in Fig. 18f).

This innovative fabrication technique demonstrates the feasibility of integrating MSCs into textile-based electronics, paving the way for wearable energy-storing textiles with enhanced functionality and aesthetic appeal.

The resulting all-solid-state Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs) on a textile substrate exhibited impressive performance metrics, including high capacitance, stable cycling performance, superior rate capability, and stable mechanical stability under harsh deformation conditions. Specifically, the textile-based MSCs showcased a high capacitance of 8.19 mF cm−2 at a scanning rate of 0.01 V s−1, indicative of their efficient energy storage capabilities. Furthermore, these devices exhibited stable cycling performance, with 91 % capacitance retention over 10,000 cycles, highlighting their long-term durability and reliability. Additionally, the textile MSCs demonstrated superior rate capability, with 53 % capacitance retention even as the scanning rate increased from 0.01 to 2 V s−1, underscoring their ability to maintain performance under varying operating conditions. Importantly, these textile MSCs also exhibited stable mechanical stability under harsh deformation conditions, ensuring their resilience and suitability for integration into wearable electronics and smart textiles. Overall, these findings emphasize the potential of textile-based MSCs as high-performance energy storage solutions for wearable applications, offering efficient energy storage, long-term reliability, and mechanical robustness in demanding environments.

In a recent study by Wen et al. [81], the concept of a hybridized self-charging power textile system was introduced, offering a novel approach to sustainable energy harvesting for mobile and wearable electronics. This innovative system is designed to harness outdoor sunlight and random body motion energies simultaneously, leveraging the natural environment and human activity to generate power as illustrated in Fig. 18 g . The gathered energy is then transferred to an energy-storing cell, enabling the sustainable powering of electronic devices integrated into smart garments or wearable accessories. This hybridized self-charging textile technology, seamlessly woven into electronic textiles, presents exciting opportunities for the development of smart garments capable of controlling wearable electronic devices without the need for external power sources. By combining energy harvesting capabilities with wearable technology, this system enables enhanced functionality and autonomy, paving the way for the next generation of intelligent and self-sustaining wearable electronics. Abdelkader and his team [269] addressed the challenges associated with fabricating electronic devices on textiles by successfully printing graphene oxide (GO) on cotton textile, resulting in the creation of a flexible and durable solid-state supercapacitor (SC) device. This fabric-based SC exhibited an impressive aerial capacitance of 2.5 mF cm−2, demonstrating its high energy storage capability. Notably, the device maintained 95.6 % of these capacitance values even under bending conditions, highlighting its exceptional flexibility and mechanical durability. Moreover, the fabric-based SC demonstrated remarkable electrochemical stability, retaining 97 % of its original capacitance after 10,000 cycles of operation. This outstanding stability establishes the device as one of the most durable textile-based electronic devices reported to date, promising reliable performance over extended periods of use. Overall, the successful fabrication of a flexible and stable solid-state supercapacitor on cotton textile represents a significant advancement in the field of textile-based electronics, opening up new possibilities for the development of wearable energy storage solutions with enhanced functionality and durability. The integration of MSCs into textiles offers promising opportunities for the development of wearable energy storage devices, addressing the need for flexible, lightweight, and high-performance solutions in the field of smart textiles and wearable electronics.
