This pioneering research work represents a significant advancement in the field of micro-supercapacitor technology, offering a promising solution for energy storage in wearable electronics. The utilization of 3D textiles as the scaffold enhances both the performance and flexibility of MSCs, making them ideal candidates for integration into wearable garments and accessories. With their impressive performance characteristics and seamless integration capabilities, fabric-based MSCs hold tremendous potential for powering a wide range of wearable devices, enabling innovative applications in health monitoring, fitness tracking, and smart textiles.

The emergence of implanted medical devices, offering substantial potential in illness diagnosis and treatment, has spurred the demand for advanced electronic gadgets. Traditional medical electronics, especially energy-supplying devices, often raise concerns related to safety or toxicity, necessitating surgical removal after completing their tasks[270]. Such procedures can lead to secondary trauma for patients, heightened discomfort, and an increased risk of wound infection and chronic inflammations. Consequently, the imperative development of green, biocompatible, and biodegradable electronics is not only crucial for environmental considerations but also for the advancement of future intelligent medical treatments[271]. A revolutionary category of electronic devices, referred to as "transient electronics," has emerged in response to the rapid progress in biodegradable materials [272]. Transient electronics represent a sophisticated class of electronic devices designed to fully or partially dissolve or disintegrate in biological buffers or body fluids, with environmentally and physiologically friendly consequences. The unique biodegradability of transient electronics positions them as a pivotal direction for the future development of implantable medical devices [273]. This advancement holds the promise of mitigating potential risks and chronic inflammation associated with traditional permanent devices, thereby reducing hospital costs and enhancing patient outcomes. Utilizing screen printing and electrochemical deposition, Tian et al. [274]successfully fabricated an implantable and fully biodegradable three-dimensional network Zn@PPy hybrid electrode. The patterned Zn@PPy electrode, combined with a NaCl/agarose electrolyte, demonstrated substantial capacitances and exceptional rate performances, resulting in a superbly assembled interdigital pseudocapacitor with superior electrochemical characteristics. The optimized biodegradable Super Capacitor (SC) achieved the highest energy density of 0.394 mWh cm−2. To evaluate the biodegradation and biocompatibility of the developed Transient Super Capacitor (TSC) devices, a silk-encapsulated TSC was surgically implanted into the subcutaneous region of rats. This allowed for the real-time monitoring of the in vivo biodegradation process of the temporary device. After a 20-day duration, remnants of Transient Super Capacitor (TSC) components become evident in the subdermal region, with complete elimination observed after 30 days of implantation. Notably, the in vivo breakdown rate is slower compared to the in vitro process, attributed to variations in aqueous environments and circulatory conditions. Importantly, throughout this entire process, the rats exhibited no signs of sickness or apparent debilitation, underscoring the remarkable biocompatibility and biodegradability of the fabricated TSC devices.

To further assess the biocompatibility of the Supercapacitors (SCs), the Cell Count Kit-8 (CCK-8) analysis method and calcein-AM/PI staining were employed to investigate the cytotoxicity of each component of the device. Mouse macrophages were incubated with plasticized silk film, NaCl/agarose gel, silk protein-Zn film, and silk protein-Zn@PPy for varying durations. Notably, no significant difference in cell viability was observed among the different components tested. Both the vitality data and the fluorescence images collectively indicate that the fabricated Biocompatible Supercapacitor (BSC) exhibits ideal biocompatibility. Sheng and colleague[275] have developed a revolutionary wireless power system tailored for in vivo drug delivery applications. In response to these challenges, this team have engineered a wireless power system capable of receiving energy wirelessly from outside the body and storing it to power implantable electronic devices (Fig. 19a ). This integrated power supply device not only stores energy using supercapacitors but also generates stabilized and sustained DC power directly after rectification (Fig. 19a), which illustrate that simulated equivalent strains in the Mg layers remain below the elastic limit (0.3 %) in ∼30° twisted and ∼180° bent devices.

For instance, the integrated power system can adapt to curved surfaces such as biological tissue (Fig. 19a), human skin, and glassware with varying curvatures. To evaluate the degradation behavior of the integrated power system, accelerated hydrolysis is observed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions (pH 7.4, 80 °C) at different stages (Fig. 19b). This innovative system not only delivers instantaneous DC voltage but also sustains power generation over a specific duration through an energy storage module. Notably, this integrated device boasts exceptional tissue integrability, allowing it to conform seamlessly to curved biological surfaces with minimal tissue disruption for implantable purposes. Furthermore, it is designed to biodegrade within the body eventually. The meticulous selection of materials and device design results in outstanding electrochemical performance for the energy storage module, as evidenced by a thorough examination of electrode material structural changes during electrochemical degradation. Moreover, this wireless power system can seamlessly integrate with a compact, biodegradable, membrane-based drug delivery device, enabling remote control over the drug release process. This prototyped power supply system marks a significant advancement in transient implantable bioelectronic devices, offering promising energy solutions that are both effective and reliable. Overcoming the formidable challenge of creating implantable energy modules that are mechanically soft, compact, and biodegradable has been a significant feat. Sheng and his team[276] have achieved this milestone by developing a flexible, biodegradable, and bioabsorbable supercapacitor implant of high performance. Their approach involves the utilization of two-dimensional, defective amorphous MoOx flakes grown in situ on water-soluble Mo foil, serving as binder-free pseudocapacitive electrodes via a one-step electrochemical oxidation process. The primary focus of this study was to assess the cell compatibility of MoOx flakes and Alg-Na gel electrolyte. To achieve this, extraction solutions with varying concentrations were utilized in a 96-well plate setup, as depicted in Fig. 19c. Human endometrial adenocarcinoma (HEC-1-A) cells were chosen for their suitability in both short-term and long-term compatibility testing. Viability data and fluorescence images of the cells at different incubation times are presented in Fig. 19d, which illustrates the dissolution process of encapsulated biodegradable supercapacitors, with PLA polymer forming the edge and starch paper on the outside, immersed in PBS solution at different stages. Each MoOx electrode's dimensions are 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm, with a thickness of approximately 30 cm for the biodegradable supercapacitor device (inset of Fig. 19d). Moreover, in vivo evaluation of the supercapacitor's biodegradability as an implant was conducted. Encapsulated biodegradable supercapacitor implants (sized 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm) were subcutaneously implanted in the dorsal side of SD rats, as depicted in Fig. 19d. Following charging, successful illumination of a red LED light (with a threshold voltage of 1.5 V) was observed, as demonstrated in Fig. 19d.This unique design results in a lightweight, thin structure with exceptional flexibility and remarkable charge storage capabilities, boasting ultrahigh areal capacitance and energy density. Both in vitro and in vivo evaluations confirm the excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability of these supercapacitor implants. Moreover, the operational and degradation lifespan of these implants can be tailored across a wide range by employing suitable encapsulation strategies. Importantly, the supercapacitor implants can be entirely degraded in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and absorbed by SD rats after implantation. These findings pave the way for future advancements in biodegradable and bioabsorbable implantable bioelectronics, with potential implications extending to other fields such as hardware security.

Milani and colleagues [277] have introduced a remarkable fully natural-derived, flexible, and ultra-thin micro-supercapacitor, which operates above 1.5 V, achieved through an all-printed fabrication process. The ionogel electrolyte utilized comprises 2-hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) and the biodegradable ionic liquid choline lactate (CL), exhibiting an electrochemical stability window of 1.6 V. The electrodes are crafted from highly porous nanostructured carbon films. This micro-supercapacitor is fabricated through a layer-by-layer process leveraging printing technologies, ensuring scalability and micro-patterning capabilities. The energetic performances of this micro-supercapacitor have been rigorously tested, demonstrating delivery powers of 120 kW kg–1 (2.4 mW cm–2) and stored energies of 11.8 Wh kg–1 (0.36 μWh cm–2). Furthermore, the device demonstrates exceptional stability even under bending conditions. This advancement signifies a notable stride towards sustainable and high-performance micro-supercapacitors. Given their impressive performance metrics, natural derivation, lightweight nature, and rapid dissolution in aqueous solutions, our devices hold the potential to serve as valuable solutions for energy storage functionalities in transient electronic systems. These systems have diverse applications across strategic domains such as biomedicine, precision agriculture, and environmental monitoring. Despite extensive research on biodegradable materials and the emergence of various transient devices, the development of a biodegradable power source remains a significant challenge for future clinical applications. This underscores the ongoing need for innovative solutions in the realm of transient electronics.

Flexible energy storage facilities encompass flexible batteries and supercapacitors (SCs), both characterized by low energy density due to modest mass loading, necessitating frequent replenishment. An effective strategy involves integrating energy harvesting and storage devices into a unified system, even on a single plane. These integrated systems, referred to as self-powered systems, operate in a continuous energy collection and storage mode, providing a stable energy supply for various types of flexible electronics. Various types of energy conversion devices, including nanogenerators (converting mechanical to electric energy), solar cells (transforming light to electric energy), thermoelectric devices (converting heat to electrical energy), biofuel cells (generating electric energy from biotic sources), and others (utilizing water vapor absorption or salinity for electrical energy), have been extensively researched to address the pressing challenge of the energy crisis. While these options contribute to conserving non-renewable energy resources, they fall short in simultaneously addressing the issue of environmental pollution. Recently, Xiao and colleagues [278] have developed a remarkable 1-D biosupercapacitor, designed to be biocompatible and activated by sweat, offering an innovative alternative to traditional energy storage solutions. This technology aims to revolutionize the creation of self-charging sensing textiles by harnessing the power of sweat.

The biosupercapacitor, named SYBSC, is ingeniously constructed using two cotton-wrapped stainless-steel yarns modified with PPy/PEDOT: PSS. This symmetric assembly enables the SYBSC to boast impressive features such as a high areal capacitance (343.1 mF cm− 2 at 0.5 mA cm− 2), balanced energy density (ranging from 30.5 to 22.7 μW h cm− 2), and power density (ranging from 200 to 2000 μW cm− 2). Notably, the device demonstrates remarkable durability, maintaining satisfactory capacitance levels even after undergoing 10,000 bending cycles (retaining 68 %) and enduring 25 machine wash cycles (retaining 73 %). Furthermore, the SYBSCs can be seamlessly integrated with CYSABs (charged yet sweat-activated batteries), forming hybrid energy units. In this setup, SYBSCs can be charged by the activated batteries to yield a potent power output. The integration of SYBSCs, CYSABs, and pH sensing fibers into a structured textile, alongside a mini-analyzer, facilitates the creation of a sweat-activated, self-charging pH sensing system as shown in Fig. 20b . In practical tests conducted on volunteers during physical activities, the sweat naturally produced during exercise triggers the all-in-one sensing textile, powering the analyzer for real-time data collection and wireless transmission. This innovative system enables continuous monitoring of sweat pH levels during exercise, showcasing the potential for personalized health monitoring and data-driven insights. Qiu et al. [84] introduced a novel paradigm featuring a flexible all-in-one energy chip that integrates eco-friendly Photo Fuel Cells (PFCs) and asymmetric NiCoP@NiOOH//zeolite imidazolide framework (ZIF-8) derived carbon Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs). This comprehensive system was realized through screen printing and electrochemical deposition technologies, enabling efficient large-scale production. The all-in-one energy chip, comprising four PFCs in series and two tandem MSCs (Fig. 20a), was constructed to validate its practical applicability in portable electronics, as illustrated in Fig. 20c, which demonstration experiment was devised, and the corresponding circuits were illustrated. Initially, without UV light and with the addition of urea, the Photo Fuel Cells (PFCs) failed to produce any current to charge the Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs), resulting in the non-illumination of the light-emitting diode (LED). Subsequently, upon the activation of UV light (100 mW cm−2), the PFCs gradually charged the MSCs, reaching the voltage required to illuminate the red LED. Optical pictures in Fig. 20c further confirm the success of this design. Charging and galvanostatic discharging curves were investigated for PFCs and MSCs integrated together, as depicted in Fig. 20c. Under a UV irradiation intensity of 100 mW cm−2, the MSC reached a charge of 1.2 V after 288 s and was discharged for 703 s at a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2. This cyclic process, indicates the substantial potential for continuously supplying energy to portable or flexible electronics. Under a UV intensity of 30 mW cm−2 and in a 1 M urea solution, the output power density reached a remarkable 3.04 μW cm−2.

Furthermore, the bendable PFCs demonstrated the capability to continuously output energy by refilling fresh urea. The integration of two MSCs with the four PFCs in one plane enabled self-powering for small electronics, such as an LED, easily and consistently. This implies significant potential for future self-powered portable electronics. Juan Zeng et al. [279] have developed flexible solar-charging self-powered units based on a printed Zn-ion hybrid micro-capacitor serving as the energy storage module. The micro-Zinc Hybrid Capacitors (micro-ZHCs) feature a unique 3D hierarchical architecture using kelp-carbon and a multivalent ion storage mechanism, providing high areal capacity (10.28 μAh cm−2) and high energy density (8.2 μWh cm−2). This mechanism involves highly reversible and fast ion (Zn2+ and CF3SO3 −) adsorption/desorption on the kelp-carbon cathode, Zn2+ depositing/stripping reaction on the Zn anode, with Zn(CF3SO3)2 aqueous solution acting as the electrolyte. The integrated unit exhibits rapid photoelectric conversion, charging to 1.6 V in just 23 seconds, with tolerance for a wide range of light intensities (0.135–4.14 mW cm−2). Demonstrating excellent mechanical robustness and cycling stability, the unit retains 95 % of its capacity after 100 cycles. Moreover, this integrated unit can effortlessly power an electronic watch under indoor natural light, showcasing its wearability and practicality. This portable, wearable, and environmentally friendly self-powered unit represents a pioneering approach in designing energy-integrated systems, contributing towards the development of highly safe, lightweight, cost-effective, and long-life smart wearable electronics. The energy storage module is a crucial component of self-powered systems. While conventional micro-batteries used in portable devices face limitations in terms of longevity and power density, supercapacitors present a viable alternative.

Supercapacitors can address these shortcomings by enabling high-current charging and discharging within a short period (a few minutes) and sustaining over 100,000 cycles. Micro-Supercapacitors (MSCs), featuring two interdigital electrodes on a single chip, have garnered attention from researchers due to their compact size, thin profile, exceptional rate performance, and ease of integration with other microdevices compared to standard sandwiched structure supercapacitors. Gao and his team [280] introduced seamlessly integrated wireless charging micro-supercapacitors by leveraging a meticulously designed material system where both wireless coils and electrodes are made of graphite paper. The use of a unified graphite material ensures a smooth contact structure, excellent conductivity, and corrosion resistance in the Integrated Wireless Charging Micro-Supercapacitors (IWC-MSCs). Moreover, the IWC-MSCs exhibit an ultrahigh areal capacitance of 454.1 mF cm−2 and a record-breaking energy density of 463.1 Wh cm−2. The wireless charging performance of the integrated device was evaluated by examining the charging and discharging capability of the IWC-MSCs. In a typical wireless charging system comprising transmitting and receiving parts, the circuit diagrams are illustrated in Fig. 20d. The wireless charging mechanism operates on the principles of Electromagnetic Induction, converting magnetic field energy into electrical energy, outperforming previously reported planar MSCs. Remarkably, assembling IWC-IMSCs with an electrical toy vehicle and wirelessly charging for just 6 minutes allows the car to be driven swiftly, showcasing the potential for practical applications (Fig. 20d). This study not only inspires innovative concepts for the fabrication of integrated electrical devices but also enhances the convenience of small and portable electronics. However, the integration of self-powered devices comprising MSCs and energy generators still faces challenges. The existing loose and haphazard connectivity between power-generating equipment and MSCs leads to energy waste, resulting in low charging efficiency and insufficient charging voltage for MSCs. While structural developments have addressed some of these issues, there remains a scarcity of compatible and multifunctional materials. Creating integrated device structures that meet the demands of various real-world applications presents a substantial challenge. Therefore, the future of MSC-integrated devices should focus on creating multifunctional materials and designing integrated devices with compatibility between MSCs and other electronic components.

The increasing popularity of multifunctional wearable sensors and systems over recent decades can be attributed to their ability to offer real-time health monitoring and disease diagnosis. Scientific advancements have led to the development of wearable sensors and systems characterized by features like flexibility, comfort, and long-term stability. These technologies find widespread use in various applications, including temperature monitoring, pulse wave detection, and gait pattern analysis[281–283]. Given the intricacies of human physiological signals, simultaneous assessment of multiple physiological data points is crucial for a comprehensive evaluation of human health. In recent times, there has been a significant interest in integrated multifunctional devices that can incorporate triboelectric nanogenerators, supercapacitors, sensing devices, temperature sensors, and energy conversion/harvesting electronics[284]. However, in many integrated devices, each function operates independently, necessitating multiple processes. This approach increases both the cost and complexity of device integration. Consequently, achieving multifunctionality in a single wearable electronics device is an appealing goal, and the design of functional materials with dual properties presents a substantial challenge. Wang and collaborators [285] have introduced a multifunctional flexible self-powered device system based on MXene/TiS2 thin films, which seamlessly integrates electrochemical energy storage with a pressure-sensitive sensing function. They conducted a systematic exploration of the pressure sensing performance in conjunction with the developed solid-state micro-supercapacitor. The MXene/TiS2 thin film electrode demonstrated an impressive specific capacitance of 403.6 F/g (equivalent to 302.7 mF/cm2) at 1 A/g (0.75 mA/cm2). When configured as a symmetrical MXene/TiS2//MXene/TiS2 supercapacitor, it exhibited a satisfactory energy density of 31.6 Wh/kg at 400 W/kg, exceptional cyclic voltammetry (CV) characteristics even at 600 mV/s, and retained 79.8 % capacitance after 10,000 cycles. The pressure-sensing mechanism involves a pseudocapacitive iontronic self-powering MXene/TiS2//MXene/TiS2 microsensor. When fully charged, positive and negative charges are evenly distributed at both ends of the interdigital MXene/TiS2 electrode, as depicted in Fig. 21a . When attached to a finger, the sensor can distinguish various gripping actions. Different hand postures result in corresponding changes in current, with the clenching fist posture eliciting a stronger reaction due to maximum sensor deformation. Furthermore, serving as a self-powered pseudocapacitive iontronic microsensor system, the flexible pressure sensor exhibits exceptional detection sensitivity and rapid response/recovery times. This enables accurate and continuous detection of human body motion signals. This innovative multifunctional self-powering system design holds promising applications in the realization of smart wearable devices as the next generation of flexible electronics. Park and colleagues[286] showcased the development of a high-performance solid-state supercapacitor alongside multifunctional sensors for pressure, temperature, and strain utilizing a common active material of microporous PDMS/PPy/GF, as depicted in Fig. 21b. The created dual-mode sensor demonstrated the ability to sense pressure and temperature independently. It exhibited good sensitivity, with 2.01 kPa−1 at pressures less than 1 kPa, a low detection limit of 18 Pa, a rapid response time of 20 ms, and high durability over 10,000 pressure loading cycles. Additionally, it displayed a high thermoelectric sensitivity of 49.8 V K−1 and a swift temperature response/recovery time of 1.5 and 8.3 s. The applied pressure could be sensed by touching a finger to the dual-mode sensor, utilizing the self-generated thermoelectric power created by the temperature differential.

The study presented the current change and output voltage response of the dual-mode sensor to finger touches on a device, as depicted in Fig. 21d, to evaluate the self-powered and simultaneous sensing capability of the developed device. Impressively, the device successfully detected pressure differences of 0.8 kPa (weak pressure) and 6.5 kPa (strong pressure), as well as the temperature of the fingertip (30 °C with T = 4 K). Furthermore, the temperature gradient induced consistent changes in current and self-generated thermoelectric power within the device. A resistor-type strain sensor, utilizing the same material, demonstrated the capability to detect up to 50 % of the strain with consistent and predictable performance. Moreover, the flexible supercapacitor manufactured in the study exhibited an impressive areal capacitance of 89.6 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.6 mA cm−2, along with notable energy and power density values of 24 Wh cm−2 and 2.3 mW cm−2, respectively. Lastly, the integration of supercapacitors on a single flexible and skin-attachable substrate holds the potential for enabling wireless powering of these dual-mode and strain sensors. Overall, the study demonstrates that the new PDMS/PPy/GF material with a porous structure has promising applications in various energy storage devices and active sensor devices for wirelessly powered wearable electronics.

In addition, Yuan and colleagues [254]devised MSC tapes without current collectors for a self-powered strain sensing system. These MSC tapes were fabricated on medical tape using a straightforward and cost-effective mask-assisted drop-casting technique, which can be applied to various aqueous inks. The resulting MSC exhibited a high areal capacitance of 10.96 mF cm2 and remarkable flexibility, retaining 96.2 % of capacitance after 500 bending cycles. Leveraging the stickiness of the medical tape, the MSC can be easily affixed to various substrates such as gloves, cloth, paper, and walls. This feature facilitates applications in wearable electronics and the Internet of Things. Furthermore, integrating the MSC with a solar cell and a flex sensor enables the creation of a self-powered strain sensing system capable of real-time detection of finger movements, as illustrated in Fig. 21c. This research introduces a novel strategy for developing adhesive energy storage devices suitable for wearable electronics, thereby expanding the potential applications of MSC technology in the field.

A photodetector is an optoelectronic device designed to convert absorbed photons into an electrical signal[287]. The demand for high-performance photodetectors is significant across various applications such as fire detection, video imaging, biomedical imaging, night vision, and motion detection. Most commercially available photodetectors are constructed from bulky crystalline silicon or other 3D materials (Si/Ge heterojunctions, InGaAs semiconductor alloys) and are typically built on rigid substrates. However, their application in flexible and stretchable devices is impeded by their bulk volume, high cost, and complex manufacturing processes. The utilization of novel functional materials, including 0D and 1D inorganic nanostructures, 2D layered materials, and perovskite materials, has enabled the development of flexible photodetectors with excellent mechanical flexibility[288–291]. To address cost concerns, processing methods have been modified. Combining MSCs with a photodetector on a single substrate to create a flexible device presents a promising approach for future wearable and portable photo detecting applications. Cai and colleagues [246] utilized a cost-effective approach employing a 405 nm semiconductor laser to fabricate a flexible carbon micro-supercapacitor (MSC) with enhanced performance. This MSC, subjected to Ar-plasma treatment (referred to as MSC-Ar-plasma), not only served as an energy storage unit but also acted as a stabilizer within a self-powered system. The integration of this MSC into the system alongside a commercial solar panel and a digital LCD clock resulted in the maintenance of nearly constant current and voltage output, as visually depicted in Fig. 22a . This integration highlights the versatility and practicality of the MSC in real-world applications, offering stability and reliability in energy storage and distribution. Furthermore, the researchers extended their exploration by demonstrating a prototype self-powered photodetection system. This showcases the adaptability of the MSC in various energy harvesting and utilization scenarios, paving the way for innovative solutions in self-powered electronics and sensor technologies.

In this setup, a commercial solar panel was combined with an integrated circuit comprising an MSC and a UV photodetector based on ZnO nanoparticles. These ZnO nanoparticles were prepared using laser direct writing on a polyimide film. When driven by the MSC, the photodetector exhibited stable and high on/off ratio of 31.2, comparable to that observed when driven by a constant external voltage of 0.9 V. This innovative approach showcases the potential of flexible carbon micro-supercapacitors in self-powered systems, paving the way for diverse applications in energy storage and photodetection. Yang Yue and colleagues [292] introduced an economical and scalable approach for fabricating flexible all-solid-state asymmetric micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) using an enhanced inkjet printing method. These MSCs, along with perovskite nanowire-based photoconductive-type photodetectors and a wireless charging coil, were incorporated into a flexible microcircuit device system on a polyimide (PI) substrate. The complete system comprises a power receiver, an asymmetric MSC, and a perovskite nanowire photodetector for light detection, all seamlessly integrated onto a single substrate. The circuit diagram and a photograph of the actual product are depicted in Fig. 22d, with all elements seamlessly integrated on a single-planar substrate. The power receiver fully charges the MSC, which then powers the photodetector for light detection. To optimize light detection performance, perovskite nanowires (NWs) were chosen as the photo-responsive materials, synthesized through a straightforward two-step method.

The photo-responsive properties of the device were evaluated by measuring the I-V characteristics of the photoconductive-type photodetectors under both white illumination and darkness Fig. 22f. The current between the source and drain electrodes exhibited a linear dependence on the applied bias, indicating the ohmic contact between perovskite NWs and electrodes (Fig. 22d). A significantly stronger photocurrent compared to that in the dark highlighted the excellent photo-response of the perovskite NWs. Fig. 22f, illustrates the performance of the photodetector over time, powered by an external source and the MSC in the integrated device.

Chen and colleagues[294] developed a highly stretchable integrated system for micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) equipped with AC line filtering and UV photodetection capabilities. They achieved this by designing buckled micro-electrodes made from single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) films and titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) layered onto polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). These MSCs demonstrate an ultrafast frequency response, featuring a phase angle of −75.2° and a RC time constant of just 0.2 ms at 120 Hz, even when using a gel electrolyte. The design also allows for the MSCs to be configured in series, parallel, or a combination of both on a single PDMS substrate, which enhances the overall output potential and current while maintaining efficient AC line filtering. Notably, the AC line filtering performance of these MSCs remains consistent, even when stretched to 200 % strain, thanks to their uniquely structured electrodes and device architecture. TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) are widely recognized as effective electrode materials in photoelectrochemical devices due to their impressive efficiency, affordability, and chemical robustness. Leveraging these properties, TiO2 NPs were applied as a coating on the surface of SWCNT film micro-electrodes to impart them with an additional capability: photodetection illustrated in Fig. 22c. Additionally, by coating the buckled SWCNT micro-electrodes with TiO2 NPs, the MSCs gain the capability of detecting UV light with a sensitivity rating of 6.2. Remarkably, the integrated device retains its UV detection capabilities even when stretched to 200 % strain and after being repeatedly stretched 100 times. This thoughtful design of stretchable MSCs with dual functionality-AC line filtering and UV photodetection opens new avenues for incorporating supercapacitor technology and other electronic functionalities into highly stretchable integrated devices, suitable for advanced applications.

Zhong and team [295] have introduced a ground-breaking in-plane flexible microsystem, pioneering the integration of an all-solid-state flexible micro-supercapacitor (MSC) device with a photoconductive-type photodetector based on a perovskite film. The fabrication process of the MSC device is notable for its simplicity and scalability, incorporating techniques such as laser etching, electrochemical exfoliation, and electrochemical deposition. This innovative integrated system harnesses the power of MSC devices to drive the photoconductive detector. Remarkably, the system exhibited a stable photocurrent response, akin to the performance achieved when the detector is powered by an external power source. This remarkable outcome underscores the potential for constructing a flexible integrated system on a single chip, offering immense promise for applications in the realm of flexible electronics and photodetection. The seamless integration of energy storage and sensing functionalities within a single microsystem represents a significant advancement in the field, unlocking new avenues for the development of compact, flexible, and self-powered electronic devices. Such technologies hold immense potential for applications ranging from wearable electronics to smart sensors and beyond, paving the way for a future where flexibility and efficiency converge in next-generation electronic systems.

The integration of a flexible micro-supercapacitor (MSC) with a triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) represents a remarkable advancement in the realm of energy harvesting and storage. This innovative approach leverages the synergistic capabilities of both technologies to create a self-powered system capable of harvesting mechanical energy from ambient movements and storing it efficiently for later use. The flexible micro-supercapacitor serves as an essential component for energy storage within the integrated system. Fabricated using flexible substrates and employing advanced manufacturing techniques, such as laser etching and electrochemical deposition, the MSC offers high-performance energy storage capabilities in a compact and flexible form factor. Its all-solid-state design ensures robustness and reliability, making it ideal for integration into wearable and portable electronic devices. On the other hand, the triboelectric nanogenerator is responsible for harvesting mechanical energy from various sources, such as human motion, vibrations, or wind. Through the triboelectric effect, friction between two materials generates an imbalance of charge, which can be harnessed to produce electrical power. By integrating the TENG with the flexible MSC, the generated electrical energy can be efficiently stored for later use, enabling continuous and sustainable power generation in a self-sufficient manner.

The recent invention of triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs), leveraging the coupling effect of contact electrification and electrostatic induction, holds great promise for harvesting ambient mechanical energy [296–300]. However, TENGs face limitations in directly powering microelectronics due to their pulsed AC signal and low current output [301]. To overcome this challenge, an energy storage device can be employed to store the energy generated by TENGs, leading to the development of a self-charging power unit. While thin-film or micro-batteries are commonly used as small-scale energy storage units, they often exhibit drawbacks such as low power density and a limited cycle life. Additionally, these batteries require charging with a constant voltage, posing challenges in compatibility with TENGs. Micro-supercapacitors (MSCs), characterized by quick electron-ion kinetics and sustainable electrode materials, offer improved power density and longer cycle life compared to micro-batteries. Given their high-rate capability and rapid response, MSCs are excellent candidates for integration with triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) as energy storage units. Several strategies have been proposed for combining TENGs with supercapacitors [302–308]. However, existing methods face certain limitations. TENGs operating in contact-separation mode often require a spacer, making the construction of integrated and compact devices challenging. Additionally, conventional supercapacitors with liquid electrolytes can experience evaporation when exposed to air, necessitating proper encapsulation and resulting in larger TENG-supercapacitor devices. Ma et al. [309]devised an integrated self-charging power paper system that harnesses mechanical energy through Triboelectric Nanogenerators (TENG) and Micro Supercapacitors (MSCs) as illustrated in the Fig. 23a . This study underscores the importance of optimizing TENG device structures to enhance practical performance, enabling continuous energy generation from simple human motions. Notably, the device eliminates the need for additional processes like wire bonding, circuit pad installation, and packaging, reducing fabrication costs. Additionally, through the combination of pencil writing and surface functionalization, the system achieves an areal capacitance of 6.96 mAh g−1 cm−2, surpassing pure pencil lead coatings by over five times. Moreover, the self-charging power paper successfully powered several microelectronic devices, showcasing its potential as a power source for future smart technologies. Moreover, since TENGs and supercapacitors are typically fabricated as separate entities, the need for external wiring for electrical connection introduces extra bulk to the system.

Mao et al. [310] introduced a unique round-tripping-knitting strategy to craft a TENG array textile by knitting PTFE yarn around CCT@PDMS/MnO2 elastic yarns. This innovative textile not only harvests bio-motion energy but also serves as wearable fabric. By weaving this TENG array into a textile integrated with an all-solid-state yarn-type ASC, they created a self-powered wearable that can continuously convert and store biomechanical energy from human movements into electricity as shown in the Fig. 23b. The ASC exhibits flexibility, a high voltage window, ultrahigh volumetric energy density, and excellent cyclic stability, making it suitable for integration with the TENG array textile. The power generation efficiency of the TENG array is enhanced by adjusting the dielectric permittivity of the PDMS/MnO2NW elastomer, which varies with the mass-loading of MnO2NW. This adjustment allows the TENG array textile to produce an instantaneous output voltage of up to 380 V, capable of lighting 200 LEDs, sustainably charging the ASC, and powering a red LED. Additionally, this TENG array textile can convert various forms of bio-motion, such as finger taps and movements from walking or jogging, into electrical energy. This capability, combined with superior energy harvesting and excellent pressure-sensing properties, positions the self-powered TENG-SC electronic textile as a highly practical solution for wearable, lightweight, and comfortable continuous bio-motion energy capture and storage, as well as for versatile pressure sensing applications.

In a pioneering effort, Jiang and colleague[311] developed a self-charging power unit by integrating MXene-based micro-supercapacitors with a soft and flexible triboelectric nanogenerator using the skin as contact. This power system can be continuously charged by ordinary human motion (5 Hz) without significant current leakage. The functionality of the system was demonstrated by powering various electronic devices and sensors. This approach showcases the versatility of MXene micro-supercapacitors, offering new possibilities for their integration with energy harvesting devices in wearable and implantable sensor networks. illustrates the schematic representation of the detailed device structure. The three main components, including the current collector for the TENG, rectifier, and micro-supercapacitor, were effectively sealed by silicone rubber, which itself served both as the encapsulating material and stretchable dielectric. The intricate operation of the TENG device is elucidated in Fig. 23c.

Initially, when human skin and silicone come into contact, positive and negative charges are generated on their surfaces. As the positively charged skin departs from the TENG device, a potential difference is generated across the inputs of the bridge rectifier, causing electrons to charge the supercapacitor, embedded beneath the negatively charged silicone. This process is reiterated until the skin reaches its maximum separation distance. Conversely, as the skin returns to the TENG, the potential on the carbon fiber increases, prompting electrons to flow back and charge the supercapacitor via the bridge rectifier. The entire cycle resets when the skin makes contact with the TENG again. The TENG's performance was comprehensively characterized under various load conditions. At a working frequency of 1.3 Hz, the maximum charge transferred per cycle was approximately 4.2 nC/cm2, and the peak current reached around 0.13 A/cm2. Simultaneously, the open-circuit voltage for a device with a 4 cm2 active surface was approximately 50 V. Combining MXene-based micro-supercapacitors with a soft and flexible triboelectric nanogenerator, utilizing skin as contact, the power system demonstrated continuous charging through regular human motion (approximately 5 Hz) without significant current leakage.

In another study by Ye et al. [312], two multi-layered graphene-MSCs (MG-MSCs) were integrated as line-filters, along with multi-layered graphene-polyaniline hybrid MSCs (MG-PANI MSCs), PEG (energy harvester), a diode bridge rectifier (transforming AC into DC), gas sensor, and pressure sensor, all incorporated into a flexible PI substrate. By tapping PEG at a frequency of 110 Hz, electricity was generated by PEG and stored in MG-PANI MSCs. The charged voltage reached up to 1.5 V through the rectifier and MG-MSC line-filter. The current response of the pressure sensor in the integrated device, detecting walking pressure pulses (≈5 kPa), remains stable, as depicted in with an on/off ratio of 10,000 after 10,000 s. When powered by MSCs, the pressure sensor exhibits rhythmic signals during the detection of multiple steps, and PANI gas sensors sensing NO2 or NH3 gas under 200 ppm show sensitive and rapid responses. This indicates that the integrated MSC system functions well in each electrical element. In a study conducted by Zhu et al. [313], a micro-supercapacitor (MSC) was ingeniously coupled with a piezoelectric energy generator (PEG) to create an innovative energy harvesting system. In this setup, the PEG harvested mechanical energy from ambient sources and converted it into electricity. The alternating current (AC) generated by the PEG was then rectified into direct current (DC) using a rectifier, and the electricity was subsequently stored in a polypyrrole nanowire-based micro-supercapacitor (PPyNW μSC). The charging and discharging curves of the μSC revealed that the voltage of the MSC reached 0.1 V after 400 seconds of PEG charging. Notably, the initial voltage drop was only 0.01 V, indicating excellent integration between the MSC and PEG circuits. To assess the stored energy in the PPyNW μSC, a red LED was incorporated, illuminating with the released electricity, thus confirming the successful integration of the PPyNW μSC with electronic components. Despite the MSC's capability to be charged by an external PEG, the study highlighted certain limitations, such as dimensional incompatibility and discrepant stiffness between the two components, which hinder their seamless integration into integrated circuits. Consequently, the trend is shifting towards the development of integrated systems comprising MSCs and triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) or PEGs, aiming for enhanced flexibility, lightweight design, and portability. Furthermore, these integrated systems, facilitated by a shared substrate, demonstrate more compact and robust integration, paving the way for the advancement of efficient and versatile energy harvesting solutions for various application. The seamless integration of the flexible micro-supercapacitor with the triboelectric nanogenerator opens up new possibilities for self-powered electronic systems, particularly in wearable electronics, smart textiles, and IoT devices. This integrated approach offers the benefits of enhanced energy harvesting efficiency, improved energy storage capacity, and increased device flexibility, paving the way for the development of innovative and sustainable energy solutions for diverse applications.

Merely connecting micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) with other electronics using conductive wires falls short of achieving satisfactory integration in integrated circuits. An all-in-one device represents a comprehensive system that incorporates all components into a single, intact, and compact structure[314]. There are primarily two methods for assembling such devices. One approach involves introducing multifunctional materials with dynamically reversible properties under specific environmental conditions. The other method entails designing the integrated structure using specific materials with multifunctional capabilities[315].

Experiments involving dynamic reversible building materials in micro-supercapacitors are relatively rare due to the limited types of materials meeting the necessary requirements. Haichao et al. [316] showcased the fabrication of symmetric on-chip micro-supercapacitors with MXene-blended ink for all-in-one Si-electronics. The resulting MSCs exhibited a high volumetric capacitance of 350.4 F cm⁻³, operating at a high voltage of 1.2 V. This corresponds to a ground breaking volumetric energy density of 75.5 mWh cm⁻³ for MXene-based MSCs. Importantly, these MSCs can be seamlessly integrated with other on-chip electronics, effectively enhancing integration density. Thus, the development of MSCs based on solution inks of MXene holds potential for advancing high-performance and highly integrated all-in-one Si-electronics. The concept of a self-charged power pack, amalgamating energy harvesting and energy storage functions into a single device, presents a promising energy system[317,318]. Junyeong et al [319]. successfully demonstrated the production of a stretchable array consisting of high-performance solid-state micro-supercapacitors (MSCs). These MSCs were designed to be charged by integrated silicon-based commercial supercapacitors (SCs) for the wireless powering of an integrated strain sensor. The planar MSC was composed of electrodes deposited potential statically with poly-pyrrole (Ppy) on a spray-coated carbon nanotube (CNT) film, and it utilized a gel-type electrolyte containing LiCl/PVA with a redox additive of MPII. A strain sensor was created using a composite film of FGF and PDMS. The fabrication included twelve parallel-connected MSCs, a strain sensor, and two serially connected SCs, all integrated on a single Eco-flex substrate.

In the integrated structure of the all-in-one device designed with multifunctional materials, as demonstrated by Song et al. [321], a piezo-resistance sensor was ingeniously combined with micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) to create a versatile all-in-one sensing patch. This patch utilized porous carbon nanotube-polydimethylsiloxane (CNT-PDMS) elastomer, serving both as the electrodes for MSCs and the sensing element for the piezo-resistance sensor. The assembled sensing patch could be conveniently attached to the wrists and arms, enabling the identification of various motions based on the resistance variation corresponding to bending states and compressive strains. Furthermore, this innovative sensing patch could serve as a 3D touch interface for user identification and safety communication purposes. Through the sensor, the touching force and intervals of different individuals could be accurately recorded, allowing for personalized user authentication based on their unique touching habits. Users could simply press the sensor to log in to the program, leveraging the sensor's capability to recognize individual touch patterns. Moreover, this all-in-one self-charging electrokinetic supercapacitor offered a completely integrated structure, enabling simultaneous energy harvesting and storage functionalities. This ground-breaking technology opens up new possibilities for the development of multifunctional wearable devices, offering enhanced user interaction, personalized authentication, and self-sustaining energy capabilities.

Flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) represent a cutting-edge advancement in energy storage technology, merging flexibility, high power density, and compact size to cater to a broad spectrum of modern electronic applications. Their development encompasses a blend of innovative materials and advanced fabrication techniques, making them suitable for integration into various devices, from wearable technology to medical implants. The core structure of FMSCs typically consists of flexible current collectors, active electrode materials, and an electrolyte. Current collectors, which provide both electrical conductivity and mechanical support, are often made from conductive polymers like polyaniline or poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) or from metallic foils such as stainless steel or aluminum. These materials are chosen for their ability to maintain flexibility while ensuring efficient electrical conduction. The electrode materials, sandwiched between these current collectors, are responsible for storing and releasing energy. These materials are designed to maximize the surface area available for charge storage, which is essential for enhancing the performance of the FMSC. The electrolyte, positioned between the electrodes, facilitates ion transport, which is crucial for the charge and discharge cycles of the device. The selection of the electrolyte impacts the overall performance and efficiency of the FMSC. FMSCs operate based on two primary energy storage mechanisms: electrochemical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and pseudocapacitance. EDLC relies on the formation of an electric double layer at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte when a voltage is applied. In this process, ions from the electrolyte adsorb onto the electrode surface, storing energy through electrostatic interactions. This mechanism is renowned for its high power density and long cycle life, making it ideal for applications that require rapid charge and discharge cycles. In contrast, pseudocapacitance involves faradaic (redox) reactions, where energy is stored through the transfer of electrons between the electrode and the electrolyte. This method offers higher energy density compared to EDLC but may result in lower power density. Combining both mechanisms EDLC for high power and pseudocapacitance for high energy density allows for a balanced performance tailored to specific application needs. The choice of electrode materials is critical to the performance of FMSCs. Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, is highly prized for its exceptional electrical conductivity, large surface area, and mechanical strength. These attributes make graphene an excellent choice for high-performance electrodes. Activated carbon, which is derived from carbon-rich materials and treated to develop a porous structure, is a cost-effective option with a high surface area, although it has lower conductivity compared to graphene. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), cylindrical nanostructures of carbon atoms, offer superior mechanical strength and electrical conductivity but can be expensive and complex to process. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which are crystalline materials composed of metal ions coordinated to organic ligands, provide high surface area and tunable properties, enhancing energy storage capabilities. However, the synthesis of MOFs can be challenging and costly. Quantum dots, nanoscale semiconductor particles, contribute to higher energy density due to their size-dependent electronic properties. MXenes, a family of two-dimensional materials consisting of transition metal carbides, nitrides, or carbonitrides, offer high conductivity and improved ion transport. Hybrid electrodes that combine multiple materials, such as graphene with CNTs or MOFs, aim to optimize performance by leveraging the strengths of each component. The choice of electrolyte significantly affects the performance of FMSCs. Aqueous electrolytes, composed of water and soluble salts or acids, provide high ionic conductivity and are considered safe, as water-based solutions are less hazardous compared to organic solvents. However, their utility is limited by a narrow voltage window, typically around 1.0–1.2 Vs, which restricts the energy density of the FMSC. Organic electrolytes, consisting of organic solvents mixed with salts, offer a wider voltage window (up to 2.7 Vs) and higher energy density, though they can present safety and environmental concerns due to the flammability and toxicity of the solvents used. Solid-state electrolytes, including solid polymer or inorganic types, support flexible and compact designs while enhancing safety by eliminating leakage risks and providing stability over a broad temperature range. Gel electrolytes, a subset of solid-state electrolytes, offer a balance between flexibility and performance, providing good ionic conductivity and mechanical support.

Fabrication techniques for FMSCs are crucial for their performance and integration into various applications. Inkjet printing is a versatile and cost-effective method used to deposit electrode materials and electrolytes onto flexible substrates. This technique allows for precise control over material placement and thickness, enabling the creation of intricate patterns and designs. Screen printing is another widely used method, suitable for fabricating large-area electrodes and conducting layers. It involves pressing ink through a mesh screen onto a substrate, which results in thick, uniform layers of electrode materials. 3D printing offers the ability to create complex, three-dimensional structures with customized geometries. This technique is used to fabricate intricate electrode designs and integrate them into flexible substrates, enhancing the versatility and functionality of FMSCs. Laser scribing uses lasers to etch or ablate material from a substrate, allowing for the precise definition of electrode patterns. This method enables high-resolution patterning, which is essential for achieving accurate and detailed electrode designs. The mask-assisted method involves using a physical mask to shield certain areas of the substrate while material is deposited or etched away. This technique is useful for creating complex electrode patterns with high precision. Photolithography, a well-established technique in semiconductor fabrication, uses light to transfer geometric patterns onto a substrate coated with a photosensitive material. This method is employed to define intricate electrode patterns and ensure high-resolution and uniformity. In summary, FMSCs are a dynamic and evolving technology that integrates advanced materials with sophisticated fabrication techniques. The combination of flexible and miniaturized designs, high-performance electrode materials, and innovative electrolytes positions FMSCs as a key technology for various applications, including wearable electronics, medical implants, and self-charging units. As research continues to advance, the development of new materials and fabrication techniques will further enhance the performance of FMSCs and expand their potential applications, solidifying their role in the future of energy storage solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and future perspectives of flexible MSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of flexible microsupercapacitors (FMSCs) faces numerous research challenges that need to be addressed to unlock their full potential. These challenges span across material development, fabrication techniques, performance optimization, practical application integration, and packaging technologies. A significant challenge in material development involves finding advanced electrode materials that strike a balance between high conductivity, large surface area, and mechanical flexibility. While materials like graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have shown promise, researchers need to discover and develop new materials that can enhance performance while remaining cost-effective for large-scale production. Additionally, the innovation of electrolytes is crucial. Current options, including aqueous, organic, and solid-state electrolytes, each come with limitations such as narrow voltage windows or safety concerns. New electrolytes must offer high ionic conductivity, wide electrochemical stability windows, and compatibility with flexible substrates. The development of gel-based or solid-state electrolytes that are flexible, safe, and efficient is vital for improving FMSC performance.

In terms of fabrication techniques, scaling up production while maintaining cost-effectiveness is a major challenge. Techniques like inkjet printing, screen printing, 3D printing, and laser scribing need optimization to ensure they can be used for mass production without compromising the quality and performance of FMSCs. These methods must be refined to increase throughput, reproducibility, and material efficiency. Precision in patterning and layer thickness is also critical. Enhancing techniques such as photolithography and mask-assisted methods to achieve high-resolution and complex geometries is essential for creating high-performance FMSCs. Integrating various components electrodes, electrolytes, and current collectors into a single flexible device poses another challenge. Effective fabrication methods must ensure that these components are combined seamlessly, maintaining the device's flexibility and functionality. Developing techniques that can handle the diverse properties of these materials while ensuring reliable performance is crucial.

Performance optimization remains a central concern. Researchers are working to enhance both energy and power density of FMSCs by developing new materials and designs that increase charge storage capacity and improve charge-discharge rates. Balancing energy density and power density to meet specific application needs is an ongoing challenge. Additionally, ensuring the longevity and reliability of FMSCs under various operating conditions is essential. This includes understanding how factors like environmental conditions, mechanical stress, and repeated charge-discharge cycles affect performance and addressing these issues through improved materials and design. Maintaining mechanical flexibility while ensuring high performance is another significant challenge. FMSCs must endure bending, stretching, and other mechanical deformations without compromising their functionality. This requires the development of materials and designs that can withstand mechanical stress while preserving device performance. Practical application integration involves several challenges. In wearable and implantable devices, ensuring biocompatibility and user comfort is crucial. FMSCs need to be integrated into medical devices and wearable technology in a way that is safe and comfortable, addressing potential concerns like irritation or discomfort.

Environmental impact and sustainability are also important. The production and disposal of FMSCs must be environmentally friendly. Research should focus on creating sustainable materials and processes, as well as developing effective recycling and disposal methods for used devices. Minimizing the environmental footprint of FMSCs is crucial for ensuring their long-term viability. Packaging technology is a critical aspect of FMSC development. Efficient packaging techniques are necessary to protect the delicate components of FMSCs while ensuring their flexibility and functionality. Advanced packaging technologies, such as roll-to-roll processing, are essential for producing FMSCs on a large scale. These techniques involve continuously processing materials and assembling devices on flexible substrates, which can significantly reduce manufacturing costs and improve scalability. Additionally, developing packaging materials that provide protection against environmental factors while maintaining flexibility is important for ensuring the durability and reliability of FMSCs in various applications.

The integration of FMSCs with energy-harvesting systems is another area of focus. Combining FMSCs with energy sources like solar cells or piezoelectric generators presents challenges in optimizing the interface and ensuring efficient energy transfer. Research must aim to improve the efficiency of these combined systems and ensure they operate reliably under various conditions. Hence, addressing these research challenges is crucial for advancing the field of FMSCs. Innovations in materials, fabrication techniques, performance optimization, and practical applications, along with advancements in packaging technologies, will drive the development of FMSCs and expand their potential uses across various industries. Collaborative efforts among researchers, engineers, and industry professionals will be essential in overcoming these challenges and realizing the full potential of FMSCs in next-generation electronic devices and systems.
